7-3-costs-in-the-short-run.question: average marginal costs cost producing a firms output depends on how much labor physical capital firm uses a list costs involved producing cars will look very different from costs involved producing computer software or haircuts or fastfood meals we can measure costs a variety ways each way provides its own insight into costs sometimes firms need look at their cost per unit output not just their total cost there are two ways measure per unit costs most intuitive way average cost average cost cost on average producing a given quantity we define average cost as total cost divided by quantity output produced actcqactcq if producing two widgets costs a total 44 average cost per widget 4422244222 per widget other way measuring cost per unit marginal cost if average cost cost average unit output produced marginal cost cost each individual unit produced more formally marginal cost cost producing one more unit output mathematically marginal cost change total cost divided by change output mcδtcδqmcδtcδq if cost first widget 3250 cost two widgets 44 marginal cost second widget 44325011504432501150 we can see widget cost table redrawn below average marginal cost added q 1 2 3 4 total cost 3250 4400 5200 9000 average cost 3250 2200 1733 2250 marginal cost 3250 1150 800 3800 table 78 extended cost function producing widgets note that marginal cost first unit output always same as total cost

fixed variable costs we can decompose costs into fixed variable costs fixed costs are costs fixed inputs eg capital because fixed inputs do not change short run fixed costs are expenditures that do not change regardless level production whether you produce a great deal or a little fixed costs are same one example rent on a factory or a retail space once you sign lease rent same regardless how much you produce at least until lease expires fixed costs can take many other forms example cost machinery or equipment produce product research development costs develop new products even an expense like advertising popularize a brand name amount fixed costs varies according specific line business instance manufacturing computer chips requires an expensive factory but a local moving hauling business can get by almost no fixed costs at all if rents trucks by day when needed variable costs are costs variable inputs eg labor only way increase or decrease output by increasing or decreasing variable inputs therefore variable costs increase or decrease output we treat labor as a variable cost since producing a greater quantity a good or service typically requires more workers or more work hours variable costs would also include raw materialstotal costs are sum fixed plus variable costs lets look at another example consider barber shop called clip joint figure 77 data output costs are table 79 fixed costs operating barber shop including space equipment are 160 per day variable costs are costs hiring barbers which our example 80 per barber each day first two columns table show quantity haircuts barbershop can produce as hires additional barbers third column shows fixed costs which do not change regardless level production fourth column shows variable costs at each level output we calculate these by taking amount labor hired multiplying by wage example two barbers cost 2 80 160 adding together fixed costs third column variable costs fourth column produces total costs 480 640 7 82 160 560 720 table 79 output total costs at zero production fixed costs 160 are still present as production increases we add variable costs fixed costs total cost sum two figure 77 they begin increasing at an increasing rate this caused by diminishing marginal productivity which we discussed earlier production short run section this chapter which easiest see an example as number barbers increases from zero one table output increases from 0 16 a marginal gain or marginal product 16 as number rises from one two barbers output increases from 16 40 a marginal gain 24 from that only 12to understand reason behind this pattern consider that a oneman barber shop a very busy operation single barber needs do everything say hello people entering
7-3-costs-in-the-short-run.answer: phone cut hair sweep run cash register a second barber reduces level disruption from jumping back forth between these tasks allows a greater division labor specialization result can be increasing marginal productivity however as shop adds other barbers advantage each additional barber less since specialization labor can only go so far addition a sixth or seventh or eighth barber just greet people at door will have less impact than second one did this pattern diminishing marginal productivity as a result total costs production will begin rise more rapidly as output increases at some point you may even see negative returns as additional barbers begin bumping elbows getting each others way this case addition still more barbers would actually cause output decrease as last row table 79 showsthis pattern diminishing marginal productivity common production as another example consider problem irrigating a crop on a farmers field plot land fixed factor production while water that farmer can add land key variable cost as farmer adds water land output increases however adding increasingly more water brings smaller increases output until at some point water floods field actually reduces output diminishing marginal productivity occurs because fixed inputs land this example each additional unit input eg water contributes less overall production

7-3-costs-in-the-short-run.question: a variety cost patterns pattern costs varies among industries even among firms same industry some businesses have high fixed costs but low marginal costs consider example an internet company that provides medical advice customers consumers might pay such a company directly or perhaps hospitals or healthcare practices might subscribe on behalf their patients setting up website collecting information writing content buying or leasing computer space handle web traffic are all fixed costs that company must undertake before site can work however when website up running can provide a high quantity service relatively low variable costs like cost monitoring system updating information this case total cost curve might start at a high level because high fixed costs but then might appear close flat up a large quantity output reflecting low variable costs operation if website popular however a large rise number visitors will overwhelm website increasing output further could require a purchase additional computer spacefor other firms fixed costs may be relatively low example consider firms that rake leaves fall or shovel snow off sidewalks driveways winter fixed costs such firms may need little more than a car transport workers homes customers some rakes shovels still other firms may find that diminishing marginal returns set quite sharply if a manufacturing plant tried run 24 hours a day seven days a week little time remains routine equipment maintenance marginal costs can increase dramatically as firm struggles repair replace overworked equipmentevery firm can gain insight into its task earning profits by dividing its total costs into fixed variable costs then using these calculations as a basis average total cost average variable cost marginal cost however making a final decision about profitmaximizing quantity produce price charge will require combining these perspectives on cost an analysis sales revenue which turn requires looking at market structure which firm finds itself before we turn analysis market structure other chapters we will analyze firms cost structure from a longrun perspective

15-5-pitfalls-for-monetary-policy.question: excess reserves banks are legally required hold a minimum level reserves but no rule prohibits them from holding additional excess reserves above legally mandated limit example during a recession banks may be hesitant lend because they fear that when economy contracting a high proportion loan applicants become less likely repay their loans when many banks are choosing hold excess reserves expansionary monetary policy may not work well this may occur because banks are concerned about a deteriorating economy while central bank trying expand money supply if banks prefer hold excess reserves above legally required level central bank cannot force individual banks make loans similarly sensible businesses consumers may be reluctant borrow substantial amounts money a recession because they recognize that firms sales employees jobs are more insecure a recession they do not want face need make interest payments result that during an especially deep recession an expansionary monetary policy may have little effect on either price level or real gdp japan experienced this situation 1990s early 2000s japans economy entered a period very slow growth dipping out recession early 1990s by february 1999 bank japan had lowered equivalent its federal funds rate 0 kept there most time through 2003 moreover two years from march 2001 march 2003 bank japan also expanded countrys money supply by about 50an enormous increase even this highly expansionary monetary policy however had no substantial effect on stimulating aggregate demand japans economy continued experience extremely slow growth into mid2000s problem excess reserves does not affect contractionary policy central bankers have an old saying that monetary policy can be like pulling pushing on a string when central bank pulls on string uses contractionary monetary policy can definitely raise interest rates reduce aggregate demand however when central bank tries push on string expansionary monetary policy string may sometimes just fold up limp have little effect because banks decide not loan out their excess reserves do not take this analogy too literallyexpansionary monetary policy usually does have real effects after that inconveniently long variable lag there are also times like japans economy late 1990s early 2000s when expansionary monetary policy has been insufficient lift a recessionprone economy

right up moment that they want write a check on money transfer m1 so far at least has proven difficult draw clear links between these kinds factors specific upanddown fluctuations m1 given many changes banking prevalence electronic banking economists now favor m2 as a measure money rather than narrower m1 access multimedia content velocity calculated using m1 1970s when velocity as measured by m1 seemed predictable a number economists led by nobel laureate milton friedman 19122006 argued that best monetary policy was central bank increase money supply at a constant growth rate these economists argued that long variable lags monetary policy political pressures on central bankers central bank monetary policies were as likely have undesirable as have desirable effects thus these economists believed that monetary policy should seek steady growth money supply 3 per year they argued that a steady monetary growth rate would be correct over longer time periods since would roughly match growth real economy addition they argued that giving central bank less discretion conduct monetary policy would prevent an overly activist central bank from becoming a source economic instability uncertainty this spirit friedman wrote 1967 first most important lesson that history teaches about what monetary policy can doand a lesson most profound importanceis that monetary policy can prevent money itself from being a major source economic disturbanceas velocity m1 began fluctuate 1980s having money supply grow at a predetermined unchanging rate seemed less desirable because as quantity theory money shows combination constant growth money supply fluctuating velocity would cause nominal gdp rise fall unpredictable ways jumpiness velocity 1980s caused many central banks focus less on rate at which quantity money economy was increasing instead set monetary policy by reacting whether economy was experiencing or danger higher inflation or unemployment
unemployment inflation if you were survey central bankers around world ask them what they believe should be primary task monetary policy most popular
15-5-pitfalls-for-monetary-policy.answer: by far would be fighting inflation most central bankers believe that neoclassical model economics accurately represents economy over medium long term remember that neoclassical model economy we draw aggregate supply curve as a vertical line at level potential gdp as figure 1510 shows neoclassical model economists determine level potential gdp natural rate unemployment that exists when economy producing at potential gdp by real economic factors if original level aggregate demand ad0 then an expansionary monetary policy that shifts aggregate demand ad1 only creates an inflationary increase price level but does not alter gdp or unemployment from this perspective all that monetary policy can do lead low inflation or high inflationand low inflation provides a better climate a healthy growing economy after all low inflation means that businesses making investments can focus on real economic issues not on figuring out ways protect themselves from costs risks inflation this way a consistent pattern low inflation can contribute longterm growththis vision focusing monetary policy on a low rate inflation so attractive that many countries have rewritten their central banking laws since 1990s have their bank practice inflation targeting which means that central bank legally required focus primarily on keeping inflation low by 2014 central banks 28 countries including austria brazil canada israel korea mexico new zealand spain sweden thailand united kingdom faced a legal requirement target inflation rate a notable exception federal reserve united states which does not practice inflationtargeting instead law governing federal reserve requires take both unemployment inflation into accounteconomists have no final consensus on whether a central bank should be required focus only on inflation or should have greater discretion those who subscribe inflation targeting philosophy fear that politicians who are worried about slow economic growth unemployment will constantly pressure central bank conduct a loose monetary policyeven if economy already producing at potential gdp some countries central bank may lack political power resist such pressures result higher inflation but no longterm reduction unemployment us federal reserve has a tradition independence but central banks other countries may be under greater political pressure all these reasonslong variable lags excess reserves unstable velocity controversy over economic goalsmonetary policy real world often difficult basic message remains however that central banks can affect aggregate demand through conduct monetary policy that way influence macroeconomic outcomes

15-5-pitfalls-for-monetary-policy.question: bring home problem zero percent interest rate lower bound 2008 us federal reserve found itself a difficult position federal funds rate was on its way near zero which meant that traditional open market operations by which fed purchases us treasury bills lower short term interest rates was no longer viable this so called zero bound problem prompted fed under then chair ben bernanke attempt some unconventional policies collectively called quantitative easing by early 2014 quantitative easing nearly quintupled amount bank reserves this likely contributed us economys recovery but impact was muted probably due some hurdles mentioned last section this module unprecedented increase bank reserves also led fears inflation which never bore out throughout 2010s there have been no serious signs inflation core inflation around a stable 152 as early 2015 however there have been no serious signs a boom core inflation around a stable 17the zero lower bound was encountered again 2020 due pandemic when target federal funds rate dropped by over two percentage points a matter weeks fed further responded by increasing asset purchases by an even greater amount at a faster rate than 2009 when us started experiencing higherthanaverage inflation 2021 fed chair jerome powell responded criticisms by saying that fed was poised consider rate increases start tapering rate asset purchases into 2022 remains be seen whether inflation can be tamed moving forward or if more aggressive policy measures will be required

asset bubbles leverage cycles one longstanding concern about having central bank focus on inflation unemployment that may be overlooking certain other economic problems that are coming future example from 1994 2000 during what was known as dotcom boom us stock market which dow jones industrial index measures which includes 30 very large companies from across us economy nearly tripled value nasdaq index which includes many smaller technology companies increased value by a multiple five from 1994 2000 these rates increase were clearly not sustainable stock values as measured by dow jones were almost 20 lower 2009 than they had been 2000 stock values nasdaq index were 50 lower 2009 than they had been 2000 dropoff stock market values contributed 2001 recession higher unemployment that followedwe can tell a similar story about housing prices mid2000s during 1970s 1980s 1990s housing prices increased at about 6 per year on average during what came be known as housing bubble from 2003 2005 housing prices increased at almost double this annual rate these rates increase were clearly not sustainable when housing prices fell 2007 2008 many banks households found that their assets were worth less than they expected which contributed recession that started 2007at a broader level some economists worry about a leverage cycle where leverage a term financial economists use mean borrowing when economic times are good banks financial sector are eager lend people firms are eager borrow remember that a money multiplier determines amount money credit an economy a process loans made money deposited more loans made good economic times this surge lending exaggerates episode economic growth can even be part what lead prices certain assetslike stock prices or housing pricesto rise at unsustainably high annual rates at some point when economic times turn bad banks financial sector become much less willing lend credit becomes expensive or unavailable many potential borrowers sharp reduction credit perhaps combined deflating prices a dotcom stock price bubble or a housing bubble makes economic downturn worse than would otherwise bethus some economists have suggested that central bank should not just look at economic growth inflation unemployment rates but should also keep an eye on asset prices leverage cycles such proposals are quite controversial if a central bank had announced 1997 that stock prices were rising too fast or 2004 that housing prices were rising too fast then taken action hold down price increases many people their elected political representatives would have been outraged neither federal reserve nor any other central banks want take responsibility deciding when stock prices housing prices are too high too low or just right as further research explores how asset price bubbles leverage cycles can affect an economy central banks may need think about whether they should conduct monetary policy a way that would seek moderate these effects lets end this chapter a work out exercise how fedor any central bankwould stir up economy by increasing money supply work out calculating effects monetary stimulus suppose that central bank wants stimulate economy by increasing money supply bankers estimate that velocity money 3 that price level will increase from 100 110 due stimulus using quantity equation money what will be impact an 800 billion dollar increase money supply on quantity goods services economy given an initial money supply 4 trillion step 1 we begin by writing quantity equation money mv pq we know that initially v 3 m 4000 billion p 100 substituting these numbers we can solve q mv pq4000 3 100 qq 120mv pq4000 3 100 qq 120 step 2 now we want find effect addition 800 billion money supply together increase price level new equation mv pq4800 3 110 qq 1309mv pq4800 3 110 qq 1309 step 3 if we take difference between two quantities we find that monetary stimulus increased quantity goods services economy by 109 billion discussion this chapter has focused on domestic monetary policy that view monetary policy within an economy exchange rates international capital flows explores international dimension monetary policy how monetary policy becomes involved exchange rates international flows financial capital

7-self-check-questions.question: question 9 would following events usually lead capital deepening why or why not a weak economy which businesses become reluctant make longterm investments physical capital a rise international trade a trend which many more adults participate continuing education courses through their employers at colleges universities
7-self-check-questions.answer: 9 no capital deepening refers an increase amount capital per person an economy a decrease investment by firms will actually cause opposite capital deepening since population will grow over time there no direct connection between an increase international trade capital deepening one could imagine particular scenarios where trade could lead capital deepening example if international capital inflowswhich are counterpart increasing trade deficitlead an increase physical capital investment but general no yes capital deepening refers an increase either physical capital or human capital per person continuing education or any time lifelong learning adds human capital thus creates capital deepening

7-self-check-questions.question: question 11 would you expect capital deepening result diminished returns why or why not would you expect improvements technology result diminished returns why or why not
7-self-check-questions.answer: 11 capital deepening by definition should lead diminished returns because youre investing more more but using same methods production leading marginal productivity declining this shown on a production function as a movement along curve improvements technology should not lead diminished returns because you are finding new more efficient ways using same amount capital this can be illustrated as a shift upward production function curve

21-self-check-questions.question: question 19 what are gains from competition
21-self-check-questions.answer: 19 consumers get better or less expensive products businesses better or cheaper products increase their profits employees those businesses earn more income on balance gains outweigh losses a nation

21-self-check-questions.question: question 17 what might account dramatic increase international trade over past 50 years
21-self-check-questions.answer: 17 reductions tariffs quotas other trade barriers improved transportation communication media have made people more aware what available rest world

21-self-check-questions.question: question 15 what difference between a free trade association a common market an economic union
21-self-check-questions.answer: 15 a free trade association offers free trade between its members but each country can determine its own trade policy outside association a common market requires a common external trade policy addition free trade within group an economic union a common market coordinated fiscal monetary policy

21-self-check-questions.question: question 18 how does competition whether domestic or foreign harm businesses
21-self-check-questions.answer: 18 competition from firms better or cheaper products can reduce a businesss profits may drive out business workers would similarly lose income or even their jobs

21-self-check-questions.question: question 16 why would countries promote protectionist laws while also negotiate freer trade internationally
21-self-check-questions.answer: 16 international agreements can serve as a political counterweight domestic special interests thereby preventing stronger protectionist measures

21-self-check-questions.answer: 4 trade based on comparative advantage raises average wage rate economywide though can reduce incomes importsubstituting industries by moving away from a countrys comparative advantage trade barriers do opposite they give workers protected industries an advantage while reducing average wage economywide

temporary permanent fiscal policy a temporary tax cut or spending increase will explicitly last only a year or two then revert its original level a permanent tax cut or spending increase expected stay place foreseeable future effect temporary permanent fiscal policies on aggregate demand can be very different consider how you would react if government announced a tax cut that would last one year then be repealed comparison how you would react if government announced a permanent tax cut most people firms will react more strongly a permanent policy change than a temporary onethis fact creates an unavoidable difficulty countercyclical fiscal policy appropriate policy may be have an expansionary fiscal policy large budget deficits during a recession then a contractionary fiscal policy budget surpluses when economy growing well however if both policies are explicitly temporary ones they will have a less powerful effect than a permanent policy

structural economic change takes time when an economy recovers from a recession does not usually revert its exact earlier shape instead economys internal structure evolves changes this process can take time example much economic growth mid2000s was construction sector especially housing finance however when housing prices started falling 2007 resulting financial crunch led into recession as we discussed monetary policy bank regulation both sectors contracted manufacturing sector us economy has been losing jobs recent years as well under pressure from technological change foreign competition many people who lost work from these sectors 20082009 great recession will never return same jobs same sectors economy instead economy will need grow new different directions as following clear up feature shows fiscal policy can increase overall demand but process structural economic changethe expansion a new set industries movement workers those industriesinevitably takes time

limitations fiscal policy fiscal policy can help an economy that producing below its potential gdp expand aggregate demand so that produces closer potential gdp thus lowering unemployment however fiscal policy cannot help an economy produce at an output level above potential gdp without causing inflation at this point unemployment becomes so low that workers become scarce wages rise rapidly

budget amendment prevents even small temporary deficits that might some cases be necessary

17-4-using-fiscal-policy-to-fight-recess.question: expansionary fiscal policy expansionary fiscal policy increases level aggregate demand through either increases government spending or reductions tax rates expansionary policy can do this by 1 increasing consumption by raising disposable income through cuts personal income taxes or payroll taxes 2 increasing investment spending by raising aftertax profits through cuts business taxes 3 increasing government purchases through increased federal government spending on final goods services raising federal grants state local governments increase their expenditures on final goods services contractionary fiscal policy does reverse decreases level aggregate demand by decreasing consumption decreasing investment decreasing government spending either through cuts government spending or increases taxes aggregate demandaggregate supply model useful judging whether expansionary or contractionary fiscal policy appropriateconsider first situation figure 1711 which similar us economy during 20072009 recession intersection aggregate demand ad0 aggregate supply sras0 occurring below level potential gdp as lras curve indicates at equilibrium e0 a recession occurs unemployment rises this case expansionary fiscal policy using tax cuts or increases government spending can shift aggregate demand ad1 closer fullemployment level output addition price level would rise back level p1 associated potential gdpshould government use tax cuts or spending increases or a mix two carry out expansionary fiscal policy during 20072009 great recession us economy suffered a 31 cumulative loss gdp that may not sound like much but its more than one years average growth rate gdp over that time frame unemployment rate doubled from 5 10 choice between whether use tax or spending tools often has a political tinge as a general statement conservatives republicans prefer see expansionary fiscal policy carried out by tax cuts while liberals democrats prefer that government implement expansionary fiscal policy through spending increases a bipartisan effort address extreme situation obama administration congress passed an 830 billion expansionary policy early 2009 involving both tax cuts increases government spending at same time however federal stimulus was partially offset when state local governments whose budgets were hard hit by recession began cutting their spendingevents were even more severe during more recent pandemicinduced recession a single quarter quarter 2 2020 gdp fell by over 9 or at an annualized rate about 34 policymakers were quick respond expanded unemployment insurance aid state local governments so that they didnt have cut their spending like they did during great recession grants tax breaks small businesses perhaps most significantly stimulus checks sent over 100 million households totaling thousands dollars each since these were mostly spending measures they were supported more by democrats than by republicans although both groups recognized severity problem were largely agreement early on especially during debates over later rounds stimulus checks many discussions were had over appropriate size target checks ultimately compromises were made no side got exactly what wantedthe conflict over which policy tool use can be frustrating those who want categorize economics as liberal or conservative or who want use economic models argue against their political opponents however advocates smaller government who seek reduce taxes government spending can use ad as model as well as advocates bigger government who seek raise taxes government spending economic studies specific taxing spending programs can help inform decisions about whether government should change taxes or spending what ways ultimately decisions about whether use tax or spending mechanisms implement macroeconomic policy a political decision rather than a purely economic one

contractionary fiscal policy fiscal policy can also contribute pushing aggregate demand beyond potential gdp a way that leads inflation as figure 1712 shows a very large budget deficit pushes up aggregate demand so that intersection aggregate demand ad0 aggregate supply sras0 occurs at equilibrium e0 which an output level above potential gdp economists sometimes call this an overheating economy where demand so high that there upward pressure on wages prices causing inflation this situation contractionary fiscal policy involving federal spending cuts or tax increases can help reduce upward pressure on price level by shifting aggregate demand left ad1 causing new equilibrium e1 be at potential gdp where aggregate demand intersects lras curveagain adas model does not dictate how government should carry out this contractionary fiscal policy some may prefer spending cuts others may prefer tax increases still others may say that depends on specific situation model only argues that this situation government needs reduce aggregate demand

up payroll taxes rise as jobs payrolls go up corporate income taxes rise as profits go up at same time government spending on transfer payments such as unemployment benefits foods stamps welfare recession slow growth that followed deficits returned when severe recession hit late 2007 spending climbed tax collections fell historically unusual levels resulting enormous deficitslongerterm us 175 shows total federal taxes as a share gdp since 1960 although line rises falls typically remains within range 17 20 gdp except 20092011 when taxes fell substantially below this level due great recession figure 175 also shows taxation patterns main categories that federal government taxes individual income taxes corporate income taxes social insurance retirement receipts when most people think federal government taxes first tax that comes mind individual income tax that due every year on april 15 or first business day after personal income tax largest single source federal government revenue but still represents less than half federal tax revenuethe second largest source federal revenue payroll tax captured social insurance retirement receipts which provides funds social security medicare payroll taxes have increased steadily over time together personal income tax payroll tax accounted over 85 federal tax revenues 2020 although personal income tax revenues account more total revenue than payroll tax nearly threequarters households pay more payroll taxes than income taxesthe income tax a progressive tax which means that tax rates increase as a households income increases taxes also vary marital status family size other factors marginal tax rates tax due on all yearly income a single taxpayer range from 10 35 depending on income as following clear up feature explains key fact here that federal income tax designed so that tax rates increase as income increases up a certain level payroll taxes that support social security medicare are designed a different way first payroll taxes social security are imposed at a rate 124 up a certain wage limit set at 137700 2020 medicare on other hand pays elderly healthcare fixed at 29 no upper ceilingin both cases employer employee split payroll taxes an employee only sees 62 deducted from their paycheck social security 145 from medicare however as economists are quick point out employers half taxes are probably passed along employees form lower wages so reality worker pays all payroll taxes if you are a member gig economy receive a 1099 tax statement then you are considered an independent contractor so you must pay employee employer side payroll taxwe also call medicare payroll tax a proportional tax that a flat percentage all wages earned social security payroll tax proportional up wage limit but above that level becomes a regressive tax meaning that people higher incomes pay a smaller share their income taxthe thirdlargest source federal tax revenue as figure 175 shows corporate income tax common name corporate income profits over time corporate income tax receipts have declined as a share gdp from about 4 1960s an average 1 2 gdp past 40 yearsthe federal government has a few other smaller sources revenue imposes an excise taxthat a tax on a particular goodon gasoline tobacco alcohol as a share gdp amount government collects from these taxes has stayed nearly constant over time from about 2 gdp 1960s roughly 3 by 2020 according nonpartisan congressional budget office government also imposes an estate gift tax on people who pass large amounts assets next generationeither after death or during life form gifts these estate gift taxes collected about 02 gdp 2020 by a quirk legislation government repealed estate gift tax 2010 but reinstated 2011 other federal taxes which are also relatively small magnitude include tariffs government collects on imported goods charges inspections goods entering country

state local taxes at state local level taxes have been rising as a share gdp over last few decades match gradual rise spending as figure 176 illustrates main revenue sources state local governments are sales taxes property taxes revenue passed along from federal government but many state local governments also levy personal corporate income taxes as well as impose a wide variety fees charges specific sources tax revenue vary widely across state local governments some states rely more on property taxes some on sales taxes some on income taxes some more on revenues from federal government

hard c a merged currency monetary policy can focus on a variety goals a inflation b inflation or unemployment depending on which most dangerous obstacle c a longterm rule based policy designed keep money supply stable predictable

16-1-how-the-foreign-exchange-market-wor.question: participants exchange rate market foreign exchange market does not involve ultimate suppliers demanders foreign exchange literally seeking each other if martina decides leave her home venezuela take a trip united states she does not need find a us citizen who planning take a vacation venezuela arrange a persontoperson currency trade instead foreign exchange market works through financial institutions operates on several levelsmost people firms who are exchanging a substantial quantity currency go a bank most banks provide foreign exchange as a service customers these banks a few other firms known as dealers then trade foreign exchange this called interbank market world economy roughly 2000 firms are foreign exchange dealers us economy has less than 100 foreign exchange dealers but largest 12 or so dealers carry out more than half total transactions foreign exchange market has no central location but major dealers keep a close watch on each other at all times foreign exchange market huge not because demands tourists firms or even foreign direct investment but instead because portfolio investment actions interlocking foreign exchange dealers international tourism a very large industry involving about 1 trillion per year global exports are about 235 global gdp or about 19 trillion per year foreign direct investment totaled about 870 billion end 2021 these quantities are dwarfed however by 66 trillion per day traded foreign exchange markets most transactions foreign exchange market are portfolio investmentrelatively shortterm movements financial capital between currenciesand because large foreign exchange dealers actions as they constantly buy sell each other

16-1-how-the-foreign-exchange-market-wor.question: strengthening weakening currency when prices most goods services change price rises or falls exchange rates terminology different when exchange rate a currency rises so that currency exchanges more other currencies we refer as appreciating or strengthening when exchange rate a currency falls so that a currency trades less other currencies we refer as depreciating or weakeningto illustrate use these terms consider exchange rate between us dollar canadian dollar since 1980 figure 163 a vertical axis figure 163 a shows price 1 us currency measured terms canadian currency clearly exchange rates can move up down substantially a us dollar traded 117 canadian 1980 us dollar appreciated or strengthened 139 canadian 1986 depreciated or weakened 115 canadian 1991 then appreciated or strengthened 160 canadian by early 2002 fell roughly 120 canadian 2009 then had a sharp spike up decline 2009 2010 august 2022 us dollar stood at 128 canadian units which we measure exchange rates can be confusing because we measure exchange rate us dollar exchange using a different currencythe canadian dollar however exchange rates always measure price one unit currency by using a different currencyin looking at exchange rate between two currencies appreciation or strengthening one currency must mean depreciation or weakening other figure 163 b shows exchange rate canadian dollar measured terms us dollars exchange rate us dollar measured canadian dollars figure 163 a a perfect mirror image canadian dollar exchange rate measured us dollars figure 163 b a fall canada us ratio means a rise us canada ratio vice versa access multimedia content canadian dollars per 1 us dollar price a typical good or service clear that higher prices benefit sellers hurt buyers while lower prices benefit buyers hurt sellers case exchange rates where buyers sellers are not always intuitively obvious useful trace how a stronger or weaker currency will affect different market participants consider example impact a stronger us dollar on six different groups economic actors as figure 164 shows 1 us exporters selling abroad 2 foreign exporters that firms selling imports us economy 3 us tourists abroad 4 foreign tourists visiting united states 5 us investors either foreign direct investment or portfolio investment considering opportunities other countries 6 foreign investors considering opportunities us economyfor a us firm selling abroad a stronger us dollar a curse a strong us dollar means that foreign currencies are correspondingly weak when this exporting firm earns foreign currencies through its export sales then converts them back us dollars pay workers suppliers investors stronger dollar means that foreign currency buys fewer us dollars than if currency had not strengthened that firms profits as measured dollars fall as a result firm may choose reduce its exports or may raise its selling price which will also tend reduce its exports this way a stronger currency reduces a countrys exports conversely a foreign firm selling us economy a stronger dollar a blessing each dollar earned through export sales when traded back into exporting firms home currency will now buy more home currency than expected before dollar had strengthened as a result stronger dollar means that importing firm will earn higher profits than expected firm will seek expand its sales us economy or may reduce prices which will also lead expanded sales this way a stronger us dollar means that consumers will purchase more from foreign producers expanding countrys level importsfor a us tourist abroad who exchanging us dollars foreign currency as necessary a stronger us dollar a benefit tourist receives more foreign currency each us dollar consequently cost trip us dollars lower when a countrys currency strong a good time citizens that country tour abroad imagine a us tourist who has saved up 5000 a trip south africa february 2018 1 bought 119 south african rand so tourist had 59500 rand spend by 2018 1 bought 145 rand which tourist translates 72500 rand foreign visitors united states opposite pattern holds true a relatively stronger us dollar means that their own currencies are relatively weaker so that as they shift from their own currency us dollars they have fewer us dollars than previously when a countrys currency strong not an especially good time foreign tourists visita stronger dollar injures prospects a us financial investor who has already invested money another country a us financial investor abroad must first convert us dollars a foreign currency invest a foreign country then later convert that foreign currency back us dollars if meantime us dollar becomes stronger foreign currency becomes weaker then when investor converts back us dollars rate return on that investment will be less than originally expected at time was made however a stronger us dollar boosts returns a foreign investor putting money into a us investment that foreign investor converts from home currency us dollars seeks a us investment while later planning switch back home currency if meantime dollar grows stronger then when time comes convert from us dollars back foreign currency investor will receive more foreign currency than expected at time original investment was made preceding paragraphs all focus on case where us dollar becomes stronger first column figure 164 illustrates corresponding happy or unhappy economic reactions following work out feature centers analysis on opposite a weaker dollar work out effects a weaker dollar lets work through effects a weaker dollar on a us exporter a foreign exporter into united states a us tourist going abroad a foreign tourist coming united states a us investor abroad a foreign investor united states step 1 note that demand us exports a function price those exports which depends on dollar price those goods exchange rate dollar terms foreign currency example a ford pickup truck costs 25000 united states when sold united kingdom price 25000 130 per british pound or 19231 dollar affects price foreigners face who may purchase us exportsstep 2 consider that if dollar weakens pound rises value if pound rises 200 per pound then price a ford pickup now 25000 200 12500 a weaker dollar means foreign currency buys more dollars which means that us exports appear less expensive step 3 summarize that a weaker us dollar leads an increase us exports a foreign exporter outcome just opposite step 4 suppose a brewery england interested selling its bass ale a grocery store united states if price a six pack bass ale 600 exchange rate 130 per british pound price grocery store 600 130 780 per six pack if dollar weakens 200 per pound price bass ale now 600 200 12step 5 summarize that from perspective us purchasers a weaker dollar means that foreign currency more expensive which means that foreign goods are more expensive also this leads a decrease us imports which bad foreign exporter step 6 consider us tourists going abroad they face same situation as a us importerthey are purchasing a foreign trip a weaker dollar means that their trip will cost more since a given expenditure foreign currency eg hotel bill will take more dollars result that tourist may not stay as long abroad some may choose not travel at all step 7 consider that foreign tourist united states a weaker dollar a boon means their currency goes further so cost a trip united states will be less foreigners may choose take longer trips united states more foreign tourists may decide take us trips step 8 note that a us investor abroad faces same situation as a us importerthey are purchasing a foreign asset a us investor will see a weaker dollar as an increase price investment since same number dollars will buy less foreign currency thus less foreign assets this should decrease amount us investment abroad step 9 note also that foreign investors unites states will have opposite experience since foreign currency buys more dollars they will likely invest more us assets at this point you should have a good sense major players foreign exchange market firms involved international 16-self-check-questions.question: question 7 how would a contractionary monetary policy affect exchange rate net exports aggregate demand aggregate supply
16-self-check-questions.answer: 7 a contractionary monetary policy by driving up domestic interest rates would cause currency appreciate higher value currency foreign exchange markets would reduce exports since from perspective foreign buyers they are now more expensive higher value currency would similarly stimulate imports since they would now be cheaper from perspective domestic buyers lower exports higher imports cause net exports ex im fall which causes aggregate demand fall result would be a decrease gdp working through exchange rate mechanism reinforcing effect contractionary monetary policy has on domestic investment expenditure however cheaper imports would stimulate aggregate supply bringing gdp back potential though at a lower price level

34-3-arguments-in-support-of-restricting.question: infant industry argument imagine bhutan wants start its own computer industry but has no computer firms that can produce at a low enough price high enough quality compete world markets however bhutanese politicians business leaders workers hope that if local industry had a chance get established before needed face international competition then a domestic company or group companies could develop skills management technology economies scale that needs become a successful profitearning domestic industry thus infant industry argument protectionism block imports a limited time give infant industry time mature before starts competing on equal terms global economy revisit macroeconomic policy around world more information on infant industry argumentthe infant industry argument theoretically possible even sensible give an industry a shortterm indirect subsidy through protection then reap longterm economic benefits having a vibrant healthy industry implementation however tricky many countries infant industries have gone from babyhood senility obsolescence without ever having reached profitable maturity stage meanwhile protectionism that was supposed be shortterm often took a very long time be repealed as one example brazil treated its computer industry as an infant industry from late 1970s until about 1990 an attempt establish its computer industry global economy brazil largely barred imports computer products several decades this policy guaranteed increased sales brazilian computers however by mid1980s due lack international competition brazil had a backward outofdate industry typically lagging behind world standards price performance by three five yearsa long time this fastmoving industry after more than a decade during which brazilian consumers industries that would have benefited from uptodate computers paid costs brazils computer industry never competed effectively on world markets brazil phased out its infant industry policy computer industry protectionism infant industries always imposes costs on domestic users product typically has provided little benefit form stronger competitive industries however several countries east asia offer an exception japan korea thailand other countries this region have sometimes provided a package indirect direct subsidies targeted at certain industries including protection from foreign competition government loans at interest rates below market equilibrium japan korea example subsidies helped get their domestic steel auto industries up running why did infant industry policy protectionism other subsidies work fairly well east asia an early 1990 world bank study offered three guidelines countries thinking about infant industry protection do not hand out protectionism other subsidies all industries but focus on a few industries where your country has a realistic chance be a worldclass producer be very hesitant about using protectionism areas like computers where many other industries rely on having best products available because not useful help one industry by imposing high costs on many other industries have clear guidelines when infant industry policy will end korea 1970s 1980s a common practice was link protectionism subsidies export sales global markets if export sales rose then infant industry had succeeded government could phase out protectionism if export sales did not rise then infant industry policy had failed government could phase out protectionism either way protectionism would be temporaryfollowing these rules easier said than done politics often intrudes both choosing which industries will receive benefits treatment as infants when phase out import restrictions other subsidies also if countrys government wishes impose costs on its citizens so that can provide subsidies a few key industries has many tools doing such as direct government payments loans targeted tax reductions government support research development new technologies other words protectionism not only or even best way support key industries

antidumping argument dumping refers selling goods below their cost production antidumping laws block imports that are sold below cost production by imposing tariffs that increase price these imports reflect their cost production since dumping not allowed under world trade organization wto rules nations that believe they are on receiving end dumped goods can file a complaint wto according wto between 1995 2020 oversaw 137 antidumping disputes note that dumping cases are countercyclical during recessions case filings increase during economic booms case filings go down individual countries have also frequently started their own antidumping investigations us government has dozens antidumping orders place from past investigations 2022 example some us imports that were under antidumping orders included olives from spain steel from south korea coated paper from indonesia light commercial vehicles from germany italy fish fillets from vietnam cellulose pulp from canadawhy might dumping occur why would foreign firms export a product at less than its cost productionwhich presumably means taking a loss this question has two possible
34-3-arguments-in-support-of-restricting.answer: s one innocent one more sinister innocent explanation that demand supply set market prices not cost production perhaps demand a product shifts back left or supply shifts out right which drives market price low levelseven below cost production when a local store has a goingoutofbusiness sale example may sell goods at below cost production if international companies find that there excess supply steel or computer chips or machine tools that driving market price down below their cost productionthis may be market actionthe sinister explanation that dumping part a longterm strategy foreign firms sell goods at prices below cost production a short period time when they have driven out domestic us competition they then raise prices economists sometimes call this scenario predatory pricing which we discuss monopoly chaptershould antidumping cases be limited antidumping cases pose two questions how much sense do they make economic theory how much sense do they make as practical policy terms economic theory case antidumping laws weak a market governed by demand supply government does not guarantee that firms will be able make a profit after all low prices are difficult producers but benefit consumers moreover although there are plenty cases which foreign producers have driven out domestic firms there are zero documented cases which foreign producers then jacked up prices instead foreign producers typically continue competing hard against each other providing low prices consumers short difficult find evidence predatory pricing by foreign firms exporting united states even if one could make a case that government should sometimes enact antidumping rules short term then allow free trade resume shortly thereafter there a growing concern that antidumping investigations often involve more politics than careful analysis us commerce department charged calculating appropriate cost production which can be as much an art as a science example if a company built a new factory two years ago should count part factorys cost this years cost production when a company a country where government controls prices like china example how can one measure true cost production when a domestic industry complains loudly enough government regulators seem very likely find that unfair dumping has occurred a common pattern has arisen where a domestic industry files an antidumping complaint governments meet negotiate a reduction imports then domestic producers drop antidumping suit such cases antidumping cases often appear be little more than a cover story imposing tariffs or import quotasin 1980s united states canada european union australia new zealand implemented almost all antidumping cases by 2000s countries like argentina brazil south korea south africa mexico india were filing majority antidumping cases before wto as number antidumping cases has increased as countries such as united states european union feel targeted by antidumping actions others wto may well propose some additional guidelines limit reach antidumping laws

environmental protection a luxury as soon as their people have enough eat decent healthcare longer life expectancies then they will spend more money on items such as sewage treatment plants scrubbers reduce air pollution from factory smokestacks national parks protect wildlifethis gap environmental standards between highincome lowincome countries raises two worrisome possibilities a world cost them a substantial amount money paying damages lost trust reduced salesby building uptodate plants everywhere they minimize such risks as a result these factors foreignowned plants lowincome countries often have a better record compliance environmental laws than do locallyowned plants pressuring lowincome countries higher environmental standards some cases issue not so much whether how they should balance environmental goals against other priorities their citizens furthermore if highincome countries want stronger environmental standards lowincome countries they have many options industries key importance many lowincome countriesagriculture tourism environmental advocates can set up standards labeling products like this tuna caught a net that kept dolphins safe or this product about air water rights too wto also becoming more aware environmental issues more careful about ensuring that increases trade do not inflict environmental damagefinally note that these concerns about unsafe consumer products argument one argument shutting out certain imported products that they are unsafe consumers consumer rights groups have sometimes warned that world trade organization would require nations reduce their health safety standards imported products however wto explains its current agreement on subject this way allows countries set their own standards also says regulations must be based on science they should not arbitrarily or unjustifiably discriminate between countries where identical or similar conditions prevail thus example under wto rules perfectly legitimate united states pass laws requiring that all food products or cars sold united states meet certain safety standards approved by united states government whether or not other countries choose pass similar standards however such standards must have some scientific basis improper impose one set health safety standards domestically produced goods but a different set standards imports or one set standards imports from europe a different set standards imports from latin americain 2007 mattel recalled nearly two million toys imported from china due concerns about high levels lead paint as well as some loose parts unclear if other toys were subject similar standards 2013 japan blocked imports us wheat because concerns that genetically modified gmo wheat might be included shipments science on impact gmos on health still developing

national interest argument some argue that a nation should not depend too heavily on other countries supplies certain key products such as oil or special materials or technologies that might have reserve 1977 moreover may be necessary discourage people from using oil start a highpowered program seek out alternatives oil a straightforward way do this would be raise taxes on oil additionally makes no sense argue that because oil highly important united states economy then united states should shut out oil imports use up its domestic supplies more quickly us domestic oil production increasing shale oil adding domestic supply using fracking extraction techniques whether or not limit certain kinds imports key technologies or materials that might be important national security weapons systems a slightly different issue if weapons builders are not confident that they can continue obtain a key product wartime they might decide avoid designing weapons that use this key product materials from aluminum oxides antimony bauxite tungsten vegetable tannin extracts zinc although many these stockpiles have been reduced sold recent years think every country protrade how about us government removed wool from official list strategic materials 1960 subsidies mohair continued almost 40 years until government repealed them 1993 then reinstated them 2002 all too often national key strategic material

monetary fund which addresses issues raised by international financial transactions were both created 1946 third planned organization was be an international trade organization which would manage come together negotiate reductions tariffs other barriers trade 1995 gatt transformed into wtothe gatt process was negotiate an agreement reduce barriers trade sign that agreement pause a while then start negotiating next agreement table 344 shows rounds talks gatt now wto notice that early rounds gatt talks took a relatively short time included a small number countries focused almost entirely on reducing tariffs since mid1960s however rounds trade talks have taken years included a large number countries have included an everbroadening range issues year place or name round main subjects number countries involved 1947 geneva tariff reduction 23 1949 annecy tariff reduction 13 1951 torquay tariff reduction 38 1956 geneva tariff reduction 26 196061 dillon round tariff reduction 26 196467 kennedy round tariffs antidumping measures 62 197379 tokyo round tariffs nontariff barriers 102 198694 uruguay round tariffs nontariff barriers services intellectual property dispute settlement textiles agriculture creation wto 123 2001 doha round agriculture services intellectual property competition investment environment dispute settlement 147 table 344 negotiating rounds gatt world trade organization sluggish pace gatt negotiations led an old joke that gatt really stood gentlemans agreement talk talk slow pace international trade talks however understandable even sensible having dozens nations agree any treaty a lengthy process gatt often set up separate trading rules certain industries like agriculture separate trading rules certain countries like lowincome countries there were rules exceptions rules opportunities opt out rules precise wording be fought over every case like gatt before wto not a world government power impose its decisions on others total staff wto secretariat 2021 625 people its annual budget as 2020 197 million which makes smaller size than many large universities

regional trading agreements there are different types economic integration across globe ranging from free trade agreements which participants allow each others imports without tariffs or quotas common markets which participants have a common external trade policy as well as free trade within group full economic unions which addition a common market monetary fiscal policies are coordinated many nations belong both world trade organization regional trading agreements best known these regional trading agreements european union years after world war ii leaders several european nations reasoned that if they could tie their economies together more closely they might be more likely avoid another devastating war their efforts began a free trade association evolved into a common market then transformed into what now a full economic union known as european union eu as often called has a number goals example early 2000s introduced a common currency europe euro phased out most former national forms money like german mark french franc though a few have retained their own currency another key element union eliminate barriers mobility goods labor capital across europe 2016 britain 18-3-flaws-in-the-democratic-system-of-g.question: a balanced view markets government british statesman sir winston churchill 18741965 once wrote no one pretends that democracy perfect or allwise indeed has been said that democracy worst form government except all other forms which have been tried from time time that spirit theme this discussion certainly not that we should abandon democratic government a practical student public policy needs recognize that some cases like case wellorganized special interests or porkbarrel legislation a democratic government may seek enact economically unwise projects or programs other cases by placing a low priority on problems those who are not well organized or who are less likely vote government may fail act when could do some good these other cases there no automatic reason believe that government will necessarily make economically sensible choices true test a firstrate mind ability hold two contradictory ideas at same time wrote american author f scott fitzgerald 18961940 at this point your study microeconomics you should be able go one better than fitzgerald hold three somewhat contradictory ideas about interrelationship between markets government your mind at same time first markets are extraordinarily useful flexible institutions through which society can allocate its scarce resources we introduced this idea subjects international trade demand supply other chapters reinforced all subsequent discussions how households firms make decisionssecond markets may sometimes produce unwanted results a short list cases which markets produce unwanted results includes monopoly other cases imperfect competition pollution poverty inequality incomes discrimination failure provide insurance third while government may play a useful role addressing problems markets government action also imperfect may not reflect majority views economists readily admit that settings like monopoly or negative externalities a potential role exists government intervention however real world not enough point out that government action might be a good idea instead we must have some confidence that government likely identify carry out appropriate public policy make sensible judgments about economic policy we must see strengths weaknesses both markets government we must not idealize or demonize either unregulated markets or government actions instead consider actual strengths weaknesses realworld markets realworld governments these three insights seldom lead simple or obvious political conclusions as famous british economist joan robinson wrote some decades ago economic theory itself preaches no doctrines cannot establish any universally valid laws a method ordering ideas formulating questions study economics neither politically conservative nor moderate nor liberal there are economists who are democrats republicans libertarians socialists members every other political group you can name course conservatives may tend emphasize virtues markets limitations government while liberals may tend emphasize shortcomings markets need government programs such differences only illustrate that language terminology economics not limited one set political beliefs but can be used by all

18-3-flaws-in-the-democratic-system-of-g.question: bring home chinese tire tariffs april 2009 union representing us tire manufacturing workers filed a request us international trade commission itc asking investigate tire imports from china under us trade law if imports from a country increase point that they cause market disruption united states as determined by itc then can also recommend a remedy this market disruption this case itc determined that from 2004 2008 us tire manufacturers suffered declines production financial health employment as a direct result increases tire imports from china itc recommended placing an additional tax on tire imports from china president obama congress agreed itc recommendation june 2009 tariffs on chinese tires increased from 4 39 addition tariffs on chinese tires increased further as part president trumps increases on a broad range chinese productswhy would us consumers buy imported tires from china first place most likely because they are cheaper than tires produced domestically or other countries therefore this tariff increase should cause us consumers pay higher prices tires either because chinese tires are now more expensive or because us consumers are pushed by tariff buy more expensive tires made by us manufacturers or those from other countries end this tariff made us consumers pay more tires was this tariff met outrage expressed via social media traditional media or mass protests were there occupy wall streettype demonstrations
18-3-flaws-in-the-democratic-system-of-g.answer: a resounding no most us tire consumers were likely unaware tariff increase although they may have noticed price increase which was between 4 13 depending on type tire tire consumers are also potential voters conceivably a tax increase even a small one might make voters unhappy however voters probably realized that was not worth their time learn anything about this issue or cast a vote based on they probably thought their vote would not matter determining outcome an election or changing this policyestimates impact this tariff show costs us consumers around 111 billion annually this amount roughly 817 million ends up pockets foreign tire manufacturers other than china remaining 294 million goes us tire manufacturers other words tariff increase on chinese tires may have saved 1200 jobs domestic tire sector but cost 3700 jobs other sectors as consumers had reduce their spending because they were paying more tires people actually lost their jobs as a result this tariff workers us tire manufacturing firms earned about 40000 2010 given number jobs saved total cost us consumers cost saving one job amounted 926500this tariff caused a net decline us social surplus we discuss total surplus demand supply chapter tariffs introduction international trade chapter instead saving jobs cost jobs those jobs that saved cost many times more than people working them could ever hope earn why would government do thisthe chapter

18-3-flaws-in-the-democratic-system-of-g.question: a balanced view markets government british statesman sir winston churchill 18741965 once wrote no one pretends that democracy perfect or allwise indeed has been said that democracy worst form government except all other forms which have been tried from time time that spirit theme this discussion certainly not that we should abandon democratic government a practical student public policy needs recognize that some cases like case wellorganized special interests or porkbarrel legislation a democratic government may seek enact economically unwise projects or programs other cases by placing a low priority on problems those who are not well organized or who are less likely vote government may fail act when could do some good these other cases there no automatic reason believe that government will necessarily make economically sensible choices true test a firstrate mind ability hold two contradictory ideas at same time wrote american author f scott fitzgerald 18961940 at this point your study microeconomics you should be able go one better than fitzgerald hold three somewhat contradictory ideas about interrelationship between markets government your mind at same time first markets are extraordinarily useful flexible institutions through which society can allocate its scarce resources we introduced this idea subjects international trade demand supply other chapters reinforced all subsequent discussions how households firms make decisionssecond markets may sometimes produce unwanted results a short list cases which markets produce unwanted results includes monopoly other cases imperfect competition pollution poverty inequality incomes discrimination failure provide insurance third while government may play a useful role addressing problems markets government action also imperfect may not reflect majority views economists readily admit that settings like monopoly or negative externalities a potential role exists government intervention however real world not enough point out that government action might be a good idea instead we must have some confidence that government likely identify carry out appropriate public policy make sensible judgments about economic policy we must see strengths weaknesses both markets government we must not idealize or demonize either unregulated markets or government actions instead consider actual strengths weaknesses realworld markets realworld governments these three insights seldom lead simple or obvious political conclusions as famous british economist joan robinson wrote some decades ago economic theory itself preaches no doctrines cannot establish any universally valid laws a method ordering ideas formulating questions study economics neither politically conservative nor moderate nor liberal there are economists who are democrats republicans libertarians socialists members every other political group you can name course conservatives may tend emphasize virtues markets limitations government while liberals may tend emphasize shortcomings markets need government programs such differences only illustrate that language terminology economics not limited one set political beliefs but can be used by all
18-3-flaws-in-the-democratic-system-of-g.section_name: A Balanced View of Markets and Government

investors are trying do same thing thus attempting get rich stock market no help identify a company that going earn high profits if many other investors have already reached same conclusion because stock price will already be high based on expected high level future profitsthe idea that stock prices are based on expectations about future has a powerful unexpected implication if expectations determine stock price then shifts expectations will determine shifts stock price thus what matters predicting whether stock price a company will do well not whether company will actually earn profits future instead you must find a company that analysts widely believe at present have poor prospects but that will actually turn out be a shining star brigades stock market analysts individual investors are carrying out such research 24 hours a daythe fundamental problem predicting future stock winners that by definition no one can predict future news that alters expectations about profits because stock prices will shift response unpredictable future news these prices will tend follow what mathematicians call a random walk a trend random walk part means that on any given day stock prices are just as likely rise as fall a trend means that over time upward steps tend be larger than downward steps so stocks do gradually climb if stocks follow a random walk then not even financial professionals will be able choose those that will beat average consistently while some investment advisers are better than average any given year some even succeed a number years a row majority financial investors do not outguess market if we look back over time typically true that half or twothirds mutual funds that attempted pick stocks which would rise more than market average actually ended up performing worse than market average average investor who reads newspaper business pages over a cup coffee morning odds doing better than fulltime professionals not very good at all trying pick stocks that will gain a great deal future a risky unlikely way become rich

prices starting 2007 as home values dipped many home prices fell below amount borrower owed on mortgage owners stopped paying defaulted on their loan banks found that their assets loans became worthless many financial institutions around world had invested mortgagebacked securities or had purchased insurance on mortgagebacked securities when housing prices collapsed value those financial assets collapsed as well asset side banks balance sheets dropped causing bank failures bank runs around globe financial institutions were bankrupted or nearly so result was a large decrease lending borrowing or a freezing up available credit when credit dries up economy on its knees crisis was not limited united states iceland ireland united kingdom spain portugal greece all had similar housing boom bust cycles similar credit freezesif businesses cannot access financial capital they cannot make physical capital investments those investments ultimately lead job creation when credit dried up businesses invested less they ultimately laid off millions workers this caused incomes drop which caused demand drop turn businesses sold less so they laid off more workers compounding these events as economic conditions worsened financial institutions were even less likely make loansto make matters even worse as businesses sold less their expected future profit decreased this led a drop stock prices combining all these effects led major decreases incomes demand consumption employment great recession which united states officially lasted from december 2007 june 2009 during this time unemployment rate rose from 5 a peak 101 four years after recession officially ended unemployment was still stubbornly high at 76 118 million people were still unemployed as worlds leading consumer if united states goes into recession usually drags other countries down great recession was no exception few exceptions us trading partners also entered into recessions their own varying lengths or suffered slower economic growth like united states many european countries also gave direct financial assistance socalled bailouts institutions that make up their financial markets there was good reason do this financial markets bridge gap between demanders suppliers financial capital these institutions markets need function order an economy invest new financial capital however much this bailout money was borrowed this borrowed money contributed another crisis europe because impact on their budgets financial crisis resulting bailouts many countries found themselves unsustainably high deficits they chose undertake austerity measures large decreases government spending large tax increases order reduce their deficits greece ireland spain portugal all had undertake relatively severe austerity measures ramifications this crisis have spread economists even called into question euros viability
