17-2-how-households-supply-financial-cap.question: expected rate return risk actual rate return expected rate return refers how much a project or an investment expected return investor either future interest payments capital gains or increased profitability usually average return over a period time usually years or even decades we normally measure as a percentage rate risk measures uncertainty that projects profitability there are several types risk including default risk interest rate risk default risk as its name suggests risk that borrower fails pay back bond or loan interest rate risk danger that you might buy a long term bond at a 6 interest rate right before market rates suddenly rise so had you waited you could have received a similar bond that paid 9 a highrisk investment one which a wide range potential payoffs reasonably probable a lowrisk investment may have actual returns that are fairly close its expected rate return year after year a highrisk investment will have actual returns that are much higher than expected rate return some months or years much lower other months or years actual rate return refers total rate return including capital gains interest paid on an investment at end a time period
17-2-how-households-supply-financial-cap.section_name: Expected Rate of Return, Risk, and Actual Rate of Return

17-2-how-households-supply-financial-cap.question: bank accounts an intermediary one who stands between two other parties example a person who arranges a blind date between two other people one kind intermediary financial capital markets banks are an example a financial intermediarythat an institution that operates between a saver who deposits funds a bank a borrower who receives a loan from that bank when a bank serves as a financial intermediary unlike situation a couple on a blind date saver borrower never meet fact not even possible make direct connections between those who deposit funds banks those who borrow from banks because all deposited funds end up one big pool which financial institution then lends outfigure 173 illustrates position banks as a financial intermediary a pattern deposits flowing into a bank loans flowing out then repayment loans flowing back bank interest payments original savers banks offer a range accounts serve different needs a checking account typically pays little or no interest but facilitates transactions by giving you easy access your money either by writing a check or by using a debit card that a card which works like a credit card except that purchases are immediately deducted from your checking account rather than billed separately through a credit card company a savings account typically pays some interest rate but getting money typically requires you make a trip bank or an automatic teller machine or you can access funds electronically lines between checking savings accounts have blurred last couple decades as many banks offer checking accounts that will pay an interest rate similar a savings account if you keep a certain minimum amount account or conversely offer savings accounts that allow you write at least a few bonds an investor who buys a bond expects receive a rate return however bonds vary rates return that they offer according riskiness borrower we always can divide an interest rate into three components as we explained choice a world scarcity compensation delaying consumption an adjustment an inflationary rise overall level prices a risk premium that takes borrowers riskiness into accountthe us government an extremely safe borrower so when us government issues treasury bonds can pay a relatively low interest rate firms that appear be safe borrowers perhaps because their sheer size or because they have consistently earned profits over time will pay a higher interest rate than us government firms that appear be riskier borrowers perhaps because they are still growing or their businesses appear shaky will pay highest interest rates when they issue bonds we call bonds that offer high interest rates compensate their relatively high chance default highyield bonds or junk bonds a number todays wellknown firms issued junk bonds 1980s when they were starting grow including turner broadcasting microsofta bond issued by us government or a large corporation may seem be relatively low risk after all bond issuer has promised make certain payments over time except rare bankruptcy cases these payments will occur if a corporate bond issuer fails make payments that owes its bondholders bondholders can require that company declare bankruptcy sell off its assets pay them as much as can even case junk bonds a wise investor can reduce risk by purchasing bonds from a wide range different companies since even if a few firms go broke do not pay they are not all likely go bankruptas we noted before bonds carry an interest rate risk example imagine you decide buy a 10year bond 1000 that would pay an annual interest rate 8 soon after you buy bond interest rates on bonds rise so that now similar companies are paying an annual rate 12 anyone who buys a 1000 bond now can receive annual payments 120 per year but since your bond was issued at an interest rate 8 you have tied up 1000 receive payments only 80 per year meaningful sense opportunity cost you are missing out on higher payments that you could have received furthermore you can calculate amount you should be willing pay now future payments place a present discounted value on a future payment decide what you would need present equal a certain amount future this calculation will require an interest rate example if interest rate 25 then a payment 125 a year from now will have a present discounted value 100that you receives exchange capital money bond has a face value this amount borrower agrees pay investor at maturity bond has a coupon rate or interest rate which usually semiannual but can be paid at different times throughout year bonds used be paper documents coupons that investors clipped turned bank receive interest bond has a maturity date when borrower will pay back its face value as well as its last interest payment combining bonds face value interest rate maturity date market interest rates allows a buyer compute a bonds present value which most that a buyer would be willing pay a given bond drivers pay same 1860 then those low damages are effect paying those high damagesif possible classify drivers according risk group then insurance company can charge each group according its expected future or as a lowrisk driver who was just extremely unlucky driver likely claim be lowrisk thus someone who should be a risk group those who pay low insurance premiums future insurance company likely believe that on average having a major accident a signal being a highrisk driver thus try charge this driver higher insurance premiums next two sections discuss two major problems imperfect information insurance marketscalled moral hazard adverse selection both problems arise from attempts categorize those purchasing insurance into risk groups

moral hazard problem moral hazard refers case when people engage riskier behavior insurance than they would if they did not have insurance example if you have health insurance that covers cost visiting doctor you may be less likely take precautions against catching an illness that might require a doctors visit if you have car insurance you will worry less about driving or parking your car ways that make more likely get dented another example a business without insurance might install absolute toplevel security fire sprinkler systems guard against theft fire if insured that same business might only install a minimum level security fire sprinkler systems we cannot eliminate moral hazard but insurance companies have some ways reducing its effect investigations prevent insurance fraud are one way reducing extreme cases moral hazard insurance companies can also monitor certain kinds behavior return example from above they might offer a business a lower rate on property insurance if business installs a toplevel security fire sprinkler system has those systems inspected once a yearanother method reduce moral hazard require injured party pay a share costs example insurance policies often have deductibles which an amount that insurance policyholder must pay out their own pocket before insurance coverage starts paying example auto insurance might pay all losses greater than 500 health insurance policies often have a copayment which policyholder must pay a small amount example a person might have pay 20 each doctor visit insurance company would cover rest another method cost sharing coinsurance which means that insurance company covers a certain percentage cost example insurance might pay 80 costs repairing a home after a fire but homeowner would pay other 20all these forms cost sharing discourage moral hazard because people know that they will have pay something out their own pocket when they make an insurance claim effect can be powerful one prominent study found that when people face moderate deductibles copayments their health insurance they consume about onethird less medical care than people who have complete insurance do not pay anything out pocket presumably because deductibles copayments reduce level moral hazard however those who consumed less health care did not seem have any difference health statusa final way reducing moral hazard which especially applicable health care focus on healthcare incentives providers rather than consumers traditionally most health care united states has been provided on a feeforservice basis which means that medical care providers are paid services they provide are paid more if they provide additional services however last decade or so structure healthcare provision has shifted an emphasis on health maintenance organizations hmos a health maintenance organization hmo provides healthcare that receives a fixed amount per person enrolled planregardless how many services are provided this case a patient insurance has an incentive demand more care but healthcare provider which receiving only a fixed payment has an incentive reduce moral hazard problem by limiting quantity care providedas long as will not lead worse health problems higher costs later today many doctors are paid some combination managed care feeforservice that a flat amount per patient but additional payments treatment certain health conditionsimperfect information cause moral hazard problem if an insurance company had perfect information on risk could simply raise its premiums every time an insured party engages riskier behavior however an insurance company cannot monitor all risks that people take all time so even various checks cost sharing moral hazard will remain a problem

asymmetric information problem insurance company because buyers who are highrisk tend want buy more insurance without letting insurance company know about their higher risk example someone purchasing government social insurance federal state governments run a number insurance programs some programs look much like private insurance sense that members a group make steady payments into a fund those group who suffer an adverse experience receive payments other programs protect against risk but without an explicit fund set up following are some examplesunemployment insurance employers every state pay a small amount unemployment insurance which goes into a fund pay benefits workers who lose their jobs do not find new jobs a period time usually up six months pension insurance employers that offer pensions their retired employees are required by law pay a small fraction what they are setting aside pensions pension benefit guarantee corporation which pays at least some pension benefits workers if a company goes bankrupt cannot pay pensions has promised deposit insurance banks are required by law pay a small fraction their deposits federal deposit insurance corporation which goes into a fund that pays depositors value their bank deposits up 250000 amount was raised from 100000 250000 2008 if bank should go bankrupt workmans compensation insurance employers are required by law pay a small percentage salaries that they pay into funds typically run at state level that pay benefits workers who suffer an injury on job retirement insurance all workers pay a percentage their income into social security into medicare which then provides income health care benefits elderly social security medicare are not literally insurance sense that those currently contributing fund are not eligible benefits they function like insurance however sense that individuals make regular payments into programs today exchange benefits they will receive case a later eventeither becoming old or becoming sick when old a name such programs social insurance major additional costs insurance companies other than payment claims are costs running a business administrative costs hiring workers administering accounts processing insurance claims most insurance companies insurance premiums coming claims payments going out are much larger than amounts earned by investing money or administrative costs thus while factors like investment income earned on reserves administrative costs groups different risks complicate overall picture a fundamental law insurance must hold true average persons payments into insurance over time must cover 1 average persons claims 2 costs running company 3 leave room firms profits

risk groups actuarial fairness not all those who purchase insurance face same risks some people may be more likely because genetics or personal habits fall sick certain diseases some people may live an area where car theft or home robbery more likely than other areas some drivers are safer than others a risk group can be defined as a group that shares roughly same risks an adverse event occurringinsurance companies often classify people into risk groups charge lower premiums those lower risks if people are not separated into risk groups then those low risk must pay those high risks simple example how car insurance works 60 drivers had very low damage 100 each 30 drivers had mediumsized accidents that cost 1000 each 10 drivers had large accidents that cost 15000 if all 100 these drivers pay same 1860 then those low damages are effect paying those high damagesif possible classify drivers according risk group then insurance company can charge each group according its expected losses example insurance company might charge 60 drivers who seem safest all 100 apiece which average value damages they cause then intermediate group could pay 1000 apiece highcost group 15000 each when level insurance premiums that someone pays equal amount that an average person that risk group would collect insurance payments level insurance said be actuarially fairclassifying people into risk groups can be controversial example if someone had a major automobile accident last year should insurance company classify that person as a highrisk driver who likely have similar accidents future or as a lowrisk driver who was just extremely unlucky driver likely claim be lowrisk thus someone who should be a risk group those who pay low insurance premiums future insurance company likely believe that on average having a major accident a signal being a highrisk driver thus try charge this driver higher insurance premiums next two sections discuss two major problems imperfect information insurance marketscalled moral hazard adverse selection both problems arise from attempts categorize those purchasing insurance into risk groups

moral hazard problem moral hazard refers case when people engage riskier behavior insurance than they would if they did not have insurance example if you have health insurance that covers cost visiting doctor you may be less likely take precautions against catching an illness that might require a doctors visit if you have car insurance you will worry less about driving or parking your car ways that make more likely get dented another example a business without insurance might install absolute toplevel security fire sprinkler systems guard against theft fire if insured that same business might only install a minimum level security fire sprinkler systems we cannot eliminate moral hazard but insurance companies have some ways reducing its effect investigations prevent insurance fraud are one way reducing extreme cases moral hazard insurance companies can also monitor certain kinds behavior return example from above they might offer a business a lower rate on property insurance if business installs a toplevel security fire sprinkler system has those systems inspected once a yearanother method reduce moral hazard require injured party pay a share costs example insurance policies often have deductibles which an amount that insurance policyholder must pay out their own pocket before insurance coverage starts paying example auto insurance might pay all losses greater than 500 health insurance policies often have a copayment which policyholder must pay a small amount example a person might have pay 20 each doctor visit insurance company would cover rest another method cost sharing coinsurance which means that insurance company covers a certain percentage cost example insurance might pay 80 costs repairing a home after a fire but homeowner would pay other 20all these forms cost sharing discourage moral hazard because people know that they will have pay something out their own pocket when they make an insurance claim effect can be powerful one prominent study found that when people face moderate deductibles copayments their health insurance they consume about onethird less medical care than people who have complete insurance do not pay anything out pocket presumably because deductibles copayments reduce level moral hazard however those who consumed less health care did not seem have any difference health statusa final way reducing moral hazard which especially applicable health care focus on healthcare incentives providers rather than consumers traditionally most health care united states has been provided on a feeforservice basis which means that medical care providers are paid services they provide are paid more if they provide additional services however last decade or so structure healthcare provision has shifted an emphasis on health maintenance organizations hmos a health maintenance organization hmo provides healthcare that receives a fixed amount per person enrolled planregardless how many services are provided this case a patient insurance has an incentive demand more care but healthcare provider which receiving only a fixed payment has an incentive reduce moral hazard problem by limiting quantity care providedas long as will not lead worse health problems higher costs later today many doctors are paid some combination managed care feeforservice that a flat amount per patient but additional payments treatment certain health conditionsimperfect information cause moral hazard problem if an insurance company had perfect information on risk could simply raise its premiums every time an insured party engages riskier behavior however an insurance company cannot monitor all risks that people take all time so even various checks cost sharing moral hazard will remain a problem

adverse selection problem adverse selection refers problem which insurance buyers have more information about whether they are highrisk or lowrisk than insurance company does this creates an asymmetric information problem insurance company because buyers who are highrisk tend want buy more insurance without letting insurance company know about their higher risk example someone purchasing health insurance or life insurance probably knows more about their familys health history than an insurer can reasonably find out even a costly investigation someone purchasing car insurance may know that they are a highrisk driver who has not yet had a major accidentbut hard insurance company collect information about how people actually driveto understand how adverse selection can strangle an insurance market recall situation 100 drivers who are buying automobile insurance where 60 drivers had very low damages 100 each 30 drivers had mediumsized accidents that cost 1000 each 10 drivers had large accidents that cost 15000 that would equal 186000 total payouts by insurance company imagine that while insurance company knows overall size losses cannot identify highrisk mediumrisk lowrisk drivers however drivers themselves know their risk groups since there asymmetric information between insurance company drivers insurance company would likely set price insurance at 1860 per year cover average loss not including cost overhead profit result that those low risks only 100 will likely decide not buy insurance after all makes no sense them pay 1860 per year when they are likely only experience losses 100 those medium risks a 1000 accident will not buy insurance either therefore insurance company ends up only selling insurance 1860 highrisk drivers who will average 15000 claims apiece as a consequence insurance company ends up losing considerable money if insurance company tries raise its premiums cover losses those high risks then those low or medium risks will be even more discouraged from buying insurancerather than face such a situation adverse selection insurance company may decide not sell insurance this market at all if potential buyers are receive insurance then one two things must happen first insurance company might find some way separating insurance buyers into risk groups some degree accuracy charging them accordingly which practice often means that insurance company tries not sell insurance those who may pose high risks another scenario that those low risks must buy insurance even if they have pay more than actuarially fair amount their risk group notion that people can be required purchase insurance raises issue government laws regulations that influence insurance industry

possible way addressing moral hazard adverse selection problemsthe moral hazard problem health insurance that when people have insurance they will demand higher quantities health care united states redistribution redistribution means taking income from those higher incomes providing income those lower incomes earlier this chapter we considered some key government policies that provide support people experiencing poverty welfare program tanf earned income tax credit snap medicaid if a reduction inequality desired these programs could receive additional funding federal income tax which a progressive tax system designed such a way that rich pay a higher percent income taxes than poor funds programs data from household income tax returns 2018 shows that top 1 households had an average income 1679000 per year pretax income paid an average federal tax rate 254 effective income tax which total taxes paid divided by total income all sources income such as wages profits interest rental income government transfers such as veterans benefits was much lower effective tax paid by that top 1 householders paid was 204 while bottom two quintiles actually paid negative effective income taxes because provisions like earned income tax credit news stories occasionally report on a highincome person who has managed pay very little taxes but while such individual cases exist according congressional budget office typical pattern that people higher incomes pay a higher average share their income federal income taxesof course fact that some degree redistribution occurs now through federal income tax government antipoverty programs does not settle questions how much redistribution appropriate whether more redistribution should occur

ladder opportunityeconomic inequality perhaps most troubling when not result effort or talent but instead determined by circumstances under which a child grows up one child attends a wellrun grade school high school heads on college while parents help out by supporting education other interests paying college a first car a first house offering work connections that lead internships jobs another child attends a poorly run grade school barely makes through a lowquality high school does not go college lacks family peer support these two children may be similar their underlying talents effort they put forth but their economic outcomes are likely be quite different public policy can attempt build a ladder opportunities so that even though all children will never come from identical families attend identical schools each child has a reasonable opportunity attain an economic niche society based on their interests desires talents efforts table 158 shows some those initiatives children college level adults improved day care widespread loans grants those financial need opportunities retraining acquiring new skills enrichment programs preschoolers public support a range institutions from twoyear community colleges large research universities prohibiting discrimination job markets housing on basis race gender age disability improved public schools after school community activities internships apprenticeships table 158 public policy initiatives some have called united states a land opportunity although general idea a ladder opportunity all citizens continues exert a powerful attraction specifics are often quite controversial society can experiment a wide variety proposals building a ladder opportunity especially those who otherwise seem likely start their lives a disadvantaged position government needs carry out such policy experiments a spirit openmindedness because some will succeed while others will not show positive results or will cost too much enact on a widespread basis

inheritance taxes there always a debate about inheritance taxes goes like this why should people who have worked hard all their lives saved up a substantial nest egg not be able give their money possessions their children grandchildren particular would seem unamerican if children were unable inherit a family business or a family home alternatively many americans are far more comfortable inequality resulting from highincome people who earned their money by starting innovative new companies than they are inequality resulting from highincome people who have inherited money from rich parentsthe united states does have an estate taxthat a tax imposed on value an inheritancewhich suggests a willingness limit how much wealth one can pass on as an inheritance however 2022 estate tax applied only those leaving inheritances more than 1206 million thus applies only a tiny percentage those high levels wealth

tradeoff between incentives income equality government policies reduce poverty or encourage economic equality if carried extremes can injure incentives economic output poverty trap example defines a situation where guaranteeing a certain level income can eliminate or reduce incentive work an extremely high degree redistribution very high taxes on rich would be likely discourage work entrepreneurship thus common draw tradeoff between economic output equality as figure 1510 a shows this formulation if society wishes a high level economic output like point a must also accept a high degree inequality conversely if society wants a high level equality like point b must accept a lower level economic output because reduced incentives productionthis view tradeoff between economic output equality may be too pessimistic figure 1510 b presents an alternate vision here tradeoff between economic output equality first slopes up vicinity choice c suggesting that certain programs might increase both output economic equality example policy providing free public education has an element redistribution since value public schooling received by children lowincome families clearly higher than what lowincome families pay taxes a welleducated population however also an enormously powerful factor providing skilled workers tomorrow helping economy grow expand this case equality economic growth may complement each other moreover policies diminish inequality soften hardship poverty may sustain political support a market economy after all if society does not make some effort toward reducing inequality poverty alternative might be that people would rebel against market forces citizens might seek economic security by demanding that their legislators pass laws forbidding employers from ever laying off workers or reducing wages or laws that would impose price floors price ceilings shut off international trade from this viewpoint policies reduce inequality may help economic output by building social support allowing markets operate tradeoff figure 1510 b then flattens out area between points d e which reflects pattern that a number countries that provide similar levels income their citizensthe united states canada european union nations japan australiahave different levels inequality pattern suggests that countries this range could choose a greater or a lesser degree inequality without much impact on economic output only if these countries push a much higher level equality like at point f will they experience diminished incentives that lead lower levels economic output this view while a danger always exists that an agenda reduce poverty or inequality can be poorly designed or pushed too far also possible discover design policies that improve equality do not injure incentives economic output by very muchor even improve such incentives

15-5-government-policies-to-reduce-incom.question: bring home occupy wall street occupy movement took on a life its own over last few months 2011 bringing light issues that many people faced on lower end income distribution contents this chapter indicate that there a significant amount income inequality united states question what should be done about itthe 20082009 great recession caused unemployment rise incomes fall many people attribute recession mismanagement financial system by bankers financial managersthose 1 income distributionbut those lower quintiles bore greater burden recession through unemployment this seemed present picture inequality a different light group that seemed responsible recession was not group that seemed bear burden decline output a burden shared can bring a society closer together a burden pushed off onto others can polarize iton one level problem trying reduce income inequality comes down whether you still believe american dream if you believe that one day you will have your american dreama large income large house happy family or whatever else you would like have lifethen you do not necessarily want prevent anyone else from living out their dream you certainly would not want run risk that someone would want take part your dream away from you thus there some reluctance engage a redistributive policy reduce inequalityhowever when those whom likelihood living american dream very small are considered there are sound arguments favor trying create greater balance as text indicated a little more income equality gained through longterm programs like increased education job training can increase overall economic output then everyone made better off 1 will not seem like such a small group any more

redistribution redistribution means taking income from those higher incomes providing income those lower incomes earlier this chapter we considered some key government policies that provide support people experiencing poverty welfare program tanf earned income tax credit snap medicaid if a reduction inequality desired these programs could receive additional funding federal income tax which a progressive tax system designed such a way that rich pay a higher percent income taxes than poor funds programs data from household income tax returns 2018 shows that top 1 households had an average income 1679000 per year pretax income paid an average federal tax rate 254 effective income tax which total taxes paid divided by total income all sources income such as wages profits interest rental income government transfers such as veterans benefits was much lower effective tax paid by that top 1 householders paid was 204 while bottom two quintiles actually paid negative effective income taxes because provisions like earned income tax credit news stories occasionally report on a highincome person who has managed pay very little taxes but while such individual cases exist according congressional budget office typical pattern that people higher incomes pay a higher average share their income federal income taxesof course fact that some degree redistribution occurs now through federal income tax government antipoverty programs does not settle questions how much redistribution appropriate whether more redistribution should occur

ladder opportunityeconomic inequality perhaps most troubling when not result effort or talent but instead determined by circumstances under which a child grows up one child attends a wellrun grade school high school heads on college while parents help out by supporting education other interests paying college a first car a first house offering work connections that lead internships jobs another child attends a poorly run grade school barely makes through a lowquality high school does not go college lacks family peer support these two children may be similar their underlying talents effort they put forth but their economic outcomes are likely be quite different public policy can attempt build a ladder opportunities so that even though all children will never come from identical families attend identical schools each child has a reasonable opportunity attain an economic niche society based on their interests desires talents efforts table 158 shows some those initiatives children college level adults improved day care widespread loans grants those financial need opportunities retraining acquiring new skills enrichment programs preschoolers public support a range institutions from twoyear community colleges large research universities prohibiting discrimination job markets housing on basis race gender age disability improved public schools after school community activities internships apprenticeships table 158 public policy initiatives some have called united states a land opportunity although general idea a ladder opportunity all citizens continues exert a powerful attraction specifics are often quite controversial society can experiment a wide variety proposals building a ladder opportunity especially those who otherwise seem likely start their lives a disadvantaged position government needs carry out such policy experiments a spirit openmindedness because some will succeed while others will not show positive results or will cost too much enact on a widespread basis

inheritance taxes there always a debate about inheritance taxes goes like this why should people who have worked hard all their lives saved up a substantial nest egg not be able give their money possessions their children grandchildren particular would seem unamerican if children were unable inherit a family business or a family home alternatively many americans are far more comfortable inequality resulting from highincome people who earned their money by starting innovative new companies than they are inequality resulting from highincome people who have inherited money from rich parentsthe united states does have an estate taxthat a tax imposed on value an inheritancewhich suggests a willingness limit how much wealth one can pass on as an inheritance however 2022 estate tax applied only those leaving inheritances more than 1206 million thus applies only a tiny percentage those high levels wealth

tradeoff between incentives income equality government policies reduce poverty or encourage economic equality if carried extremes can injure incentives economic output poverty trap example defines a situation where guaranteeing a certain level income can eliminate or reduce incentive work an extremely high degree redistribution very high taxes on rich would be likely discourage work entrepreneurship thus common draw tradeoff between economic output equality as figure 1510 a shows this formulation if society wishes a high level economic output like point a must also accept a high degree inequality conversely if society wants a high level equality like point b must accept a lower level economic output because reduced incentives productionthis view tradeoff between economic output equality may be too pessimistic figure 1510 b presents an alternate vision here tradeoff between economic output equality first slopes up vicinity choice c suggesting that certain programs might increase both output economic equality example policy providing free public education has an element redistribution since value public schooling received by children lowincome families clearly higher than what lowincome families pay taxes a welleducated population however also an enormously powerful factor providing skilled workers tomorrow helping economy grow expand this case equality economic growth may complement each other moreover policies diminish inequality soften hardship poverty may sustain political support a market economy after all if society does not make some effort toward reducing inequality poverty alternative might be that people would rebel against market forces citizens might seek economic security by demanding that their legislators pass laws forbidding employers from ever laying off workers or reducing wages or laws that would impose price floors price ceilings shut off international trade from this viewpoint policies reduce inequality may help economic output by building social support allowing markets operate tradeoff figure 1510 b then flattens out area between points d e which reflects pattern that a number countries that provide similar levels income their citizensthe united states canada european union nations japan australiahave different levels inequality pattern suggests that countries this range could choose a greater or a lesser degree inequality without much impact on economic output only if these countries push a much higher level equality like at point f will they experience diminished incentives that lead lower levels economic output this view while a danger always exists that an agenda reduce poverty or inequality can be poorly designed or pushed too far also possible discover design policies that improve equality do not injure incentives economic output by very muchor even improve such incentives

15-3-the-safety-net.question: temporary assistance needy families from great depression until 1996 united states most visible antipoverty program was aid families dependent children afdc which provided cash payments mothers children who were below poverty line many just called this program welfare 1996 congress passed president bill clinton signed into law personal responsibility work opportunity reconciliation act more commonly called welfare reform act new law replaced afdc temporary assistance needy families tanftanf brought several dramatic changes how welfare operated under old afdc program states set level welfare benefits that they would pay people experiencing poverty federal government guaranteed would chip some money as well federal governments welfare spending would rise or fall depending on number people need on how each state set its own welfare contribution under tanf however federal government gives a fixed amount money each state state can then use money almost any program an antipoverty component example state might use money give funds families low income or reduce teenage pregnancy or even raise high school graduation rate however federal government imposed two key requirements first if states are keep receiving tanf grants they must impose work requirements so that most those receiving tanf benefits are working or attending school second no one can receive tanf benefits federal money more than a total five years over their lifetime old afdc program had no such work requirements or time limits tanf attempts avoid poverty trap by requiring that welfare recipients work by limiting length time they can receive benefits its first few years program was quite successful number families receiving payments 1995 last year afdc was 48 million november 2020 according congressional research service number families receiving payments under tanf was 10 milliona decline nearly 80 tanf benefits poor families vary considerably across states example again according congressional research service july 2020 highest monthly payment new hampshire a single mother one child was 862 while mississippi highest monthly payment that family was 146 part these payments reflect differences states cost living as reported by department health human services 1995 total spending on tanf was approximately 19 billion spending increased yearly through 2001 then was roughly flat at approximately 26 billion until 2005 then increased again through 2010 where peaked at nearly 35 billion then decreased again around 30 billion 2020 when you take into account effects inflation decline even greater moreover there seemed little evidence that families were suffering a reduced standard living as a result tanfalthough on other side there was not much evidence that families had greatly improved their total levels income either

earned income tax credit eitc earned income tax credit eitc first passed 1975 a method assisting working poor through tax system eitc one largest assistance program lowincome groups as december 2021 about 25 million eligible workers families received about 60 billion eitc 2021 tax year earned income credit ranges from 1502 6728 depending on taxfiling status income number children average amount eitc received nationwide was about 2411 2021 example a single parent two children would have received a tax credit 5980 up a modest income level amount tax break increases amount income earned up a point earned income tax credit has often been popular both economists general public because way effectively increases payment received workwhat about danger poverty trap that every additional 1 earned will reduce government support payments by close 1 minimize this problem earned income tax credit phased out slowly example according tax policy center a singleparent family two children 2013 credit not reduced at all but neither increased as earnings rise from 13430 17530 then every 1 earned above 17530 amount received from credit reduced by 2106 cents until credit phases out completely at an income level 46227 figure 155 illustrates that earned income tax credits child tax credits tanf program all cost federal government moneyeither direct outlays or loss tax revenues ctc stands government tax cuts child tax credit recent years eitc has become a hugely expensive government program providing income assistance people below or near poverty line costing about 60 billion 2021 that year eitc provided benefits about 25 million families individuals on average worth about 2411 per family children according tax policy center one reason that tanf law worked as well as did that government greatly expanded eitc late 1980s again early 1990s which increased returns work lowincome americans

supplemental nutrition assistance program snap often called food stamps supplemental nutrition assistance program snap a federally funded program started 1964 which each month people receive a card like a debit card that they can use buy food amount food aid which a household eligible varies by income number children other factors but general households are expected spend about 30 their own net income on food if 30 their net income not enough purchase a nutritionally adequate diet then those households are eligible snapsnap can contribute poverty trap every 100 earned government assumes that a family can spend 30 more food thus reduces its eligibility food aid by 30 this decreased benefit not a complete disincentive workbut combined how other programs reduce benefits as income increases adds problem snap however does try address poverty trap its own set work requirements time limits why give debit cards not just cash part political support snap comes from a belief that since recipients must spend cards on food they cannot waste them on other forms consumption from an economic point view however belief that cards must increase spending on food seems wrongheaded after all say that a family spending 2500 per year on food then starts receiving 1000 per year snap aid family might react by spending 3500 per year on food income plus aid or might react by continuing spend 2500 per year on food but use 1000 food aid free up 1000 that can now spend on other goods thus reasonable think snap cards as an alternative method along tanf earned income tax credit transferring income those working but still experiencing povertyanyone eligible tanf also eligible snap although states can expand eligibility food aid if they wish do so some states where tanf welfare spending relatively low a poor family may receive more support from snap than from tanf 2021 about 415 million people received food aid total benefits just over 108 billion which an average monthly benefit about 287 per person per month snap participation increased by 70 between 2007 2011 from 266 million participants 45 million according congressional budget office 20082009 great recession rising food prices caused this dramatic rise participation likewise between 2019 2021 number participants snap increased by 58 million amount per person increased by 67 total benefits nearly doubled as a consequence sharp recession due onset covid19 pandemic early 2020the federal government deploys a range income security programs that funds through departments such as health human services agriculture housing urban development hud see figure 156 according office management budget collectively these three departments provided an estimated 62 billion aid through programs such as supplemental feeding programs women children subsidized housing energy assistance federal government also transfers funds individual states through special grant programsthe safety net includes a number other programs governmentsubsidized school lunches breakfasts children from lowincome families special supplemental food program women infants children wic which provides food assistance pregnant women newborns low income home energy assistance program which provides help home heating bills housing assistance which helps pay rent supplemental security income which provides cash support people disabilities elderly people experiencing poverty

those who are blind people disabilities children receive 21 all medicaid spending followed by adults at 15

16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.question: lemons other examples imperfect information consider marvin who trying decide whether buy a used car lets assume that marvin truly clueless about what happens inside a cars engine he willing do some background research like reading consumer reports or checking websites that offer information about used car makes models what they should cost he might pay a mechanic inspect car even after devoting some money time collecting information however marvin still cannot be absolutely sure that he buying a highquality used car he knows that he might buy car drive home use a few weeks before discovering that car a lemon which slang a defective product especially a carimagine that marvin shops a used car finds two that look very similar terms mileage exterior appearances age one car costs 4000 while other car costs 4600 which car should marvin buy if marvin were choosing a world perfect information
16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.answer: would be simple he should buy cheaper car however marvin operating a world imperfect information where sellers likely know more about cars problems than he does have an incentive hide information after all more problems sellers disclose lower cars selling pricewhat should marvin do first he needs understand that even imperfect information prices still reflect information typically used cars are more expensive on some dealer lots because dealers have a trustworthy reputation uphold those dealers try fix problems that may not be obvious their customers order create good word mouth about their vehicles long term reliability short term benefits selling their customers a lemon could cause a quick collapse dealers reputation a loss long term profits on other lots that are less wellestablished one can find cheaper used cars but buyer takes on more risk when a dealers reputation has little at stake cheapest cars all often appear on craigslist where individual seller has no reputation defend sum cheaper prices do carry more risk so marvin should balance his appetite risk versus potential headaches many more unanticipated trips repair shop similar problems imperfect information arise labor financial capital markets consider greta who applying a job her potential employer like used car buyer concerned about ending up a lemonin this case a poor quality employee employer will collect information about gretas academic work history end however a degree uncertainty will inevitably remain regarding gretas abilities which are hard demonstrate without actually observing her on job how can a potential employer screen certain attributes such as motivation timeliness ability get along others employers often look trade schools colleges prescreen candidates employers may not even interview a candidate unless he has a degree sometimes a degree from a particular school employers may also view awards a high grade point average other accolades as a signal hard work perseverance ability employers may also seek references insights into key attributes such as energy level work ethic
16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.section_name: “Lemons” and Other Examples of Imperfect Information

16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.question: how imperfect information can affect equilibrium price quantity presence imperfect information can discourage both buyers sellers from participating market buyers may become reluctant participate because they cannot determine products quality sellers highquality or mediumquality goods may be reluctant participate because difficult demonstrate quality their goods buyersand since buyers cannot determine which goods have higher quality they are likely be unwilling pay a higher price such goodseconomists sometimes refer a market few buyers few sellers as a thin market by contrast they call a market many buyers sellers a thick market when imperfect information severe buyers sellers are discouraged from participating markets may become extremely thin as a relatively small number buyer sellers attempt communicate enough information that they can agree on a price
16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.section_name: How Imperfect Information Can Affect Equilibrium Price and Quantity

16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.question: when price mixes imperfect information about quality a buyer confronted imperfect information will often believe that price reveals something about products quality example a buyer may assume that a gemstone or a used car that costs more must be higher quality even though buyer not an expert on gemstones think expensive restaurant where food must be good because so expensive or shop where clothes must be stylish because they cost so much or gallery where art must be great because price tags are high if you are hiring a lawyer you might assume that a lawyer who charges 400 per hour must be better than a lawyer who charges 150 per hour these cases price can act as a signal qualitywhen buyers use market price draw inferences about products quality then markets may have trouble reaching an equilibrium price quantity imagine a situation where a used car dealer has a lot full used cars that do not seem be selling so dealer decides cut car prices sell a greater quantity a market imperfect information many buyers may assume that lower price implies lowquality cars as a result lower price may not attract more customers conversely a dealer who raises prices may find that customers assume that higher price means that cars are higher quality as a result raising prices dealer might sell more cars whether or not consumers always behave rationally as an economist would see subject following clear up featurethe idea that higher prices might cause a greater quantity demanded that lower prices might cause a lower quantity demanded runs exactly counter basic model demand supply as we outlined demand supply chapter these contrary effects however will reach natural limits at some point if price high enough quantity demanded will decline conversely when price declines far enough buyers will increasingly find value even if quality lower addition information eventually becomes more widely known an overpriced restaurant that charges more than quality its food worth many buyers will not last forever
16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.section_name: When Price Mixes with Imperfect Information about Quality

16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.question: mechanisms reduce risk imperfect information if you were selling a good like emeralds or used cars where imperfect information likely be a problem how could you reassure possible buyers if you were buying a good where imperfect information a problem what would take reassure you buyers sellers goods market rely on reputation as well as guarantees warrantees service contracts assure product quality labor market uses occupational licenses certifications assure competency while financial capital market uses cosigners collateral as insurance against unforeseen detrimental eventsin goods market seller might offer a moneyback guarantee an agreement that functions as a promise quality this strategy may be especially important a company that sells goods through mailorder catalogs or over web whose customers cannot see actual products because encourages people buy something even if they are not certain they want keep itll bean started using moneybackguarantees 1911 when founder stitched waterproof shoe rubbers together leather shoe tops sold them as hunting shoes he guaranteed satisfaction however stitching came apart out first batch 100 pairs that were sold customers returned 90 pairs ll bean took out a bank loan repaired all shoes replaced them ll bean reputation customer satisfaction began spread many firms today offer moneybackguarantees a few weeks or months but ll bean offers a one year moneyback guarantee past ll bean offered a lifetime moneyback guarantee which allowed customers always return anything they had bought from ll bean no matter how many years later or what condition product was a full refundll bean has very few stores instead most its sales are made by mail telephone or now through their website this kind firm imperfect information may be an especially difficult problem because customers cannot see touch what they are buying a combination a moneyback guarantee a reputation quality can help a mailorder firm flourish sellers may offer a warranty which a promise fix or replace good at least a certain time period seller may also offer a buyer a chance buy a service contract where buyer pays an extra amount seller agrees fix anything that goes wrong a set time period service contracts are often an option buyers large purchases such as cars appliances even housesguarantees warranties service contracts are examples explicit reassurance that sellers provide many cases firms also offer unstated guarantees example some movie theaters might refund ticket cost a customer who walks out complaining about show likewise while restaurants do not generally advertise a moneyback guarantee or exchange policies many restaurants allow customers exchange one dish another or reduce price bill if customer not satisfiedthe rationale these policies that firms want repeat customers who turn will recommend business others as such establishing a good reputation paramount importance when buyers know that a firm concerned about its reputation they are less likely worry about receiving a poorquality product example a wellestablished grocery store a good reputation can often charge a higher price than a temporary stand at a local farmers market where buyer may never see seller againsellers labor provide information through resumes recommendations school transcripts examples their work labor market also uses occupational licenses establish quality labor market occupational licenses which government agencies typically issue show that a worker has completed a certain type education or passed a certain test some professionals who must hold a license are doctors teachers nurses engineers accountants lawyers addition most states require a license work as a barber an embalmer a dietitian a massage therapist a hearing aid dealer a counselor an insurance agent a real estate broker some other jobs require a license only one state minnesota requires a state license be a field archaeologist north dakota has a state license bait retailers louisiana one needs a state license be a stress analyst california requires a state license be a furniture upholsterer according a 2013 study from university chicago about 29 us workers have jobs that require occupational licensesoccupational licenses have their downside as well as they represent a barrier entry certain industries this makes more difficult new entrants compete incumbents which can lead higher prices less consumer choice occupations that require licenses government has decided that additional information provided by licenses outweighs negative effect on competition on buyers side labor market a standard precaution against hiring a lemon an employee specify that first few months employment are officially a trial or probationary period that employer can dismiss worker any reason or no reason during that time sometimes workers also receive lower pay during this trial periodin financial capital market before a bank makes a loan requires a prospective borrower fill out forms regarding incomes sources addition bank conducts a credit check on individuals past borrowing another approach require a cosigner on a loan that another person or firm who legally pledges repay some or all money if original borrower does not do so another approach require collateral often property or equipment that bank would have a right seize sell if borrower does not repay loanbuyers goods services cannot possibly become experts evaluating quality gemstones used cars lawyers everything else they buy employers lenders cannot be perfectly omniscient about whether possible workers will turn out well or potential borrowers will repay loans on time however mechanisms that we mentioned above can reduce risks associated imperfect information so that buyer seller are willing proceed
16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.section_name: Mechanisms to Reduce the Risk of Imperfect Information

16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.question: how imperfect information can affect equilibrium price quantity presence imperfect information can discourage both buyers sellers from participating market buyers may become reluctant participate because they cannot determine products quality sellers highquality or mediumquality goods may be reluctant participate because difficult demonstrate quality their goods buyersand since buyers cannot determine which goods have higher quality they are likely be unwilling pay a higher price such goodseconomists sometimes refer a market few buyers few sellers as a thin market by contrast they call a market many buyers sellers a thick market when imperfect information severe buyers sellers are discouraged from participating markets may become extremely thin as a relatively small number buyer sellers attempt communicate enough information that they can agree on a price
16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.section_name: How Imperfect Information Can Affect Equilibrium Price and Quantity

16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.question: when price mixes imperfect information about quality a buyer confronted imperfect information will often believe that price reveals something about products quality example a buyer may assume that a gemstone or a used car that costs more must be higher quality even though buyer not an expert on gemstones think expensive restaurant where food must be good because so expensive or shop where clothes must be stylish because they cost so much or gallery where art must be great because price tags are high if you are hiring a lawyer you might assume that a lawyer who charges 400 per hour must be better than a lawyer who charges 150 per hour these cases price can act as a signal qualitywhen buyers use market price draw inferences about products quality then markets may have trouble reaching an equilibrium price quantity imagine a situation where a used car dealer has a lot full used cars that do not seem be selling so dealer decides cut car prices sell a greater quantity a market imperfect information many buyers may assume that lower price implies lowquality cars as a result lower price may not attract more customers conversely a dealer who raises prices may find that customers assume that higher price means that cars are higher quality as a result raising prices dealer might sell more cars whether or not consumers always behave rationally as an economist would see subject following clear up featurethe idea that higher prices might cause a greater quantity demanded that lower prices might cause a lower quantity demanded runs exactly counter basic model demand supply as we outlined demand supply chapter these contrary effects however will reach natural limits at some point if price high enough quantity demanded will decline conversely when price declines far enough buyers will increasingly find value even if quality lower addition information eventually becomes more widely known an overpriced restaurant that charges more than quality its food worth many buyers will not last forever
16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.section_name: When Price Mixes with Imperfect Information about Quality

16-1-the-problem-of-imperfect-informatio.question: mechanisms reduce risk imperfect information if you were selling a good like emeralds or used cars where imperfect information likely be a problem how could you reassure possible buyers if you were buying a good where imperfect information a problem what would take reassure you buyers sellers goods market rely on reputation as well as guarantees warrantees service contracts assure product quality labor market uses occupational licenses certifications assure competency while financial capital market uses cosigners collateral as insurance against unforeseen detrimental eventsin goods market seller might offer a moneyback guarantee an agreement that functions as a promise quality this strategy may be especially important a company that sells goods through mailorder catalogs or over web whose customers cannot see actual products because encourages people buy something even if they are not certain they want keep itll bean started using moneybackguarantees 1911 when founder stitched waterproof shoe rubbers together leather shoe tops sold them as hunting shoes he guaranteed satisfaction however stitching came apart out first batch 100 pairs that were sold customers returned 90 pairs ll bean took out a bank loan repaired all shoes replaced them ll bean reputation customer satisfaction began spread many firms today offer moneybackguarantees a few weeks or months but ll bean offers a one year moneyback guarantee past ll bean offered a lifetime moneyback guarantee which allowed customers always return anything they had bought from ll bean no matter how many years later or what condition product was a full refundll bean has very few stores instead most its sales are made by mail telephone or now through their website this kind firm imperfect information may be an especially difficult problem because customers cannot see touch what they are buying a combination a moneyback guarantee a reputation quality can help a mailorder firm flourish sellers may offer a warranty which a promise fix or replace good at least a certain time period seller may also offer a buyer a chance buy a service contract where buyer pays an extra amount seller agrees fix anything that goes wrong a set time period service contracts are often an option buyers large purchases such as cars appliances even housesguarantees warranties service contracts are examples explicit reassurance that sellers provide many cases firms also offer unstated guarantees example some movie theaters might refund ticket cost a customer who walks out complaining about show likewise while restaurants do not generally advertise a moneyback guarantee or exchange policies many restaurants allow customers exchange one dish another or reduce price bill if customer not satisfiedthe rationale these policies that firms want repeat customers who turn will recommend business others as such establishing a good reputation paramount importance when buyers know that a firm concerned about its reputation they are less likely worry about receiving a poorquality product example a wellestablished grocery store a good reputation can often charge a higher price than a temporary stand at a local farmers market where buyer may never see seller againsellers labor provide information through resumes recommendations school transcripts examples their work labor market also uses occupational licenses establish quality labor market occupational licenses which government agencies typically issue show that a worker has completed a certain type education or passed a certain test some professionals who must hold a license are doctors teachers nurses engineers accountants lawyers addition most states require a license work as a barber an embalmer a dietitian a massage therapist a hearing aid dealer a counselor an insurance agent a real estate broker some other jobs require a license only one state minnesota requires a state license be a field archaeologist north dakota has a state license bait retailers louisiana one needs a state license be a stress analyst california requires a state license be a furniture upholsterer according a 2013 study from university chicago about 29 us workers have jobs that require occupational licensesoccupational licenses have their downside as well as they represent a barrier entry certain industries this makes more difficult new entrants compete incumbents which can lead higher prices less consumer choice occupations that require licenses government has decided that additional information provided by licenses outweighs negative effect on competition on buyers side labor market a standard precaution against hiring a lemon an employee specify that first few months employment are officially a trial or probationary period that employer can dismiss worker any reason or no reason during that time sometimes workers also receive lower pay during this trial periodin financial capital market before a bank makes a loan requires a prospective borrower fill out forms regarding incomes sources addition bank conducts a credit check on individuals past borrowing another approach require a cosigner on a loan that another person or firm who legally pledges repay some or all money if original borrower does not do so another approach require collateral often property or equipment that bank would have a right seize sell if borrower does not repay loanbuyers goods services cannot possibly become experts evaluating quality gemstones used cars lawyers everything else they buy employers lenders cannot be perfectly omniscient about whether possible workers will turn out well or potential borrowers will repay loans on time however mechanisms that we mentioned above can reduce risks associated imperfect information so that buyer seller are willing proceed

14-6-immigration.question: most americans would be outraged if a law prevented them from moving another city or another state however when conversation turns crossing national borders about other people arriving united states laws preventing such movement often seem more reasonable some tensions over immigration stem from worries over how might affect a countrys culture including differences language patterns family authority or gender relationships economics does not have much say about such cultural issues some worries about immigration do however have do its effects on wages income levels how affects government taxes spending on those topics economists have insights research offer economic effects immigration a surge immigration can affect economy a number different ways this section we will consider how immigrants might benefit rest economy how they might affect wage levels how they might affect government spending at federal local level understand economic consequences immigration consider following scenario imagine that immigrants entering united states matched existing us population age range education skill levels family size occupations how would immigration discrimination persists if a business located an area a large minority population refuses sell minorities will cut into its own profits if some businesses run by bigoted employers refuse pay women andor minorities a wage based on their productivity then other profitseeking employers can hire these workers a competitive market if business owners care more about color money than about color skin they will have an incentive make buying selling hiring promotion decisions strictly based on economic factorsdo not underestimate power markets offer at least a degree freedom oppressed groups many countries cohesive minority population groups like jewish people emigrant chinese people have managed carve out a space themselves through their economic activities despite legal social discrimination against them many immigrants including those who come united states have taken advantage economic freedom make new lives themselves however history teaches that market forces alone are unlikely levels among managers among workers among customers consider situation a store owner or manager who not personally prejudiced but who has many customers who are prejudiced if that manager treats all groups fairly manager may find drives away prejudiced customers such a situation a policy nondiscrimination could reduce firms profits after all a business firm part society a firm that does not follow societal norms likely suffer as economist william a darity jr points out however prejudiced customer rationale falls apart when considering many jobs that have no customer contact darity overcome employment wage discrimination on their own are unlikely end such discrimination future

14-self-check-questions.question: question 6 are firms a high percentage union employees more likely go bankrupt because higher wages that they pay why or why not
14-self-check-questions.answer: 6 no while some unions may cause firms go bankrupt other unions help firms become more competitive no overall pattern exists

14-self-check-questions.question: question 11 if immigration reduced what impact on wage lowskilled labor explain
14-self-check-questions.answer: 11 if a large share immigrants have relatively low skills then reducing number immigrants would shift supply curve lowskill labor back left which would tend raise equilibrium wage lowskill labor

14-self-check-questions.question: question 7 do countries a higher percentage unionized workers usually have less growth productivity because strikes other disruptions caused by unions why or why not
14-self-check-questions.answer: 7 from a social point view benefits unions costs seem counterbalance there no evidence that countries a higher percentage unionized workers economies grow more or less slowly

14-self-check-questions.question: question 4 do unions typically oppose new technology out a fear that will reduce number union jobs why or why not
14-self-check-questions.answer: 4 unions have sometimes opposed new technology out a fear losing jobs but other cases unions have helped facilitate introduction new technology because unionized workers felt that union was looking after their interests or that their higher skills meant that their jobs were essentially protected new technologies meant increased productivity

14-self-check-questions.question: question 5 compared share workers most other highincome countries share us workers whose wages are determined by union bargaining higher or lower why or why not
14-self-check-questions.answer: 5 a few other countries such as france spain percentage workers belonging a union similar that united states union membership rates however are generally lower united states when share workers whose wages are determined by union negotiations considered united states ranks by far lowest because countries like france spain union negotiations often determine pay even nonunion employees

14-self-check-questions.question: question 10 does earnings gap between average wages females average wages males prove labor market discrimination why or why not
14-self-check-questions.answer: 10 no earnings gap does not prove discrimination because does not compare wages men women same job who have same amounts education experience productivity

14-self-check-questions.answer: 7 from a social point view benefits unions costs seem counterbalance there no evidence that countries a higher percentage unionized workers economies grow more or less slowly

14-self-check-questions.question: question 10 does earnings gap between average wages females average wages males prove labor market discrimination why or why not
14-self-check-questions.answer: 10 no earnings gap does not prove discrimination because does not compare wages men women same job who have same amounts education experience productivity

14-self-check-questions.question: question 6 are firms a high percentage union employees more likely go bankrupt because higher wages that they pay why or why not
14-self-check-questions.answer: 6 no while some unions may cause firms go bankrupt other unions help firms become more competitive no overall pattern exists

14-self-check-questions.question: question 9 explain each following situations how market forces might give a business an incentive act a less discriminatory fashion a local flower delivery business that had intentionally served only white customers notices that many local residents are black an assembly line has traditionally only hired men but having a hard time hiring sufficiently qualified workers a relationship counselor who had a strict policy only serving straight couples notes significant increase lgbtq people moving into town
14-self-check-questions.answer: 9 firms have a profit incentive sell everyone regardless race ethnicity religion or gender a business that needs hire workers expand may also find that if draws only from its accustomed pool workerssay white menit lacks workers needs expand production such a business would have an incentive hire more women minorities a discriminatory business that underpaying its workers may find those workers leaving jobs another employer who offers better pay this market pressure could cause discriminatory business behave better

14-self-check-questions.question: question 4 do unions typically oppose new technology out a fear that will reduce number union jobs why or why not
14-self-check-questions.answer: 4 unions have sometimes opposed new technology out a fear losing jobs but other cases unions have helped facilitate introduction new technology because unionized workers felt that union was looking after their interests or that their higher skills meant that their jobs were essentially protected new technologies meant increased productivity
