13-key-concepts-and-summary.question: 131 investments innovation competition creates pressure innovate however if one can easily copy new inventions then original inventor loses incentive invest further research development new technology often has positive externalities that there are often spillovers from invention new technology that benefit firms other than innovator social benefit an invention once firm accounts these spillovers typically exceeds private benefit inventor if inventors could receive a greater share broader social benefits their work they would have a greater incentive seek out new inventions 132 how governments can encourage innovation public policy regard technology must often strike a balance example patents provide an incentive inventors but they should be limited genuinely new inventions not extend forever government has a variety policy tools increasing rate return new technology encouraging its development including direct government funding rd tax incentives rd protection intellectual property forming cooperative relationships between universities private sector 133 public goods a public good has two key characteristics nonexcludable nonrival nonexcludable means that costly or impossible one user exclude others from using good nonrival means that when one person uses good does not prevent others from using markets often have a difficult time producing public goods because free riders will attempt use public good without paying one can overcome free rider problem through measures assure that users public good pay such measures include government actions social pressures specific situations where markets have discovered a way collect payments

131 building blocks neoclassical analysis neoclassical perspective argues that long run economy will adjust back its potential gdp level output through flexible price levels thus neoclassical perspective views longrun as curve as vertical a rational expectations perspective argues that people have excellent information about economic events how economy works that as a result price other economic adjustments will happen very quickly adaptive expectations theory people have limited information about economic information how economy works so price other economic adjustments can be slow 132 policy implications neoclassical perspective neoclassical economists tend put relatively more emphasis on longterm growth than on fighting recession because they believe that recessions will fade a few years longterm growth will ultimately determine standard living they tend focus more on reducing natural rate unemployment caused by economic institutions government policies than cyclical unemployment caused by recession neoclassical economists also see no social benefit inflation an upwardsloping keynesian as curve inflation can arise because an economy approaching full employment a vertical longrun neoclassical as curve inflation does not accompany any rise output if aggregate supply vertical then aggregate demand does not affect quantity output instead aggregate demand can only cause inflationary changes price level a vertical aggregate supply curve where quantity output consistent many different price levels also implies a vertical phillips curve 133 balancing keynesian neoclassical models keynesian perspective considers changes aggregate demand be cause business cycle fluctuations keynesians are likely advocate that policy makers actively attempt reverse recessionary inflationary periods because they are not convinced that selfcorrecting economy can easily return full employment neoclassical perspective places more emphasis on aggregate supply neoclassical economists believe that long term productivity growth determines potential gdp level that economy typically will return full employment after a change aggregate demand skeptical effectiveness timeliness keynesian policy neoclassical economists are more likely advocate a handsoff or fairly limited role active stabilization policywhile keynesians would tend advocate an acceptable tradeoff between inflation unemployment when counteracting a recession neoclassical economists argue that no such tradeoff exists any shortterm gains lower unemployment will eventually vanish result active policy will only be inflation

13-3-public-goods.question: definition a public good economists have a strict definition a public good does not necessarily include all goods financed through taxes understand defining characteristics a public good first consider an ordinary private good like a piece pizza we can buy sell a piece pizza fairly easily because a separate identifiable item however public goods are not separate identifiable this wayinstead public goods have two defining characteristics they are nonexcludable nonrival first characteristic that a public good nonexcludable means that costly or impossible exclude someone from using good if larry buys a private good like a piece pizza then he can exclude others like lorna from eating that pizza however if national defense provided then includes everyone even if you strongly disagree americas defense policies or level defense spending national defense still protects you you cannot choose be unprotected national defense cannot protect everyone else exclude youthe second main characteristic a public good that nonrival means that when one person uses public good another can also use a private good like pizza if max eating pizza then michelle cannot also eat that two people are rivals consumption a public good like national defense maxs consumption national defense does not reduce amount left michelle so they are nonrival this areaa number government services are examples public goods instance would not be easy provide fire police service so that some people a neighborhood would be protected from burning burglary their property while others would not be protected at all protecting some necessarily means protecting others too positive externalities public goods are closely related concepts public goods have positive externalities like police protection or public health funding not all goods services positive externalities however are public goods investments education have huge positive spillovers but can be provided by a private company private companies can invest new inventions such as apple ipad reap profits that may not capture all social benefits we can also describe patents as an attempt make new inventions into private goods which are excludable rivalrous so that no one but inventor can use them during length patent
13-3-public-goods.section_name: The Definition of a Public Good

13-3-public-goods.question: free rider problem public goods private companies find difficult produce public goods if a good or service nonexcludable like national defense so that impossible or very costly exclude people from using this good or service then how can a firm charge people when individuals make decisions about buying a public good a free rider problem can arise which people have an incentive let others pay public good then free ride on purchases others we can express free rider problem terms prisoners dilemma game which we discussed as a representation oligopoly monopolistic competition oligopolythere a dilemma prisoners dilemma though see work out feature work out problem prisoners dilemma suppose two people rachel samuel are considering purchasing a public good difficulty prisoners dilemma arises as each person thinks through their strategic choicesstep 1 rachel reasons this way if samuel does not contribute then i would be a fool contribute however if samuel does contribute then i can come out ahead by not contributing step 2 either way i should choose not contribute instead hope that i can be a free rider who uses public good paid by samuel step 3 samuel reasons same way about rachel step 4 when both people reason that way public good never gets built there no movement option where everyone cooperateswhich actually best all parties
13-3-public-goods.section_name: The Free Rider Problem of Public Goods

13-3-public-goods.question: role government paying public goods key insight paying public goods find a way assuring that everyone will make a contribution prevent free riders example if people come together through political process agree pay taxes make group decisions about quantity public goods they can defeat free rider problem by requiring through law that everyone contributes however government spending taxes are not only way provide public goods some cases markets can produce public goods example think about radio nonexcludable since once radio signal broadcast would be very difficult stop someone from receiving nonrival since one person listening signal does not prevent others from listening as well because these features practically impossible charge listeners directly listening conventional radio broadcastsradio has found a way collect revenue by selling advertising which an indirect way charging listeners by taking up some their time ultimately consumers who purchase goods advertised are also paying radio service since station builds cost advertising into product cost a more recent development satellite radio companies such as siriusxm charge a regular subscription fee streaming music without commercials this case however product excludableonly those who pay subscription will receive broadcastsome public goods will also have a mixture public provision at no charge along fees some purposes like a public city park that free use but government charges a fee parking your car reserving certain picnic grounds food sold at a refreshment stand other cases we can use social pressures personal appeals rather than force law reduce number free riders collect resources public good example neighbors sometimes form an association carry out beautification projects or patrol their area after dark discourage crime lowincome countries where social pressure strongly encourages all farmers participate farmers a region may come together work on a large irrigation project that will benefit all we can view many fundraising efforts including raising money local charities endowments colleges universities as an attempt use social pressure discourage free riding generate outcome that will produce a public benefit
13-3-public-goods.section_name: The Role of Government in Paying for Public Goods

13-3-public-goods.question: common resources tragedy commons there are some goods that do not fall neatly into categories private good or public good while easy classify a pizza as a private good a city park as a public good what about an item that nonexcludable rivalrous such as queen conch caribbean queen conch a large marine mollusk that lives shallow waters sea grass these waters are so shallow so clear that a single diver may harvest many conch a single day not only conch meat a local delicacy an important part local diet but artists use large ornate shells craftsmen transform them because almost anyone a small boat snorkel mask can participate conch harvest essentially nonexcludable at same time fishing conch rivalrous once a diver catches one conch another diver cannot catch itwe call goods that are nonexcludable rivalrous common resources because waters caribbean are open all conch fishermen because any conch that you catch a conch that i cannot catch fishermen tend overharvest common resources like conchthe problem overharvesting common resources not a new one but ecologist garret hardin put tag tragedy commons problem a 1968 article magazine science economists view this as a problem property rights since nobody owns ocean or conch that crawl on sand beneath no one individual has an incentive protect that resource responsibly harvest address issue overharvesting conch other marine fisheries economists have advocated simple devices like fishing licenses harvest limits shorter fishing seasons one approach that has been turned more recently implementation catch shares whereby regulators establish a total allowable catch then fishermen are allocated a portion that total allowable catch catch shares appear slow race fish when population a species drops critically low numbers governments have even banned harvest until biologists determine that population has returned sustainable levels fact such case conch harvesting which government has effectively banned united states since 1986 tragedy commons a frequent economic social framework discussions about a range common resources even extending into digital resources such as open media repositories online libraries prominent economist elinor ostrom first woman receive nobel prize economics proposed an alternate version sometimes referred as nontragedy commons after extensive fieldwork areas as diverse as indonesia kenya maine us nepal she challenged notion that people would only avoid depletion common resources if they were forced by regulatory laws property rights she noted that farmers working shared land could communicate cooperate order maximize preserve fields over time she argued that when those who benefit most from a resource are close proximity like a farm field that directly serves a town resource better managed without external influence

components aggregate demand tight or contractionary monetary policy that leads higher interest rates a reduced quantity loanable funds will reduce two components aggregate demand business investment will decline because less attractive firms borrow money even firms that have money will notice that higher interest rates relatively more attractive put those funds a financial investment than make an investment physical capital addition higher interest rates will discourage consumer borrowing bigticket items like houses cars conversely loose or expansionary monetary policy that leads lower interest rates a higher quantity loanable funds will tend increase business investment consumer borrowing bigticket items if economy suffering a recession high unemployment output below potential gdp expansionary monetary policy can help economy return potential gdp figure 156 a illustrates this situation this example uses a shortrun upwardsloping keynesian aggregate supply curve sras original equilibrium during a recession e0 occurs at an output level 600 an expansionary monetary policy will reduce interest rates stimulate investment consumption spending causing original aggregate demand curve ad0 shift right ad1 so that new equilibrium e1 occurs at potential gdp level 700conversely if an economy producing at a quantity output above its potential gdp a contractionary monetary policy can reduce inflationary pressures a rising price level figure 156 b original equilibrium e0 occurs at an output 750 which above potential gdp a contractionary monetary policy will raise interest rates discourage borrowing investment consumption spending cause original demand curve ad0 shift left ad1 so that new equilibrium e1 occurs at potential gdp level 700these examples suggest that monetary policy should be countercyclical that should act counterbalance business cycles economic downturns upswings fed should loosen monetary policy when a recession has caused unemployment increase tighten when inflation threatens course countercyclical policy does pose a danger overreaction if loose monetary policy seeking end a recession goes too far may push aggregate demand so far right that triggers inflation if tight monetary policy seeking reduce inflation goes too far may push aggregate demand so far left that a recession begins figure 157 a summarizes chain effects that connect loose tight monetary policy changes output price level

15-3-how-a-central-bank-executes-monetar.question: open market operations since early 1920s most common monetary policy tool us has been open market operations these take place when central bank sells or buys us treasury bonds order influence quantity bank reserves level interest rates specific interest rate targeted open market operations federal funds rate name a bit a misnomer since federal funds rate interest rate that commercial banks charge making overnight loans other banks as such a very short term interest rate but one that reflects credit conditions financial markets very wellthe federal open market committee fomc makes decisions regarding these open market operations fomc comprises seven members federal reserves board governors also includes five voting members who board draws on a rotating basis from regional federal reserve banks new york district president a permanent fomc voting member board fills other four spots on a rotating annual basis from other 11 districts fomc typically meets every six weeks but can meet more frequently if necessary fomc tries act by consensus however federal reserves chairman has traditionally played a very powerful role defining shaping that consensus federal reserve most central banks open market operations have over last few decades been most commonly used tool monetary policy understand how open market operations affect money supply a limited reserves environment assume that happy bank starts 460 million assets divided among reserves bonds loans 400 million liabilities form deposits a net worth 60 million when central bank purchases 20 million bonds from happy bank bond holdings happy bank fall by 20 million banks reserves rise by 20 million however happy bank only wants hold 40 million reserves so bank decides loan out extra 20 million reserves its loans rise by 20 million central banks open market operation causes happy bank make loans instead holding its assets form government bonds which expands money supply as new loans are deposited banks throughout economy these banks will turn loan out some deposits they receive triggering money multiplier that we discussed money banking see impact on happy banks balance sheet refer appendix how open market operations affect balance sheets a limited reserves environment where did federal reserve get 20 million that used purchase bonds a central bank has power create money practical terms federal reserve would write a check happy bank so that happy bank can have that money credited its bank account at federal reserve truth federal reserve created money purchase bonds out thin airor a few clicks on some computer keys open market operations can also reduce quantity money loans an economy when happy bank purchases 30 million bonds happy bank sends 30 million its reserves central bank but now holds an additional 30 million bonds however happy bank wants hold 40 million reserves as our previous example so will adjust down quantity its loans by 30 million bring its reserves back desired level practical terms a bank can easily reduce its quantity loans at any given time a bank receiving payments on loans that made previously also making new loans if bank just slows down or briefly halts making new loans instead adds those funds its reserves then its overall quantity loans will decrease a decrease quantity loans also means fewer deposits other banks other banks reducing their lending as well as money multiplier that we discussed money banking takes effect see impact on happy banks balance sheet refer appendix how open market operations affect balance sheets a limited reserves environment what about all those bonds how do they affect money supply read following clear up feature
15-3-how-a-central-bank-executes-monetar.answer: federal reserve was founded aftermath 1907 financial panic when many banks failed as a result bank runs as mentioned earlier since banks make profits by lending out their deposits no bank even those that are not bankrupt can withstand a bank run as a result panic federal reserve was founded be lender last resort event a bank run sound banks banks that were not bankrupt could borrow as much cash as they needed from feds discount window quell bank run we call interest rate banks pay such loans discount rate they are so named because bank makes loans against its outstanding loans at a discount their face value once depositors became convinced that bank would be able honor their withdrawals they no longer had a reason make a run on bank short federal reserve was originally intended provide credit passively but years since its founding fed has taken on a more active role monetary policy

15-3-how-a-central-bank-executes-monetar.question: changing discount rate federal reserve act phrase afford means rediscounting commercial paper contained its long title this was main tool monetary policy when fed was initially created today federal reserve has even more tools at its disposal including quantitative easing overnight repurchase agreements interest on excess reserves this illustrates how monetary policy has evolved how continues do so second traditional method conducting monetary policy a limited reserve environment raise or lower discount rate if central bank raises discount rate then commercial banks will reduce their borrowing reserves from fed instead call loans replace those reserves since fewer loans are available money supply falls market interest rates rise if central bank lowers discount rate charges banks process works reversein recent decades federal reserve has made relatively few discount loans before a bank borrows from federal reserve fill out its required reserves bank expected first borrow from other available sources like other banks this encouraged by fed charging a higher discount rate than federal funds rate given that most banks borrow little at discount rate changing discount rate up or down has little impact on their behavior more importantly fed has found from experience that open market operations are a more precise powerful means executing any desired monetary policy

changing reserve requirements a potential third method conducting monetary policy a limited reserves environment central bank raise or lower reserve requirement which as we noted earlier percentage each banks deposits that legally required hold either as cash their vault or on deposit central bank if banks are required hold a greater amount reserves they have less money available lend out if banks are allowed hold a smaller amount reserves they will have a greater amount money available lend out until very recently federal reserve required banks hold reserves equal 0 first 145 million deposits then hold reserves equal 3 deposits up 1036 million 10 any amount above 1036 million fed makes small changes reserve requirements almost every year example 1036 million dividing line sometimes bumped up or down by a few million dollars today these rates are no longer effect as march 2020 when pandemicinduced recession hit 10 3 requirements were reduced 0 effectively eliminating reserve requirement all depository institutions fed rarely uses large changes reserve requirements execute monetary policy pandemic was an exception obvious reasons also a sudden demand that all banks increase their reserves would be extremely disruptive difficult them comply while loosening requirements too much might create a danger banks inability meet withdrawal demands benefits reducing reserve requirements march 2020 exceeded risks following section we will discuss how interest on excess reserves used as a monetary policy tool

monetary policy ample reserves as we noted previously banks united states have historically had very little reason keep more than their minimum required reserves because their regional federal reserve bank did not pay any interest on those reserves this behavior changed dramatically during 20082009 financial crisis during this period time 389 banks failed banks that survived responded financial crisis by increasing their reserves well beyond their required minimum combined measures undertaken by fomc us government respond financial crisis banks reserves increased from around 15 billion 2007 27 trillion by late 2014 while reserves did decrease around 17 trillion by 2017 this was no longer an environment limited reserves but an environment ample reserves fact 2019 fomc issued a statement indicating that monetary policy would be based on ample reserves while may be a good decision banks keep ample reserves as we know banking system facilitates both shortterm longterm economic activity when makes loans finance consumption business investment expansion help fund support innovation among many other possibilities a sense loans allow economy ultimately become more productive causing an increase longrun economic growth point that we need banking system be willing make loans not just keep ample excess reserves however when banks are keeping a trillion or more dollars as excess reserves these are funds that they are choosing not lend out potentially causing economy grow at a slower rate than otherwise might how then can fomc incentivize banks lend out these excess reserves this where interest rate on reserve balances iorb comes iorb interest rate paid banks their holdings excess reserves congress granted federal reserve ability pay this interest 2006 policy was originally slated begin 2011 but financial crisis accelerated its start date 2008 a few years there were two separate rates interest rate on required reserves interest rate on excess reserves but these two were combined into iorb 2021 fomc controls iorb directly sets iorb at whatever rate chooses based on macroeconomic conditions forecasts we can now explore how iorb may affect banks decisions hold more or fewer excess reserves as we noted our discussion open market operations federal funds rate ffr specific interest rate targeted by fomc federal funds market where banks borrow lend their excess reserves from one another over a very short period time often described as overnight a market as we explained demand supply supply demand a price federal funds market you can think ffr as price a bank gets paid lending or selling excess reserves federal funds market you can think ffr as price a bank pays borrowing or buying those excess reserves ffr targeted by fomc because as ffr increases decreases most other interest rates eventually increase or decrease too monetary policy economic outcomes well show how changes interest rates affect macroeconomy illustrate how changes iorb can affect ffr assume that iorb 2 which means that a bank can earn 2 on its excess reserves free risk because federal funds market lending period often very shortterm sometimes even shorter than one day those loans are nearly riskfree as a result most banks view iorb as a very close alternative ffr if ffr also pays 2 interest generally a bank will be indifferent where keeps its excess reserves lets say that macroeconomic conditions convince fomc lower ffr do this will lower iorb lets see why if iorb decreases 175 banks will generally choose reduce their excess reserves holdings at their regional federal reserve bank instead lend those excess reserves federal funds market order earn currently higher ffr 2 but this increase supply excess reserves federal funds market will act lower price federal funds market which ffr when fomc lowers iorb also tends lower discount rate at same time some banks could then engage arbitrage which simultaneous or nearsimultaneous purchase sale a good profit from a difference price that good across markets our example if discount rate also decreases from 2 175 a bank could borrow excess reserves from federal reserve at 175 discount rate then lend those same excess reserves federal funds market at 2 earning 00025 on every 100 borrowed this may not seem like much but multiply this by 1 million or 10 million adds up quickly but this arbitrage activity also ensures that ffr will decrease as fomc desires because increase supply excess reserves will cause ffr decrease as banks leave federal funds market borrow from federal reserve decrease demand excess reserves will cause ffr decrease too alternatively lets say that macroeconomic conditions convince fomc raise ffr if fomc increases iorb 225 banks will then choose keep more excess reserves at their regional federal reserve bank earn higher iorb this decrease supply federal funds market will cause ffr increase as well arbitrage will also ensure that ffr increases as iorb increases banks will borrow more excess reserves federal funds market deposit them their regional federal reserve bank order earn a profit on difference between iorb ffr this increase demand will then cause ffr increase as well

quantitative easing most powerful commonly used three traditional tools monetary policyopen market operationsworks by expanding or contracting money supply a way that influences interest rate late 2008 as us economy struggled recession federal reserve had already reduced interest rate nearzero recession still ongoing fed decided adopt an innovative nontraditional policy known as quantitative easing qe this purchase longterm government private mortgagebacked securities by central banks make credit available so as stimulate aggregate demand quantitative easing differed from traditional monetary policy several key ways first involved fed purchasing long term treasury bonds rather than short term treasury bills 2008 however was impossible stimulate economy any further by lowering short term rates because they were already as low as they could get read closing bring home feature more on this therefore chairman bernanke sought lower longterm rates utilizing quantitative easing this leads a second way qe different from traditional monetary policy instead purchasing treasury securities fed also began purchasing private mortgagebacked securities something had never done before during financial crisis which precipitated recession mortgagebacked securities were termed toxic assets because when housing market collapsed no one knew what these securities were worth which put financial institutions which were holding those securities on very shaky ground by offering purchase mortgagebacked securities fed was both pushing long term interest rates down also removing possibly toxic assets from balance sheets private financial firms which would strengthen financial system quantitative easing qe occurred three episodes during qe1 which began november 2008 fed purchased 600 billion mortgagebacked securities from government enterprises fannie mae freddie mac november 2010 fed began qe2 which purchased 600 billion us treasury bonds qe3 began september 2012 when fed commenced purchasing 40 billion additional mortgagebacked securities per month this amount was increased december 2012 85 billion per month fed stated that when economic conditions permit will begin tapering or reducing monthly purchases by october 2014 fed had announced final 15 billion bond purchase ending quantitative easing we usually think quantitative easing policies that federal reserve adopted as did other central banks around world as temporary emergency measures if these steps are be temporary then federal reserve will need stop making these additional loans sell off financial securities has accumulated concern that process quantitative easing may prove more difficult reverse than was enact evidence suggests that qe1 was somewhat successful but that qe2 qe3 have been less sofast forward march 2020 when fed under leadership jerome powell promised another round asset purchases which was dubbed by some as qe4 intended once again provide liquidity a distressed financial system wake pandemic this round was much faster increasing fed assets by 2 trillion just a few months recently fed has begun slow down purchase these assets once again through a taper pace tapering expected increase through 2022 but as end 2021 total fed assets exceed 8 trillion compared 4 trillion february 2020

15-1-the-federal-reserve-banking-system-.question: structureorganization federal reserve unlike most central banks federal reserve semidecentralized mixing government appointees representation from privatesector banks at national level run by a board governors consisting seven members appointed by president united states confirmed by senate appointments are 14year terms they are arranged so that one term expires january 31 every evennumbered year purpose long staggered terms insulate board governors as much as possible from political pressure so that governors can make policy decisions based only on their economic merits additionally except when filling an unfinished term each member only serves one term further insulating decisionmaking from politics feds policy decisions do not require congressional approval president cannot ask a federal reserve governor resign as president can cabinet positionsone member board governors designated as chair example from 1987 until early 2006 chair was alan greenspan from 2006 until 2014 ben bernanke held post from 2014 2018 janet yellen was chair current chair jerome powell see following clear up feature find out more about former current chair fed chair first among equals on board governors while they have only one vote chair controls agenda feds public voice so they have more power influence than one might expect federal reserve more than board governors fed also includes 12 regional federal reserve banks each which responsible supporting commercial banks economy generally its district figure 153 shows federal reserve districts cities where their regional headquarters are located commercial banks each district elect a board directors each regional federal reserve bank that board chooses a president each regional federal reserve district thus federal reserve system includes both federally privatesector appointed leaders

what does a central bank do federal reserve like most central banks designed perform three important functions conduct monetary policy promote stability financial system provide banking services commercial banks other depository institutions provide banking services federal government first two functions are sufficiently important that we will discuss them their own modules third function we will discuss herethe federal reserve provides many same services banks as banks provide their customers example all commercial banks have an account at fed where they deposit reserves similarly banks can obtain loans from fed through discount window facility which we will discuss more detail later fed also responsible check processing when you write a check example buy groceries grocery store deposits check its bank account then grocery stores bank returns physical check or an image that actual check your bank after which transfers funds from your bank account grocery stores account fed responsible each these actionson a more mundane level federal reserve ensures that enough currency coins are circulating through financial system meet public demands example each year fed increases amount currency available banks around christmas shopping season reduces again january finally fed responsible assuring that banks are compliance a wide variety consumer protection laws example banks are forbidden from discriminating on basis age race sex or marital status banks are also required disclose publicly information about loans they make buying houses how they distribute loans geographically as well as by sex race loan applicants

15-2-bank-regulation.question: bank supervision several government agencies monitor banks balance sheets make sure they have positive net worth are not taking too high a level risk within us department treasury office comptroller currency has a national staff bank examiners who conduct onsite reviews 1500 or so largest national banks bank examiners also review any foreign banks that have branches united states office comptroller currency also monitors regulates about 800 savings loan institutionsthe national credit union administration ncua supervises credit unions which are nonprofit banks that their members run own there are about 5000 credit unions us economy today although typical credit union small compared most banksthe federal reserve also has some responsibility supervising financial institutions example we call conglomerate firms that own banks other businesses bank holding companies while other regulators like office comptroller currency supervises banks federal reserve supervises holding companieswhen bank supervision banklike institutions such as savings loans credit unions works well most banks will remain financially healthy most time if bank supervisors find that a bank has low or negative net worth or making too high a proportion risky loans they can require that bank change its behavioror extreme cases even force bank close or be sold a financially healthy bankbank supervision can run into both practical political questions practical question that measuring value a banks assets not always straightforward as we discussed money banking a banks assets are its loans value these assets depends on estimates about risk that customers will not repay these loans these issues can become even more complex when a bank makes loans banks or firms other countries or arranges financial deals that are much more complex than a basic loanthe political question arises because a bank supervisors decision require a bank close or change its financial investments often controversial bank supervisor often comes under political pressure from banks owners local politicians keep quiet back offfor example many observers have pointed out that japans banks were deep financial trouble through most 1990s however nothing substantial had been done about by early 2000s a similar unwillingness confront problems struggling banks visible across rest world east asia latin america eastern europe russia elsewhere united states government passed laws 1990s requiring that bank supervisors make their findings open public that they act as soon as they identify a problem however as many us banks were staggered by 20082009 recession critics bank regulators asked pointed questions about why regulators had not foreseen banks financial shakiness earlier before such large losses had a chance accumulate

bank runs back nineteenth century during first few decades twentieth century around during great depression putting your money a bank could be nervewracking imagine that net worth your bank became negative so that banks assets were not enough cover its liabilities this situation whoever withdrew their deposits first received all their money those who did not rush bank quickly enough lost their money we call depositors racing bank withdraw their deposits as figure 154 shows a bank run movie its a wonderful life bank manager played by jimmy stewart faces a mob worried bank depositors who want withdraw their money but manages allay their fears by allowing some them withdraw a portion their depositsusing money from his own pocket that was supposed pay his honeymoonthe risk bank runs created instability banking system even a rumor that a bank might experience negative net worth could trigger a bank run a bank run even healthy banks could be destroyed because a bank loans out most money receives because keeps only limited reserves on hand a bank run any size would quickly drain any banks available cash when bank had no cash remaining only intensified fears remaining depositors that they could lose their money moreover a bank run at one bank often triggered a chain reaction runs on other banks late nineteenth early twentieth century bank runs were typically not original cause a recessionbut they could make a recession much worse

deposit insurance protect against bank runs congress has put two strategies into place deposit insurance lender last resort deposit insurance an insurance system that makes sure depositors a bank do not lose their money even if bank goes bankrupt about 70 countries around world including all major economies have deposit insurance programs united states federal deposit insurance corporation fdic responsible deposit insurance banks pay an insurance premium fdic insurance premium based on banks level deposits then adjusted according riskiness a banks financial situation 2009 example a fairly safe bank a high net worth might have paid 1020 cents insurance premiums every 100 bank deposits while a risky bank very low net worth might have paid 5060 cents every 100 bank deposits bank examiners from fdic evaluate banks balance sheets looking at asset liability values determine risk level fdic provides deposit insurance about 4914 banks as third quarter 2021 even if a bank fails government guarantees that depositors will receive up 250000 their money each account which enough almost all individuals although not sufficient many businesses since united states enacted deposit insurance 1930s no one has lost any their insured deposits bank runs no longer happen at insured banks

lender last resort problem bank runs not that insolvent banks will fail they are after all bankrupt need be shut down problem that bank runs can cause solvent banks fail spread rest financial system prevent this fed stands ready lend banks other financial institutions when they cannot obtain funds from anywhere else this known as lender last resort role banks central bank acting as a lender last resort helps reinforce effect deposit insurance reassure bank customers that they will not lose their money lender last resort task can arise other financial crises as well during 1987 stock market crash panic when us stock values fell by 25 a single day federal reserve made a number shortterm emergency loans so that financial system could keep functioning during 20082009 recession we can interpret feds quantitative easing policies discussed below as a willingness make shortterm credit available as needed a time when banking financial system was under stress

14-1-defining-money-by-its-functions.question: functions money money solves problems that barter system creates we will get its definition soon first money serves as a medium exchange which means that money acts as an intermediary between buyer seller instead exchanging accounting services shoes accountant now exchanges accounting services money accountant then uses this money buy shoes serve as a medium exchange people must widely accept money as a method payment markets goods labor financial capitalsecond money must serve as a store value a barter system we saw example shoemaker trading shoes accounting services however she risks having her shoes go out style especially if she keeps them a warehouse future usetheir value will decrease each season shoes are not a good store value holding money a much easier way storing value you know that you do not need spend immediately because will still hold its value next day or next year this function money does not require that money a perfect store value an economy inflation money loses some buying power each year but remains moneythird money serves as a unit account which means that ruler by which we measure values example an accountant may charge 100 file your tax return that 100 can purchase two pair shoes at 50 a pair money acts as a common denominator an accounting method that simplifies thinking about tradeoffsfinally another function money that must serve as a standard deferred payment this means that if money usable today make purchases must also be acceptable make purchases today that purchaser will pay future loans future agreements are stated monetary terms standard deferred payment what allows us buy goods services today pay future thus money serves all these functions a medium exchange store value unit account standard deferred payment

commodity versus fiat money money has taken a wide variety forms different cultures people have used gold silver cowrie shells cigarettes even cocoa beans as money although we use these items as commodity money they also have a value from use as something other than money example people have used gold throughout ages as money although today we do not use as money but rather value its other attributes gold a good conductor electricity electronics aerospace industry use other industries use gold too such as manufacture energy efficient reflective glass skyscrapers used medical industry as well course gold also has value because its beauty malleability creating jewelryas commodity money gold has historically served its purpose as a medium exchange a store value as a unit account commoditybacked currencies are dollar bills or other currencies values backed up by gold or other commodities held at a bank during much its history gold silver backed money supply united states interestingly antique dollars dated as late as 1957 have silver certificate printed over portrait george washington as figure 142 shows this meant that holder could take bill appropriate bank exchange a dollars worth silveras economies grew became more global nature use commodity monies became more cumbersome countries moved towards use fiat money fiat money has no intrinsic value but declared by a government be a countrys legal tender united states paper money example carries statement this note legal tender all debts public private other words by government decree if you owe a debt then legally speaking you can pay that debt us currency even though not backed by a commodity only backing our money universal faith trust that currency has value nothing more

how fast speed macroeconomic adjustment how long does take wages prices adjust economy rebound its potential gdp this subject highly contentious keynesian economists argue that if adjustment from recession potential gdp takes a very long time then neoclassical theory may be more hypothetical than practical response john maynard keynes immortal words long run we are all dead neoclassical economists respond that even if adjustment takes as long as say ten years neoclassical perspective remains central importance understanding economyone subset neoclassical economists holds that wage price adjustment macroeconomy might be quite rapid theory rational expectations holds that people form most accurate possible expectations about future that they can using all information available them an economy where most people have rational expectations economic adjustments may happen very quicklyto understand how rational expectations may affect speed price adjustments think about a situation real estate market imagine that several events seem likely push up home values neighborhood perhaps a local employer announces that plans hire many more people or city announces that will build a local park or a library that neighborhood theory rational expectations points out that even though none changes will happen immediately home prices neighborhood will rise immediately because expectation that homes will be worth more future will lead buyers be willing pay more present amount immediate increase home prices will depend on how likely seems that announcements about future will actually happen on how distant local jobs neighborhood improvements are future key point that because rational expectations prices do not wait on events but adjust immediatelyat a macroeconomic level theory rational expectations points out that if aggregate supply curve vertical over time then people should rationally expect this pattern when a shift aggregate demand occurs people businesses rational expectations will know that its impact on output employment will be temporary while its impact on price level will be permanent if firms workers perceive outcome process advance if all firms workers know that everyone else perceiving process same way then they have no incentive go through an extended series shortrun scenarios like a firm first hiring more people when aggregate demand shifts out then firing those same people when aggregate supply shifts back instead everyone will recognize where this process headingtoward a change price leveland then will act on that expectation this scenario expected longrun change price level may happen very quickly without a drawnout zigzag output employment first moving one way then other theory that people firms have rational expectations can be a useful simplification but as a statement about how people businesses actually behave assumption seems too strong after all many people firms are not especially well informed either about what happening economy or about how economy works an alternate assumption that people firms act adaptive expectations they look at past experience gradually adapt their beliefs behavior as circumstances change but are not perfect synthesizers information accurate predictors future sense rational expectations theory if most people businesses have some form adaptive expectations then adjustment from short run long run will be traced out incremental steps that occur over timethe empirical evidence on speed macroeconomic adjustment prices wages not clearcut speed macroeconomic adjustment probably varies among different countries time periods a reasonable guess that initial shortrun effect a shift aggregate demand might last two five years before adjustments wages prices cause economy adjust back potential gdp thus one might think short run applying keynesian analysis as time periods less than two five years long run applying neoclassical analysis as longer than five years practical purposes this guideline frustratingly imprecise but when analyzing a complex social mechanism like an economy as evolves over time some imprecision seems unavoidable

12-2-the-building-blocks-of-keynesian-an.question: wage price stickinesskeynes also pointed out that although ad fluctuated prices wages did not immediately respond as economists often expected instead prices wages are sticky making difficult restore economy full employment potential gdp keynes emphasized one particular reason why wages were sticky coordination argument this argument points out that even if most people would be willingat least hypotheticallyto see a decline their own wages bad economic times as long as everyone else also experienced such a decline a marketoriented economy has no obvious way implement a plan coordinated wage reductions unemployment proposed a number reasons why wages might be sticky downward most which center on argument that businesses avoid wage cuts because they may one way or another depress morale hurt productivity existing workerssome modern economists have argued a keynesian spirit that along wages other prices may be sticky too many firms do not change their prices every day or even every month when a firm considers changing prices must consider two sets costs first changing prices uses company resources managers must analyze competition market demand decide new prices they must update sales materials change billing records redo product price labels second frequent price changes may leave customers confused or angryespecially if they discover that a product now costs more than they expected these costs changing prices are called menu costslike costs printing a new set menus different prices a restaurant prices do respond forces supply demand but from a macroeconomic perspective process changing all prices throughout economy takes timeto understand effect sticky wages prices economy consider figure 124 a illustrating overall labor market while figure 124 b illustrates a market a specific good or service original equilibrium e0 each market occurs at intersection demand curve d0 supply curve s0 when aggregate demand declines demand labor shifts left d1 figure 124 a demand goods shifts left d1 figure 124 b however because sticky wages prices wage remains at its original level w0 a period time price remains at its original level p0as a result a situation excess supplywhere quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at existing wage or priceexists markets both labor goods q1 less than q0 both figure 124 a figure 124 b when many labor markets many goods markets all across economy find themselves this position economy a recession that firms cannot sell what they wish produce at existing market price do not wish hire all who are willing work at existing market wage clear up feature discusses this problem more detail

12-3-the-phillips-curve.question: discovery phillips curve 1950s aw phillips an economist at london school economics was studying keynesian analytical framework keynesian theory implied that during a recession inflationary pressures are low but when level output at or even pushing beyond potential gdp economy at greater risk inflation phillips analyzed 60 years british data did find that tradeoff between unemployment inflation which became known as phillips curve figure 128 shows a theoretical phillips curve following work out feature shows how pattern appears united states work out phillips curve united states step 1 go this website see 2005 economic report presidentstep 2 scroll down locate table b63 appendices this table titled changes special consumer price indexes 19602004 step 3 download table excel by selecting xls option then selecting location which save file step 4 open downloaded excel file step 5 view third column labeled year year this inflation rate measured by percentage change consumer price index step 6 return website scroll locate appendix table b42 civilian unemployment rate 19592004 step 7 download table excel step 8 open downloaded excel file view second column this overall unemployment rate step 9 using data available from these two tables plot phillips curve 196069 unemployment rate on xaxis inflation rate on yaxis your graph should look like figure 129 step 10 plot phillips curve 19601979 what does graph look like do you still see tradeoff between inflation unemployment your graph should look like figure 1210 over this longer period time phillips curve appears have shifted out there no tradeoff any more

12-3-the-phillips-curve.question: instability phillips curve during 1960s economists viewed phillips curve as a policy menu a nation could choose low inflation high unemployment or high inflation low unemployment or anywhere between economies could use fiscal monetary policy move up or down phillips curve as desired then a curious thing happened when policymakers tried exploit tradeoff between inflation unemployment result was an increase both inflation unemployment what had happened phillips curve shiftedthe us economy experienced this pattern deep recession from 1973 1975 again backtoback recessions from 1980 1982 many nations around world saw similar increases unemployment inflation this pattern became known as stagflation recall from aggregate demandaggregate supply model that stagflation an unhealthy combination high unemployment high inflation perhaps most important stagflation was a phenomenon that traditional keynesian economics could not explaineconomists have concluded that two factors cause phillips curve shift first supply shocks like mid1970s oil crisis which first brought stagflation into our vocabulary second changes peoples expectations about inflation other words there may be a tradeoff between inflation unemployment when people expect no inflation but when they realize inflation occurring tradeoff disappears both factors supply shocks changes inflationary expectations cause aggregate supply curve thus phillips curve shiftin short we should interpret a downwardsloping phillips curve as valid shortrun periods several years but over longer periods when aggregate supply shifts downwardsloping phillips curve can shift so that unemployment inflation are both higher as 1970s early 1980s or both lower as early 1990s or first decade 2000s
12-3-the-phillips-curve.section_name: The Instability of the Phillips Curve

12-3-the-phillips-curve.question: keynesian policy fighting unemployment inflation keynesian macroeconomics argues that solution a recession expansionary fiscal policy such as tax cuts stimulate consumption investment or direct increases government spending that would shift aggregate demand curve right example if aggregate demand was originally at adr figure 1211 so that economy was recession appropriate policy would be government shift aggregate demand right from adr adf where economy would be at potential gdp full employment keynes noted that while would be nice if government could spend additional money on housing roads other amenities he also argued that if government could not agree on how spend money practical ways then could spend impractical ways example keynes suggested building monuments like a modern equivalent egyptian pyramids he proposed that government could bury money underground let mining companies start digging up money again these suggestions were slightly tongueincheek but their purpose was emphasize that a great depression no time quibble over specifics government spending programs tax cuts when goal should be pump up aggregate demand by enough lift economy potential gdpthe other side keynesian policy occurs when economy operating above potential gdp this situation unemployment low but inflationary rises price level are a concern keynesian response would be contractionary fiscal policy using tax increases or government spending cuts shift ad left result would be downward pressure on price level but very little reduction output or very little rise unemployment if aggregate demand was originally at adi figure 1211 so that economy was experiencing inflationary rises price level appropriate policy would be government shift aggregate demand left from adi toward adf which reduces pressure a higher price level while economy remains at full employment keynesian economic model too little aggregate demand brings unemployment too much brings inflation thus you can think keynesian economics as pursuing a goldilocks level aggregate demand not too much not too little but looking what just right

economist named jeanbaptiste say 17671832 says law supply creates its own demand as a matter historical accuracy seems clear that say never actually wrote down this law that oversimplifies his beliefs but law lives on as useful shorthand summarizing a point viewthe intuition behind says law that each time a good or service produced sold generates income that earned someone a worker a manager an owner or those who are workers managers owners at firms that supply inputs along chain production we alluded this earlier our discussion national income approach measuring gdp forces supply demand individual markets will cause prices rise fall bottom line remains however that every sale represents income someone so says law argues a given value supply must create an equivalent value demand somewhere else economy because jeanbaptiste say adam smith other economists writing around turn nineteenth century who discussed this view were known as classical economists modern economists who generally subscribe says law view on importance supply determining size macroeconomy are called neoclassical economistsif supply always creates exactly enough demand at macroeconomic level then as say himself recognized hard understand why periods recession high unemployment should ever occur be sure even if total supply always creates an equal amount total demand economy could still experience a situation some firms earning profits while other firms suffer losses nevertheless a recession not a situation where all business failures are exactly counterbalanced by an offsetting number successes a recession a situation which economy as a whole shrinking size business failures outnumber remaining success stories many firms end up suffering losses laying off workerssays law that supply creates its own demand does seem a good approximation long run over periods some years or decades as productive power an economy supply goods services increases total demand economy grows at roughly same pace however over shorter time horizons a few months or even years recessions or even depressions occur which firms as a group seem face a lack demand their products

11-1-macroeconomic-perspectives-on-deman.question: keynes law macroeconomics demand alternative says law its emphasis on supply keynes law demand creates its own supply as a matter historical accuracy just as jeanbaptiste say never wrote down anything as simpleminded as says law john maynard keynes never wrote down keynes law but law a useful simplification that conveys a certain point viewwhen keynes wrote his influential work general theory employment interest money during 1930s great depression he pointed out that during depression economys capacity supply goods services had not changed much us unemployment rates soared higher than 20 from 1933 1935 but number possible workers had not increased or decreased much factories closed but machinery equipment had not disappeared technologies that had been invented 1920s were not uninvented forgotten 1930s thus keynes argued that great depressionand many ordinary recessions as wellwere not caused by a drop ability economy supply goods as measured by labor physical capital or technology he argued economy often produced less than its full potential not because was technically impossible produce more existing workers machines but because a lack demand economy as a whole led inadequate incentives firms produce such cases he argued level gdp economy was not primarily determined by potential what economy could supply but rather by amount total demandkeynes law seems apply fairly well short run a few months a few years when many firms experience either a drop demand their output during a recession or so much demand that they have trouble producing enough during an economic boom however demand cannot tell whole macroeconomic story either after all if demand was all that mattered at macroeconomic level then government could make economy as large as wanted just by pumping up total demand through a large increase government spending component or by legislating large tax cuts push up consumption component economies do however face genuine limits how much they can produce limits determined by quantity labor physical capital technology institutional market structures that bring these factors production together these constraints on what an economy can supply at macroeconomic level do not disappear just because an increase demand

purposes reduce borrowing by households homes carsthus reducing consumption investment spending foreign price effect points out that if prices rise united states while remaining fixed other countries account balance includes trade goods services money flowing into out a country from investments unilateral transfers 102 trade balances historical international context united states developed large trade surpluses early 1980s swung back a tiny trade surplus 1991 then had even larger trade deficits late 1990s early 2000s as we will see below a trade deficit necessarily means a net inflow financial capital from abroad while a trade surplus necessarily means a net outflow financial capital from an economy other countries 103 trade balances flows financial capital international flows goods services are closely connected international flows financial capital a current account deficit means that after taking all flows payments from goods services income together country a net borrower from rest world a current account surplus opposite means country a net lender rest world 104 national saving investment identity national saving investment identity based on relationship that total quantity financial capital supplied from all sources must equal total quantity financial capital demanded from all sources if s private saving t taxes g government spending m imports x exports i investment then an economy a current account deficit a budget deficit supply financial capital demand financial capitals m xi g t supply financial capital demand financial capitals m xi g t a recession tends increase trade balance meaning a higher trade surplus or lower trade deficit while economic boom will tend decrease trade balance meaning a lower trade surplus or a larger trade deficit 105 pros cons trade deficits surpluses trade surpluses are no guarantee economic health trade deficits are no guarantee economic weakness either trade deficits or trade surpluses can work out well or poorly depending on whether a government wisely invests corresponding flows financial capital 106 difference between level trade trade balance there a difference between level a countrys trade balance trade government measures its level trade by percentage exports out gdp or size economy small economies that have nearby trading partners a history international trade will tend have higher levels trade larger economies few nearby trading partners a limited history international trade will tend have lower levels trade level trade different from trade balance level trade depends on a countrys history trade its geography size its economy a countrys balance trade dollar difference between its exports importstrade deficits trade surpluses are not necessarily good or badit depends on circumstances even if a country borrowing if invests that money productivityboosting investments can lead an improvement longterm economic growth

final thoughts about trade balances trade deficits can be a good or a bad sign an economy trade surpluses can be a good or a bad sign even a trade balance zerowhich just means that a nation neither a net borrower nor lender international economycan be either a good or bad sign fundamental economic question not whether a nations economy borrowing or lending at all but whether particular borrowing or lending particular economic conditions that country makes sense interesting reflect on how public attitudes toward trade deficits surpluses might change if we could somehow change labels that people news media affix them if we called a trade deficit attracting foreign financial capitalwhich accurately describes what a trade deficit meansthen trade deficits might look more attractive conversely if we called a trade surplus shipping financial capital abroadwhich accurately captures what a trade surplus doesthen trade surpluses might look less attractive either way key understanding trade balances understand relationships between flows trade flows international payments what these relationships imply about causes benefits risks different kinds trade balances first step along this journey understanding move beyond kneejerk reactions terms like trade surplus trade balance trade deficit

10-4-the-national-saving-and-investment-.answer: this question there are two main sources supply financial capital us economy saving by individuals firms called s inflow financial capital from foreign investors which equal trade deficit m x or imports minus exports there are also two main sources demand financial capital us economy private sector investment i government borrowing where government needs borrow when government spending g higher than taxes collected t we can express this national savings investment identity algebraic termssupply financial capital demand financial capitals m x i g tsupply financial capital demand financial capitals m x i g t again this equation s private savings t taxes g government spending m imports x exports i investment this relationship true as a matter definition because macro economy quantity supplied financial capital must be equal quantity demanded however certain components national savings investment identity can switch between supply side demand side some countries like united states most years since 1970s have budget deficits which mean government spending more than collects taxes so government needs borrow funds this case government term would be g t 0 showing that spending larger than taxes government would be a demander financial capital on righthand side equation that a borrower not a supplier financial capital on lefthand side however if government runs a budget surplus so that taxes exceed spending as us government did from 1998 2001 then government that year was contributing supply financial capital t g 0 would appear on left supplier or saving side national savings investment identitysimilarly if a national economy runs a trade surplus trade sector will involve an outflow financial capital other countries a trade surplus means that domestic financial capital surplus within a country can be invested other countries fundamental notion that total quantity financial capital demanded equals total quantity financial capital supplied must always remain true domestic savings will always appear as part supply financial capital domestic investment will always appear as part demand financial capital however government trade balance elements equation can move back forth as either suppliers or demanders financial capital depending on whether government budgets trade balance are surplus or deficit

10-4-the-national-saving-and-investment-.question: shortterm movements business cycle trade balance short run whether an economy a recession or on upswing can affect trade imbalances a recession tends make a trade deficit smaller or a trade surplus larger while a period strong economic growth tends make a trade deficit larger or a trade surplus smaller these are not hardandfast rules howeveras an example note figure 102 that us trade deficit declined by almost half from 2006 2009 one primary reason this change that during recession as us economy slowed down purchased fewer all goods including fewer imports from abroad however buying power abroad fell less so us exports did not fall by as much on other hand during 2020 pandemicinduced recession trade deficit expanded current account balance plummeted as us economy became more reliant on other countries goods services conversely mid2000s when us trade deficit became very large a contributing shortterm reason that us economy was growing as a result there was considerable aggressive buying us economy including buying imports thus a trade deficit or a much lower trade surplus often accompanies a rapidly growing domestic economy while a trade surplus or a much lower trade deficit accompanies a slowing or recessionary domestic economyregardless whether trade deficit falls or rises during expansions or downturns when trade deficit rises necessarily means a greater net inflow foreign financial capital national saving investment identity teaches that rest economy can absorb this inflow foreign financial capital several different ways example reduced private savings could offset additional inflow financial capital from abroad leaving domestic investment public saving unchanged alternatively inflow foreign financial capital could result higher domestic investment leaving private public saving unchanged yet another possibility that greater government borrowing could absorb inflow foreign financial capital leaving domestic saving investment unchanged national saving investment identity does not specify which these scenarios alone or combination will occuronly that one them must occur

9-4-the-confusion-over-inflation.question: land funny money what are economic problems caused by inflation why do economists often regard them less concern than general public consider a very short story land funny money one morning everyone land funny money awakened find that everything denominated money had increased by 20 change was completely unexpected every price every store was 20 higher paychecks were 20 higher interest rates were 20 higher amount money everywhere from wallets savings accounts was 20 larger this overnight inflation prices made newspaper headlines everywhere land funny money however headlines quickly disappeared as people realized that terms what they could actually buy their incomes this inflation had no economic impact everyones pay could still buy exactly same set goods as did before everyones savings were still sufficient buy exactly same car vacation or retirement that they could have bought before equal levels inflation all wages prices ended up not mattering much at allwhen people robert shillers surveys explained their concern about inflation one typical reason was that they feared that as prices rose they would not be able afford buy as much other words people were worried because they did not live a place like land funny money where all prices wages rose simultaneously instead people live here on planet earth where prices might rise while wages do not rise at all or where wages rise more slowly than prices economists note that over most periods inflation level prices roughly similar inflation level wages so they reason that on average over time peoples economic status not greatly changed by inflation if all prices wages interest rates adjusted automatically immediately inflation as land funny money then no ones purchasing power profits or real loan payments would change however if other economic variables do not move exactly sync inflation or if they adjust inflation only after a time lag then inflation can cause three types problems unintended redistributions purchasing power blurred price signals difficulties longterm planning

unintended redistributions purchasing power inflation can cause redistributions purchasing power that hurt some help others people who are hurt by inflation include those who are holding considerable cash whether a safe deposit box or a cardboard box under bed when inflation happens buying power cash diminishes however cash only an example a more general problem anyone who has financial assets invested a way that nominal return does not keep up inflation will tend be affected by inflation example if a person has money a bank account that pays 4 interest but inflation rises 5 then real rate return money invested that bank account negative 1the problem a goodlooking nominal interest rate transforming into an uglylooking real interest rate can be worsened by taxes us income tax charged on nominal interest received dollar terms without an adjustment inflation thus government taxes a person who invests 10000 receives a 5 nominal rate interest on 500 receivedno matter whether inflation rate 0 5 or 10 if inflation 0 then real interest rate 5 all 500 a gain buying power however if inflation 5 then real interest rate zero person had no real gainbut owes income tax on nominal gain anyway if inflation 10 then real interest rate negative 5 person actually falling behind buying power but would still owe taxes on 500 nominal gainsinflation can cause unintended redistributions wage earners too wages do typically creep up inflation over time eventually last row table 91 at start this chapter showed that average hourly wage manufacturing us economy increased from 323 1970 3011 2021 which an increase by a factor close ten over that time period consumer price index increased by about a factor eight however increases wages may lag behind inflation a year or two since wage adjustments are often somewhat sticky occur only once or twice a year moreover extent which wages keep up inflation creates insecurity workers may involve painful prolonged conflicts between employers employees if government adjusts minimum wage inflation only infrequently minimum wage workers are losing purchasing power from their nominal wages as figure 96 showsone sizable group people has often received a large share their income a form that does not increase over time retirees who receive a private company pension most pensions have traditionally been set as a fixed nominal dollar amount per year at retirement this reason economists call pensions defined benefits plans even if inflation low combination inflation a fixed income can create a substantial problem over time a person who retires on a fixed income at age 65 will find that losing just 1 2 buying power per year inflation compounds a considerable loss buying power after a decade or twofortunately pensions other defined benefits retirement plans are increasingly rare replaced instead by defined contribution plans such as 401ks 403bs these plans employer contributes a fixed amount workers retirement account on a regular basis usually every pay check employee often contributes as well worker invests these funds a wide range investment vehicles these plans are tax deferred they are portable so that if individual takes a job a different employer their 401k comes them extent that investments made generate real rates return retirees are not burdened by inflation costs traditional pensionershowever ordinary people can sometimes benefit from unintended redistributions inflation consider someone who borrows 10000 buy a car at a fixed interest rate 9 if inflation 3 at time loan made then borrower must repay loan at a real interest rate 6 however if inflation rises 9 then real interest rate on loan zero this case borrowers benefit from inflation lenders loss a borrower paying a fixed interest rate who benefits from inflation just flip side an investor receiving a fixed interest rate who suffers from inflation lesson that when interest rates are fixed rises rate inflation tend penalize suppliers financial capital who receive repayment dollars that are worth less because inflation while demanders financial capital end up better off because they can repay their loans dollars that are worth less than originally expectedthe unintended redistributions buying power that inflation causes may have a broader effect on society americas widespread acceptance market forces rests on a perception that peoples actions have a reasonable connection market outcomes when inflation causes a retiree who built up a pension or invested at a fixed interest rate suffer however while someone who borrowed at a fixed interest rate benefits from inflation hard believe that this outcome was deserved any way similarly when homeowners benefit from inflation because price their homes rises while renters suffer because they are paying higher rent hard see any useful incentive effects one reasons that general public dislikes inflation a sense that makes economic rewards penalties more arbitraryand therefore likely be perceived as unfair even dangerous as next clear up feature shows
