problems longterm planning inflation can make longterm planning difficult discussing unintended redistributions we considered case someone trying plan retirement a pension that fixed nominal terms a high rate inflation similar problems arise all people trying save retirement because they must consider what their money will really buy several decades future when we cannot know rate future inflationinflation especially at moderate or high levels will pose substantial planning problems businesses too a firm can make money from inflationfor example by paying bills wages as late as possible so that can pay inflated dollars while collecting revenues as soon as possible a firm can also suffer losses from inflation as case a retail business that gets stuck holding too much cash only see inflation eroding value that cash however when a business spends its time focusing on how profit by inflation or at least how avoid suffering from an inevitable tradeoff strikes less time spent on improving products services or on figuring out how make existing products services more cheaply an economy high inflation rewards businesses that have found clever ways profiting from inflation which are not necessarily businesses that excel at productivity innovation or quality servicein short term low or moderate levels inflation may not pose an overwhelming difficulty business planning because costs doing business sales revenues may rise at similar rates if however inflation varies substantially over short or medium term then may make sense businesses stick shorterterm strategies evidence as whether relatively low rates inflation reduce productivity controversial among economists there some evidence that if inflation can be held moderate levels less than 3 per year need not prevent a nations real economy from growing at a healthy pace some countries that have experienced hyperinflation several thousand percent per year an annual inflation rate 2030 may feel basically same as zero however several economists have pointed suggestive fact that when us inflation heated up early 1970sto 10us growth productivity slowed down when inflation slowed down 1980s productivity edged up again not long thereafter as figure 97 shows

any benefits inflation although economic effects inflation are primarily negative two countervailing points are worth noting first impact inflation will differ considerably according whether creeping up slowly at 0 2 per year galloping along at 10 20 per year or racing point hyperinflation at say 40 per month hyperinflation can rip an economy a society apart an annual inflation rate 2 3 or 4 however a long way from a national crisis low inflation also better than deflation which occurs severe recessions second economists sometimes argue that moderate inflation may help economy by making wages labor markets more flexible discussion unemployment pointed out that wages tend be sticky their downward movements that unemployment can result a little inflation could nibble away at real wages thus help real wages decline if necessary this way even if a moderate or high rate inflation may act as sand gears economy perhaps a low rate inflation serves as oil gears labor market this argument controversial a full analysis would have account all effects inflation does however offer another reason believe that all things considered very low rates inflation may not be especially harmful

30-key-concepts-and-summary.question: 301 government spending fiscal policy set policies that relate federal government spending taxation borrowing recent decades level federal government spending taxes expressed as a share gdp has not changed much typically fluctuating between about 18 22 gdp however level state spending taxes as a share gdp has risen from about 1213 about 20 gdp over last four decades four main areas federal spending are national defense social security healthcare interest payments which together account about 70 all federal spending when a government spends more than collects taxes said have a budget deficit when a government collects more taxes than spends said have a budget surplus if government spending taxes are equal said have a balanced budget sum all past deficits surpluses make up government debt 302 taxation two main federal taxes are individual income taxes payroll taxes that provide funds social security medicare these taxes together account more than 80 federal revenues other federal taxes include corporate income tax excise taxes on alcohol gasoline tobacco estate gift tax a progressive tax one like federal income tax where those higher incomes pay a higher share taxes out their income than those lower incomes a proportional tax one like payroll tax medicare where everyone pays same share taxes regardless income level a regressive tax one like payroll tax above a certain threshold that supports social security where those high income pay a lower share income taxes than those lower incomes 303 federal deficits national debt most twentieth century us government took on debt during wartime then paid down that debt slowly peacetime however took on quite substantial debts peacetime 1980s early 1990s before a brief period budget surpluses from 1998 2001 followed by a return annual budget deficits since 2002 very large deficits recession 2008 2009 a budget deficit or budget surplus measured annually total government debt or national debt sum budget deficits budget surpluses over time 304 using fiscal policy fight recession unemployment inflation expansionary fiscal policy increases level aggregate demand either through increases government spending or through reductions taxes expansionary fiscal policy most appropriate when an economy recession producing below its potential gdp contractionary fiscal policy decreases level aggregate demand either through cuts government spending or increases taxes contractionary fiscal policy most appropriate when an economy producing above its potential gdp 305 automatic stabilizers fiscal policy conducted both through discretionary fiscal policy which occurs when government enacts taxation or spending changes response economic events or through automatic stabilizers which are taxing spending mechanisms that by their design shift response economic events without any further legislation standardized employment budget calculation what budget deficit or budget surplus would have been a given year if economy had been producing at its potential gdp that year many economists politicians criticize use fiscal policy a variety reasons including concerns over time lags impact on interest rates inherently political nature fiscal policy we cover critique fiscal policy next module 306 practical problems discretionary fiscal policy because fiscal policy affects quantity money that government borrows financial capital markets not only affects aggregate demandit can also affect interest rates if an expansionary fiscal policy also causes higher interest rates then firms households are discouraged from borrowing spending reducing aggregate demand a situation called crowding out given uncertainties over interest rate effects time lags implementation lag legislative lag recognition lag temporary permanent policies unpredictable political behavior many economists knowledgeable policymakers have concluded that discretionary fiscal policy a blunt instrument better used only extreme situations 307 question a balanced budget balanced budget amendments are a popular political idea but economic merits behind such proposals are questionable most economists accept that fiscal policy needs be flexible enough accommodate unforeseen expenditures such as wars or recessions while persistent large budget deficits can indeed be a problem a balanced budget amendment prevents even small temporary deficits that might some cases be necessary

temporary permanent fiscal policy a temporary tax cut or spending increase will explicitly last only a year or two then revert its original level a permanent tax cut or spending increase expected stay place foreseeable future effect temporary permanent fiscal policies on aggregate demand can be very different consider how you would react if government announced a tax cut that would last one year then be repealed comparison how you would react if government announced a permanent tax cut most people firms will react more strongly a permanent policy change than a temporary onethis fact creates an unavoidable difficulty countercyclical fiscal policy appropriate policy may be have an expansionary fiscal policy large budget deficits during a recession then a contractionary fiscal policy budget surpluses when economy growing well however if both policies are explicitly temporary ones they will have a less powerful effect than a permanent policy

structural economic change takes time when an economy recovers from a recession does not usually revert its exact earlier shape instead economys internal structure evolves changes this process can take time example much economic growth mid2000s was construction sector especially housing finance however when housing prices started falling 2007 resulting financial crunch led into recession as we discussed monetary policy bank regulation both sectors contracted manufacturing sector us economy has been losing jobs recent years as well under pressure from technological change foreign competition many people who lost work from these sectors 20082009 great recession will never return same jobs same sectors economy instead economy will need grow new different directions as following clear up feature shows fiscal policy can increase overall demand but process structural economic changethe expansion a new set industries movement workers those industriesinevitably takes time

limitations fiscal policy fiscal policy can help an economy that producing below its potential gdp expand aggregate demand so that produces closer potential gdp thus lowering unemployment however fiscal policy cannot help an economy produce at an output level above potential gdp without causing inflation at this point unemployment becomes so low that workers become scarce wages rise rapidly

political realties discretionary fiscal policy a final problem discretionary fiscal policy arises out difficulties explaining politicians how countercyclical fiscal policy that runs against tide business cycle should work some politicians have a gutlevel belief that when economy tax revenues slow down time hunker down pinch pennies trim expenses countercyclical policy however says that when economy has slowed time government stimulate economy raising spending cutting taxes this offsets drop economy other sectors conversely when economic times are good tax revenues are rolling politicians often feel that time tax cuts new spending however countercyclical policy says that this economic boom should be an appropriate time keeping taxes high restraining spendingpoliticians tend prefer expansionary fiscal policy over contractionary policy there rarely a shortage proposals tax cuts spending increases especially during recessions however politicians are less willing hear message that good economic times they should propose tax increases spending limits economic upswing late 1990s early 2000s example us gdp grew rapidly estimates from respected government economic forecasters like nonpartisan congressional budget office office management budget stated that gdp was above potential gdp that unemployment rates were unsustainably low however no mainstream politician took lead saying that booming economic times might be an appropriate time spending cuts or tax increases

billion mortgagebacked securities from government enterprises fannie mae freddie mac november 2010 fed began qe2 which purchased 600 billion us treasury bonds qe3 began september 2012 when fed commenced purchasing 40 billion additional mortgagebacked securities per month this amount was increased december 2012 85 billion per month fed stated that when economic conditions permit will begin tapering or reducing monthly purchases by october 2014 fed had announced final 15 billion bond purchase ending quantitative easing we usually think quantitative easing policies that federal reserve adopted as did other central banks around world as temporary emergency measures if these steps are be temporary then federal reserve will need stop making these additional loans sell off financial securities has accumulated concern that process quantitative easing may prove more difficult reverse than was enact evidence suggests that qe1 was somewhat successful but that qe2 qe3 have been less sofast forward march 2020 when fed under leadership jerome powell promised another round asset purchases which was dubbed by some as qe4 intended once again provide liquidity a distressed financial expected increase through 2022 but as end 2021 total fed assets exceed 8 trillion compared 4 trillion february 2020

27-1-defining-money-by-its-functions.question: functions money money solves problems that barter system creates we will get its definition soon first money serves as a medium exchange which means that money acts as an intermediary between buyer seller instead exchanging accounting services shoes accountant now exchanges accounting services money accountant then uses this money buy shoes serve as a medium exchange people must widely accept money as a method payment markets goods labor financial capitalsecond money must serve as a store value a barter system we saw example shoemaker trading shoes accounting services however she risks having her shoes go out style especially if she keeps them a warehouse future usetheir value will decrease each season shoes are not a good store value holding money a much easier way storing value you know that you do not need spend immediately because will still hold its value next day or next year this function money does not require that money a perfect store value an economy inflation money loses some buying power each year but remains moneythird money serves as a unit account which means that ruler by which we measure values example an accountant may charge 100 file your tax return that 100 can purchase two pair shoes at 50 a pair money acts as a common denominator an accounting method that simplifies thinking about tradeoffsfinally another function money that must serve as a standard deferred payment this means that if money usable today make purchases must also be acceptable make purchases today that purchaser will pay future loans future agreements are stated monetary terms standard deferred payment what allows us buy goods services today pay future thus money serves all these functions a medium exchange store value unit account standard deferred payment

26-key-concepts-and-summary.question: 261 building blocks neoclassical analysis neoclassical perspective argues that long run economy will adjust back its potential gdp level output through flexible price levels thus neoclassical perspective views longrun as curve as vertical a rational expectations perspective argues that people have excellent information about economic events how economy works that as a result price other economic adjustments will happen very quickly adaptive expectations theory people have limited information about economic information how economy works so price other economic adjustments can be slow 262 policy implications neoclassical perspective neoclassical economists tend put relatively more emphasis on longterm growth than on fighting recession because they believe that recessions will fade a few years longterm growth will ultimately determine standard living they tend focus more on reducing natural rate unemployment caused by economic institutions government policies than cyclical unemployment caused by recession neoclassical economists also see no social benefit inflation an upwardsloping keynesian as curve inflation can arise because an economy approaching full employment a vertical longrun neoclassical as curve inflation does not accompany any rise output if aggregate supply vertical then aggregate demand does not affect quantity output instead aggregate demand can only cause inflationary changes price level a vertical aggregate supply curve where quantity output consistent many different price levels also implies a vertical phillips curve 263 balancing keynesian neoclassical models keynesian perspective considers changes aggregate demand be cause business cycle fluctuations keynesians are likely advocate that policy makers actively attempt reverse recessionary inflationary periods because they are not convinced that selfcorrecting economy can easily return full employment neoclassical perspective places more emphasis on aggregate supply neoclassical economists believe that long term productivity growth determines potential gdp level that economy typically will return full employment after a change aggregate demand skeptical effectiveness timeliness keynesian policy neoclassical economists are more likely advocate a handsoff or fairly limited role active stabilization policywhile keynesians would tend advocate an acceptable tradeoff between inflation unemployment when counteracting a recession neoclassical economists argue that no such tradeoff exists any shortterm gains lower unemployment will eventually vanish result active policy will only be inflation

fighting unemployment or inflationas we explained unemployment economists divide unemployment into two categories cyclical unemployment natural rate unemployment which sum frictional structural unemployment cyclical unemployment results from fluctuations business cycle created when economy producing below potential gdpgiving potential employers less incentive hire when economy producing at potential gdp cyclical unemployment will be zero because labor market dynamics which people are always entering or exiting labor force unemployment rate never falls 0 not even when economy producing at or even slightly above potential gdp probably best we can hope number job vacancies equal number job seekers we know that takes time job seekers employers find each other this time cause frictional unemployment most economists do not consider frictional unemployment be a bad thing after all there will always be workers who are unemployed while looking a job that a better match their skills there will always be employers that have an open position while looking a worker that a better match job ideally these matches happen quickly but even when economy very strong there will be some natural unemployment this what natural rate unemployment measuresthe neoclassical view unemployment tends focus attention away from cyclical unemployment problemthat unemployment caused by recessionwhile putting more attention on unemployment rate issue that prevails even when economy operating at potential gdp put another way neoclassical view unemployment tends focus on how government can adjust public policy reduce natural rate unemployment such policy changes might involve redesigning unemployment welfare programs so that they support those need but also offer greater encouragement jobhunting might involve redesigning business rules an eye whether they are unintentionally discouraging businesses from taking on new employees might involve building institutions improve flow information about jobs mobility workers help bring workers employers together more quickly those workers who find that their skills are permanently no longer demand example structurally unemployed economists can design policy provide opportunities retraining so that these workers can reenter labor force seek employmentneoclassical economists will not tend see aggregate demand as a useful tool reducing unemployment after all a vertical aggregate supply curve determining economic output then aggregate demand has no longrun effect on unemployment instead neoclassical economists believe that aggregate demand should be allowed expand only match gradual shifts aggregate supply rightkeeping price level much same inflationary pressures lowif aggregate demand rises rapidly neoclassical model long run leads only inflationary pressures figure 268 shows a vertical lras curve three different levels aggregate demand rising from ad0 ad1 ad2 as macroeconomic equilibrium rises from e0 e1 e2 price level rises but real gdp does not budge nor does rate unemployment which adjusts its natural rate conversely reducing inflation has no longterm costs either think about figure 268 reverse as aggregate demand curve shifts from ad2 ad1 ad0 equilibrium moves from e2 e1 e0 during this process price level falls but long run neither real gdp nor natural unemployment rate changes

fighting recession or encouraging longterm growth neoclassical economists believe that economy will rebound out a recession or eventually contract during an expansion because prices wage rates are flexible will adjust either upward or downward restore economy its potential gdp thus key policy question neoclassicals how promote growth potential gdp we know that economic growth ultimately depends on growth rate longterm productivity productivity measures how effective inputs are at producing outputs we know that us productivity has grown on average about 2 per year that means that same amount inputs produce 2 more output than year before we also know that productivity growth varies a great deal short term due cyclical factors also varies somewhat long term from 19531972 us labor productivity as measured by output per hour business sector grew at 32 per year from 19731992 productivity growth declined significantly 18 per year then from 19932010 productivity growth increased around 2 per year recent years has grown less than 2 per year although did pick up 2019 2020 over 2 again neoclassical economists believe underpinnings longrun productivity growth be an economys investments human capital physical capital technology operating together a marketoriented environment that rewards innovation government policy should focus on promoting these factors

increase homes value but other property values neighborhood may increase as wellthe appropriate public policy response a positive externality like a new technology help party creating positive price flu shots if market were produce qsocial would be at psocial unfortunately marketplace does not recognize positive externality flu shots will go underproduced underconsumedhow can government try move market level output closer socially desirable level output one policy would be provide a subsidy like a voucher any citizen who wishes get vaccinated this voucher would act as income that one could use purchase only a flu shot if voucher were exactly equal perunit spillover benefits would increase market equilibrium a quantity qsocial a price psocial where msb equals msc which equals mpc given assumption that there are no spillover costs producing vaccine suppliers flu shots would receive payment psocial per vaccination while consumers flu shots would redeem voucher only pay a price psubsidy when government uses a subsidy this way produces socially optimal quantity vaccinations societal change as an innovation outcome economist carlota perez draws on lessons past innovations understand current state our economy she demonstrates that prior technological turning points such as proliferation railroads emergence mass production created initial periods employment wealth shifting but eventually led greater wellbeing economic growth after difficult transition periods sometimes economic meltdowns during installment phase widespread new technologies many economies people within them have benefited from prolonged periods economic lifestyle improvement including lower unemployment better quality life most prior innovation periods such as industrial revolution had one significant downside negative impacts on environment such as pollution habitat destruction perez notes that our current revolutionin information communications technology icthas potential significant product rentalreuse networks new manufacturing methods offer promise far less consumable consumption even though appearance delivery trucks shipping boxes gives impression environmental damage most studies indicate that online shopping better environment than individuals shopping person this partly attributed greater efficiency a few trucks driving a neighborhood rather than everyone neighborhood driving several stores consumers governments can spur on those environmental benefits by choosing or partnering companies that focus on furthering their environmental impact such as by using solar power fuel their computer servers or by using electrically powered delivery trucks like other innovations ict has created some employment economic opportunities while has reduced others increased globalization efficiencies have shuttered businesses reduced wages some areas perezs research indicates that those types employment shifts can be managed through proper regulation investment especially human capital particularly as firms relevant industries become mature profitable prospects arent simple ict has created megafirms like amazon apple which despite pleasing their consumers industries that rely on clean air water such as farming fishing tourism may benefit 3 property values may be higher 4 people may simply enjoy a cleaner environment a way that does not need involve a market transaction some these benefits such as gains tourism or farming are relatively easy value economic terms harder assign a monetary value others such as value clean air someone asthma seems difficult put a clearcut monetary value on still others such as satisfaction you might feel from knowing that air clear over grand canyon even if you have never visited grand canyon but advanced techniques economics allow one generate estimatesalthough estimates environmental benefits are not precise they can still be revealing example a study by environmental protection agency looked at costs benefits clean air act from 1970 1990 found that total costs over that time period were roughly 500 billiona huge amount however also found that a middlerange estimate health other benefits from cleaner air was 22 trillionabout 44 times higher than costs a more recent epa study estimated that environmental benefits americans from clean air act will exceed their costs by a margin four one epa estimated that 2010 benefits clean air act programs will total about 110 billion this estimate represents value avoiding increases illness premature death which would have prevailed saying that overall benefits environmental regulation have exceeded costs past however very different from saying that every environmental regulation makes sense example studies suggest that when breaking down emission reductions by type contaminants benefits air pollution control outweigh costs primarily particulates lead but when looking at other air pollutants costs reducing them may be comparable or greater than benefits just because some environmental regulations have had benefits much higher than costs does not prove that every individual regulation a sensible idea

betterdefined property rights a clarified strengthened idea property rights can also strike a balance between economic activity pollution ronald coase 19102013 who won 1991 nobel prize economics offered a vivid illustration an externality a railroad track running beside a farmers field where railroad locomotive sometimes emits sparks sets field ablaze coase asked whose responsibility was address this spillover should farmer be required build a tall fence alongside field block sparks or should railroad be required place a gadget on locomotives smokestack reduce number sparkscoase pointed out that one cannot resolve this issue until one clearly defines property rightsthat legal rights ownership on which others are not allowed infringe without paying compensation does farmer have a property right not have a field burned does railroad have a property right run its own trains on its own tracks if neither party has a property right then two sides may squabble endlessly doing nothing sparks will continue set field aflame however if either farmer or railroad has a welldefined legal responsibility then that party will seek out pay least costly method reducing risk that sparks will hit field property right determines whether farmer or railroad pays billsthe property rights approach highly relevant cases involving endangered species us governments endangered species list includes about 1000 plants animals about 90 these species live on privately owned land protection these endangered species requires careful thinking about incentives property rights discovery an endangered species on private land has often triggered an automatic reaction from government prohibit landowner from using that land any purpose that might disturb imperiled creatures consider incentives that policy if you admit government that you have an endangered species government effectively prohibits you from using your land as a result rumors abounded landowners who followed a policy shoot shovel shut up when they found an endangered animal on their land other landowners have deliberately cut trees or managed land a way that they knew would discourage endangered animals from locating there a more productive policy would consider how provide private landowners an incentive protect endangered species that they find provide a habitat additional endangered species example government might pay landowners who provide maintain suitable habitats endangered species or who restrict use their land protect an endangered species again an environmental law built on incentives flexibility offers greater promise than a commandandcontrol approach when trying oversee millions acres privately owned land

12-1-the-economics-of-pollution.question: externalities private markets such as cell phone industry offer an efficient way put buyers sellers together determine what goods they produce how they produce them who gets them principle that voluntary exchange benefits both buyers sellers a fundamental building block economic way thinking however what happens when a voluntary exchange affects a third party who neither buyer nor selleras an example consider a concert producer who wants build an outdoor arena that will host country music concerts a halfmile from your neighborhood you will be able hear these outdoor concerts while sitting on your back porchor perhaps even your dining room this case sellers buyers concert tickets may both be quite satisfied their voluntary exchange but you have no voice their market transaction effect a market exchange on a third party who outside or external exchange called an externality because externalities that occur market transactions affect other parties beyond those involved they are sometimes called spilloversexternalities can be negative or positive if you hate country music then having waft into your house every night would be a negative externality if you love country music then what amounts a series free concerts would be a positive externality

11-2-regulating-anticompetitive-behavior.question: restrictive practices antitrust law includes rules against restrictive practicespractices that do not involve outright agreements raise price or reduce quantity produced but that might have effect reducing competition antitrust cases involving restrictive practices are often controversial because they delve into specific contracts or agreements between firms that are allowed some cases but not others example an exclusive dealing agreement between a manufacturer a dealer can be legal or illegal legal if purpose contract encourage competition between dealers example legal ford motor company sell its cars only ford dealers general motors sell only gm dealers so on however exclusive deals may also limit competition if one large retailer obtained exclusive rights be sole distributor televisions computers audio equipment made by a number companies then this exclusive contract would have an anticompetitive effect on other retailerstying sales happen when a customer allowed buy one product only if customer also buys a second product tying sales are controversial because they force consumers purchase a product that they may not actually want or need further additional required products are not necessarily advantageous customer suppose that purchase a popular dvd store required that you also purchase a certain portable tv model these products are only loosely related thus there no reason make purchase one contingent on other even if a customer were interested a portable tv tying a particular model prevents customer from having option selecting one from numerous types available market a related but not identical concept bundling where a firm sells two or more products as one bundling typically offers an advantage consumers by allowing them acquire multiple products or services a better price example several cable companies allow customers buy products like cable internet a phone line through a special price available through bundling customers are also welcome purchase these products separately but price bundling usually more appealing some cases we can view tying sales bundling as anticompetitive however other cases they may be legal even common common people purchase season tickets a sports team or a set concerts so as guarantee tickets few contests or shows that are most popular likely sell out computer software manufacturers may often bundle a number different programs even when buyer wants only a few think about software that included a new computer purchase examplerecall from chapter on monopoly that predatory pricing occurs when existing firm or firms reacts a new firm by dropping prices very low until new firm driven out market at which point existing firm raises prices again this pattern pricing aimed at deterring new firms from entering market however practice can be hard figure out when pricing predatory say that american airlines flying between two cities a new airline starts flying between same two cities at a lower price if american airlines cuts its price match new entrant this predatory pricing or just market competition at work a commonly proposed rule that if a firm selling less than its average variable costthat at a price where should be shutting downthen there evidence predatory pricing however calculating real world what costs are variable what costs are fixed often not obvious eitherthe microsoft antitrust case embodies many these gray areas restrictive practices as next clear up shows concept restrictive practices continually evolving as firms seek new ways earn profits government regulators define what permissible a situation where law evolving changing always somewhat troublesome since laws are most useful fair when firms know what they are advance addition since law open interpretation competitors who are losing out market can accuse successful firms anticompetitive restrictive practices try win through government regulation what they have failed accomplish market officials at federal trade commission department justice are course aware these issues but there no easy way resolve them

acquisition where at least one firms above a minimum size sales a threshold that moves up gradually over time was at 101 million 2022 or certain other conditions are met they are required under law notify us federal trade commission ftc lefthand panel figure 112 a shows number mergers submitted review ftc each year from 1999 2012 mergers follow business cycle falling after 2001 recession peaking 2007 as great recession struck then rising since 2009 righthand panel figure 112 b shows distribution those mergers submitted review 2015 as measured by size transaction important remember that this total leaves out many small mergers under 50 million which companies only need report certain limited circumstances 2012 26 percent all reported merger acquisition transactions exceeded 500 million while 11 percent exceeded 1 billionthe laws that give government power block certain mergers even some cases break up large firms into smaller ones are called antitrust laws before a large merger happens antitrust regulators at ftc fcc us department justice or other regulatory agencies can allow merger prohibit or allow if certain conditions are met one common condition that merger will be allowed if firm agrees sell off certain parts example 2006 johnson johnson bought pfizers consumer health division which included wellknown brands like listerine mouthwash sudafed cold medicine as a condition allowing merger johnson johnson was required sell off six brands other firms including zantac heartburn relief medication cortizone antiitch cream balmex diaper rash medication preserve a greater degree competition these markets us government approves most proposed mergers a marketoriented economy firms have freedom make their own choices private firms generally have freedom expand or reduce production set price they choose open new factories or sales facilities or close them hire workers or lay them off start selling new products or stop selling existing ones if owners want acquire a firm or be acquired or merge another firm this decision just one many that firms are free make these conditions managers private firms will sometimes make mistakes they may close down a factory which later turns out would have been profitable they may start selling a product that ends up losing money a merger between two companies can sometimes lead a clash corporate personalities 10-1-monopolistic-competition.question: how a monopolistic competitor chooses price quantity monopolistically competitive firm decides on its profitmaximizing quantity price much same way as a monopolist a monopolistic competitor like a monopolist faces a downwardsloping demand curve so will choose some combination price quantity along its perceived demand curve as an example a profitmaximizing monopolistic competitor consider authentic chinese pizza store which serves pizza cheese sweet sour sauce your choice vegetables meats although authentic chinese pizza must compete against other pizza businesses restaurants has a differentiated product firms perceived demand curve downward sloping as figure 103 shows first two columns table 101 quantity price total revenue marginal revenue total cost marginal cost average cost 10 23 230 23 340 34 34 20 20 400 17 400 6 20 30 18 540 14 480 8 16 40 16 640 10 580 10 1450 50 14 700 6 700 12 14 60 12 720 2 840 14 14 70 10 700 2 1020 18 1457 80 8 640 6 1280 26 16 table 101 revenue cost schedule we can multiply combinations price quantity at each point on demand curve calculate total revenue that firm would receive which third column table 101 we calculate marginal revenue fourth column as change total revenue divided by change quantity final columns table 101 show total cost marginal cost average cost as always we calculate marginal cost by dividing change total cost by change quantity while we calculate average cost by dividing total cost by quantity following work out feature shows how these firms calculate how much their products supply at what price work out how a monopolistic competitor determines how much produce at what price process by which a monopolistic competitor chooses its profitmaximizing quantity price resembles closely how a monopoly makes these decisions process first firm selects profitmaximizing quantity produce then firm decides what price charge that quantity step 1 monopolistic competitor determines its profitmaximizing level output this case authentic chinese pizza company will determine profitmaximizing quantity produce by considering its marginal revenues marginal costs two scenarios are possible if firm producing at a quantity output where marginal revenue exceeds marginal cost then firm should keep expanding production because each marginal unit adding profit by bringing more revenue than its cost this way firm will produce up quantity where mr mc if firm producing at a quantity where marginal costs exceed marginal revenue then each marginal unit costing more than revenue brings firm will increase its profits by reducing quantity output until mr mc this example mr mc intersect at a quantity 40 which profitmaximizing level output firm step 2 monopolistic competitor decides what price charge when firm has determined its profitmaximizing quantity output can then look its perceived demand curve find out what can charge that quantity output on graph we show this process as a vertical line reaching up through profitmaximizing quantity until hits firms perceived demand curve authentic chinese pizza should charge a price 16 per pizza a quantity 40once firm has chosen price quantity its a position calculate total revenue total cost profit at a quantity 40 price 16 lies above average cost curve so firm making economic profits from table 101 we can see that at an output 40 firms total revenue 640 its total cost 580 so profits are 60 figure 103 firms total revenues are rectangle quantity 40 on horizontal axis price 16 on vertical axis firms total costs are light shaded rectangle same quantity 40 on horizontal axis but average cost 1450 on vertical axis profits are total revenues minus total costs which shaded area above average cost curve although process by which a monopolistic competitor makes decisions about quantity price similar way which a monopolist makes such decisions two differences are worth remembering first although both a monopolist a monopolistic competitor face downwardsloping demand curves monopolists perceived demand curve market demand curve while perceived demand curve a monopolistic competitor based on extent its product differentiation how many competitors faces second a monopolist surrounded by barriers entry need not fear entry but a monopolistic competitor who earns profits must expect entry firms similar but differentiated products

10-2-oligopoly.question: why do oligopolies exist a combination barriers entry that create monopolies product differentiation that characterizes monopolistic competition can create setting an oligopoly example when a government grants a patent an invention one firm may create a monopoly when government grants patents example three different pharmaceutical companies that each has its own drug reducing high blood pressure those three firms may become an oligopoly similarly a natural monopoly will arise when quantity demanded a market only large enough a single firm operate at minimum longrun average cost curve such a setting market has room only one firm because no smaller firm can operate at a low enough average cost compete no larger firm could sell what produced given quantity demanded market quantity demanded market may also be two or three times quantity needed produce at minimum average cost curvewhich means that market would have room only two or three oligopoly firms they need not produce differentiated products again smaller firms would have higher average costs be unable compete while additional large firms would produce such a high quantity that they would not be able sell at a profitable price this combination economies scale market demand creates barrier entry which led boeingairbus oligopoly also called a duopoly large passenger aircraftthe product differentiation at heart monopolistic competition can also play a role creating oligopoly example firms may need reach a certain minimum size before they are able spend enough on advertising marketing create a recognizable brand name problem competing say cocacola or pepsi not that producing fizzy drinks technologically difficult but rather that creating a brand name marketing effort equal coke or pepsi an enormous task

collusion or competition when oligopoly firms a certain market decide what quantity produce what price charge they face a temptation act as if they were a monopoly by acting together oligopolistic firms can hold down industry output charge a higher price divide profit among themselves when firms act together this way reduce output keep prices high called collusion a group firms that have a formal agreement collude produce monopoly output sell at monopoly price called a cartel see following clear up feature a more indepth analysis difference between twoeconomists have understood a long time desire businesses avoid competing so that they can instead raise prices that they charge earn higher profits adam smith wrote wealth nations 1776 people same trade seldom meet together even merriment diversion but conversation ends a conspiracy against public or some contrivance raise priceseven when oligopolists recognize that they would benefit as a group by acting like a monopoly each individual oligopoly faces a private temptation produce just a slightly higher quantity earn slightly higher profitwhile still counting on other oligopolists hold down their production keep prices high if at least some oligopolists give this temptation start producing more then market price will fall a small handful oligopoly firms may end up competing so fiercely that they all find themselves earning zero economic profitsas if they were perfect competitors

market structures instead economists use game theory a branch mathematics that analyzes situations which players must make decisions then receive payoffs based on what other players decide do game theory has found widespread applications social sciences as well as business law military strategy prisoners dilemma a scenario which gains from cooperation are larger than rewards from pursuing year because youre remaining silent judge going stick you eight years prison why dont you get smart if you confess too well cut your jail time down five years your partner will get five years also over next room another police officer giving exactly same speech prisoner b what police officers do not say that if both prisoners remain silent evidence against them not especially strong prisoners will end up only two years jail each game theory situation facing two prisoners table 102 understand dilemma first consider choices from prisoner as point view if a believes that b will confess then a should confess too so as not get stuck eight years prison however if a believes that b will not confess then a will be tempted act selfishly confess so as serve only one year key point that a has an incentive confess regardless what choice b makes b faces same set choices thus will have an incentive confess regardless what choice a makes confess called dominant strategy strategy an individual or firm will pursue regardless other individuals or firms decision result that if prisoners pursue their own selfinterest both are likely confess end up being sentenced a total 10 years jail time between them prisoner b remain silent cooperate other prisoner confess do not cooperate other prisoner prisoner a remain silent cooperate other prisoner a gets 2 years b gets 2 years a gets 8 years b gets 1 year confess do not cooperate other prisoner a gets 1 year b gets 8 years a gets 5 years b gets 5 years table 102 prisoners dilemma problem game called a dilemma because if two prisoners had cooperated by both remaining silent they would only have been incarcerated two years each a total four years between them if two prisoners can work out some way cooperating so that neither one will confess they will both be better off than if they each follow their own individual selfinterest which this case leads straight into longer terms

duopoly if firms a b both agree hold down output they are acting together as a monopoly will each earn 1000 profits however both firms dominant strategy increase output which case each will earn 400 profits firm b hold down output cooperate other firm increase output do not cooperate other firm firm a hold down output cooperate other firm a gets 1000 b gets 1000 a gets 200 b gets 1500 increase output do not cooperate other firm a gets 1500 b gets 200 a gets 400 b gets 400 table 103 a prisoners dilemma oligopolists can two firms trust each other consider situation firm a if a thinks that b will cheat on their agreement increase output then a will increase output too because a profit 400 when both firms increase output bottom righthand choice table 103 better than a profit only 200 if a keeps output low b raises output upper righthand choice table if a thinks that b will cooperate by holding down output then a may seize opportunity earn higher profits by raising output after all if b going hold down output then a can earn 1500 profits by expanding output bottom lefthand choice table compared only 1000 by holding down output as well upper lefthand choice table thus firm a will reason that makes sense expand output if b holds down output that also makes sense expand output if b raises output again b faces a parallel set decisions that will lead b also expand outputthe result this prisoners dilemma often that even though a b could make highest combined profits by cooperating producing a lower level output acting like a monopolist two firms may well end up a situation where they each increase output earn only 400 each profits following clear up feature discusses one cartel scandal particular

them prisoner b remain silent cooperate other prisoner confess do not cooperate other prisoner prisoner a remain silent cooperate other prisoner a gets 2 years b gets 2 years a gets 8 years b gets 1 output do not cooperate other firm a gets 1500 b gets 200 a gets 400 b gets 400 table 103 a prisoners dilemma oligopolists can two firms trust each other consider situation firm a if a thinks that b 8-3-entry-and-exit-decisions-in-the-long.question: how entry exit lead zero profits long run no perfectly competitive firm acting alone can affect market price however combination many firms entering or exiting market will affect overall supply market turn a shift supply market as a whole will affect market price entry exit from market are driving forces behind a process that long run pushes price down minimum average total costs so that all firms are earning a zero profitto understand how shortrun profits a perfectly competitive firm will evaporate long run imagine following situation market longrun equilibrium where all firms earn zero economic profits producing output level where p mr mc p ac no firm has incentive enter or leave market lets say that products demand increases that market price goes up existing firms industry are now facing a higher price than before so they will increase production new output level where p mr mcthis will temporarily make market price rise above minimum point on average cost curve therefore existing firms market will now be earning economic profits however these economic profits attract other firms enter market entry many new firms causes market supply curve shift right as supply curve shifts right market price starts decreasing that economic profits fall new existing firms as long as there are still profits market entry will continue shift supply right this will stop whenever market price driven down zeroprofit level where no firm earning economic profitsshortrun losses will fade away by reversing this process say that market longrun equilibrium this time instead demand decreases that market price starts falling existing firms industry are now facing a lower price than before as will be below average cost curve they will now be making economic losses some firms will continue producing where new p mr mc as long as they are able cover their average variable costs some firms will have shut down immediately as they will not be able cover their average variable costs will then only incur their fixed costs minimizing their losses exit many firms causes market supply curve shift left as supply curve shifts left market price starts rising economic losses start be lower this process ends whenever market price rises zeroprofit level where existing firms are no longer losing money are at zero profits again thus while a perfectly competitive firm can earn profits short run long run process entry will push down prices until they reach zeroprofit level conversely while a perfectly competitive firm may earn losses short run firms will not continually lose money long run firms making losses are able escape from their fixed costs their exit from market will push price back up zeroprofit level long run this process entry exit will drive price perfectly competitive markets zeroprofit point at bottom ac curve where marginal cost crosses average cost

situation yoga center which has signed a contract rent space that costs 10000 per month if firm decides operate its marginal costs hiring yoga teachers 15000 month if firm shuts down must still pay fixed costs second scenario yoga center has clients that earn center revenues 10000 month but ultimately experiences losses 15000 due having hire yoga instructors cover classes third scenario yoga center earns revenues 20000 month but experiences losses 5000 all three cases yoga center loses money all three cases when rental contract expires long run assuming revenues do not improve firm should exit this business short run though decision varies depending on level losses whether firm can cover its variable costs scenario 1 center does not have any revenues so hiring yoga teachers would increase variable costs losses so should shut down only incur its fixed costs scenario 2 centers losses are greater because does not make enough revenue offset increased variable costs so should shut down immediately only incur its fixed costs if price below minimum average variable cost firm must shut down contrast scenario 3 revenue that center can earn high enough that losses diminish when remains open so center should remain open short run scenario 1 if center shuts down now revenues are zero but will not incur any variable costs would only need pay fixed costs 10000 profit total 8-self-check-questions.question: question 8 a market perfect competition longrun equilibrium what happens market if labor unions are able increase wages workers
8-self-check-questions.answer: 8 when wages increase costs production increase some firms would now be making economic losses would shut down supply curve then starts shifting left pushing market price up this process ends when all firms remaining market earn zero economic profits result a contraction output produced market

8-self-check-questions.question: question 10 explain how profitmaximizing rule setting p mc leads a perfectly competitive market be allocatively efficient
8-self-check-questions.answer: 10 think market price as representing gain society from a purchase since represents what someone willing pay think marginal cost as representing cost society from making last unit a good if p mc then benefits from producing more a good exceed costs society would gain from producing more good if p mc then social costs producing marginal good exceed social benefits society should produce less good only if p mc rule applied by a profitmaximizing perfectly competitive firm will societys costs benefits be balance this choice will be option that brings greatest overall benefit society

8-self-check-questions.question: question 7 if new technology a perfectly competitive market brings about a substantial reduction costs production how will this affect market
8-self-check-questions.answer: 7 a technological improvement that brings about a reduction costs production an adjustment process will take place market technological improvement will result an increase supply curves by individual firms at market level existing firms will experience higher profits a while which will attract other firms into market this entry process will stop whenever market supply increases enough both by existing new firms so profits are driven back zero

8-self-check-questions.question: question 9 productive efficiency allocative efficiency are two concepts achieved long run a perfectly competitive market these are two reasons why we call them perfect how would you use these two concepts analyze other market structures label them imperfect
8-self-check-questions.answer: 9 perfect competition considered be perfect because both allocative productive efficiency are met at same time a longrun equilibrium if a market structure results longrun equilibrium that does not minimize average total costs andor does not charge a price equal marginal cost then either allocative or productive or both efficiencies are not met therefore market cannot be labeled perfect

9-2-how-to-measure-changes-in-the-cost-o.question: cpi core inflation index imagine if you were driving a company truck across country you probably would care about things like prices available roadside food motel rooms as well as trucks operating condition however manager firm might have different priorities he would care mostly about trucks ontime performance much less so about food you were eating places you were staying other words company manager would be paying attention firms production while ignoring transitory elements that impacted you but did not affect companys bottom line a sense a similar situation occurs regard measures inflation as weve learned cpi measures prices as they affect everyday household spending economists typically calculate a core inflation index by taking cpi excluding volatile economic variables this way economists have a better sense underlying trends prices that affect cost living examples excluded variables include energy food prices which can jump around from month month because weather according an article by kent bernhard during hurricane katrina 2005 a key supply point nations gasoline was nearly knocked out gas prices quickly shot up across nation some places by up 40 cents a gallon one day this was not cause an economic policy but rather a shortlived event until pumps were restored region this case cpi that month would register change as a cost living event households but core inflation index would remain unchanged as a result federal reserves decisions on interest rates would not be influenced similarly droughts can cause worldwide spikes food prices that if temporary do not affect nations economic capability as former chairman federal reserve ben bernanke noted 1999 about core inflation index provides a better guide monetary policy than other indices since measures more persistent underlying inflation rather than transitory influences on price level bernanke also noted that helps communicate that federal reserve does not need respond every inflationary shock since some price changes are transitory not part a structural change economy sum both cpi core inflation index are important but serve different audiences cpi helps households understand their overall cost living from month month while core inflation index a preferred gauge from which make important government policy changes

institutions laws affecting labor market or other possibly relevant factors one primary determinant demand labor from firms how they perceive state macro economy if firms believe that business expanding then at any given wage they will desire hire a greater quantity labor labor demand curve shifts right conversely if firms perceive that economy slowing down or entering a recession then they will wish hire a lower quantity labor at any given wage labor demand curve will shift left economists call variation unemployment that economy causes moving from expansion recession or from recession expansion ie business cycle cyclical unemploymentfrom standpoint supplyanddemand model competitive flexible labor markets unemployment represents something a puzzle a supplyanddemand model a labor market as figure 85 illustrates labor market should move toward an equilibrium wage quantity at equilibrium wage we equilibrium quantity qe labor supplied by workers should be equal quantity labor people are choosing be unemployedprobably a few people are unemployed because unrealistic expectations about wages but they do not represent majority unemployed instead unemployed people often have friends or acquaintances similar skill levels who are employed unemployed would be willing work at jobs wages similar what those people are receiving however employers their friends acquaintances do not seem be hiring other words these people are involuntarily unemployed what causes involuntary unemployment

why wages might be sticky downward if a labor market model flexible wages does not describe unemployment very wellbecause predicts that anyone willing work at going wage can always find a jobthen may prove useful consider economic models which wages are not flexible or adjust only very slowly particular even though wage increases may occur relative ease wage decreases are few far between one set reasons why wages may be sticky downward as economists put involves economic laws institutions lowskilled workers receiving minimum wage illegal reduce their wages union workers operating under a multiyear contract a company wage cuts might violate contract create a labor dispute or a strike however minimum wages union contracts are not a sufficient reason why wages would be sticky downward us economy as a whole after all out 733 million or so employed workers us economy who earn wages by hour only about 11 millionless than 2 totaldo not receive compensation above minimum wage similarly labor unions represent only about 12 american wage salary workers other highincome countries more workers may have their wages determined by unions or minimum wage may be set at a level that applies a larger share workers however united states these two factors combined affect only about 15 or less labor forceeconomists looking reasons why wages might be sticky downwards have focused on factors that may characterize most labor relationships economy not just a few many have proposed a number different theories but they share a common toneone argument that even employees who are not union members often work under an implicit contract which that employer will try keep wages from falling when economy weak or business having trouble employee will not expect huge salary increases when economy or business strong this wagesetting behavior acts like a form insurance employee has some protection against wage declines bad times but pays that protection lower wages good times clearly this sort implicit contract means that firms will be hesitant cut wages lest workers feel betrayed work less hard or even leave firm efficiency wage theory argues that workers productivity depends on their pay so employers will often find worthwhile pay their employees somewhat more than market conditions might dictate one reason that employees who receive better pay than others will be more productive because they recognize that if they were lose their current jobs they would suffer a decline salary as a result they are motivated work harder stay current employer addition employers know that costly timeconsuming hire train new employees so they would prefer pay workers a little extra now rather than lose them have hire train new workers thus by avoiding wage cuts employer minimizes costs training hiring new workers reaps benefits wellmotivated employeesthe adverse selection wage cuts argument points out that if an employer reacts poor business conditions by reducing wages all workers then best workers those best employment alternatives at other firms are most likely leave least attractive workers fewer employment alternatives are more likely stay consequently firms are more likely choose which workers should depart through layoffs firings rather than trimming wages across board sometimes companies that are experiencing difficult times can persuade workers take a pay cut short term still retain most firms workers however far more typical companies lay off some workers rather than cut wages everyonethe insideroutsider model labor force simple terms argues that those already working firms are insiders while new employees at least a time are outsiders a firm depends on its insiders keep organization running smoothly be familiar routine procedures train new employees however cutting wages will alienate insiders damage firms productivity prospectsfinally relative wage coordination argument points out that even if most workers were hypothetically willing see a decline their own wages bad economic times as long as everyone else also experiences such a decline there no obvious way a decentralized economy implement such a plan instead workers confronted possibility a wage cut will worry that other workers will not have such a wage cut so a wage cut means being worse off both absolute terms relative others as a result workers fight hard against wage cuts these theories why wages tend not move downward differ their logic their implications figuring out strengths weaknesses each theory an ongoing subject research controversy among economists all tend imply that wages will decline only very slowly if at all even when economy or a business having tough times when wages are inflexible unlikely fall then either shortrun or longrun unemployment can result figure 86 illustrates thisfigure 87 shows interaction between shifts labor demand wages that are sticky downward figure 87 a illustrates situation which demand labor shifts right from d0 d1 this case equilibrium wage rises from w0 w1 equilibrium quantity labor hired increases from q0 q1 does not hurt employee morale at all wages risefigure 87 b shows situation which demand labor shifts left from d0 d1 as would tend do a recession because wages are sticky downward they do not adjust toward what would have been new equilibrium wage w1 at least not short run instead after shift labor demand curve same quantity workers willing work at that wage as before however quantity workers demanded at that wage has declined from original equilibrium q0 q2 gap between original equilibrium quantity q0 new quantity demanded labor q2 represents workers who would be willing work at going wage but cannot find jobs gap represents economic meaning unemploymentthis analysis helps explain connection that we noted earlier that unemployment tends rise recessions decline during expansions overall state economy shifts labor demand curve combined wages that are sticky downwards unemployment changes rise unemployment that occurs because a recession cyclical unemployment

8-4-what-causes-changes-in-unemployment-.question: frictional unemployment a market economy some companies are always going broke a variety reasons old technology poor management good management that happened make bad decisions shifts tastes consumers so that less firms product desired a large customer who went broke or tough domestic or foreign competitors conversely other companies will be doing very well just opposite reasons looking hire more employees a perfect world all those who lost jobs would immediately find new ones however real world even if number job seekers equal number job vacancies takes time find out about new jobs interview figure out if new job a good match or perhaps sell a house buy another proximity a new job economists call unemployment that occurs meantime as workers move between jobs frictional unemployment frictional unemployment not inherently a bad thing takes time on part both employer individual match those looking employment correct job openings individuals companies be successful productive you want people find job which they are best suited not just first job offeredin mid2000s before 20082009 recession was true that about 7 us workers saw their jobs disappear any threemonth period however periods economic growth these destroyed jobs are counterbalanced economy as a whole by a larger number jobs created 2019 example there were typically about 6 million unemployed people at any given time us economy even though about twothirds those unemployed people found a job 14 weeks or fewer unemployment rate did not change much during year because those who found new jobs were largely offset by others who lost jobsof course would be preferable if people who were losing jobs could immediately easily move into newly created jobs but real world that not possible someone who laid off by a textile mill south carolina cannot turn around immediately start working a textile mill california instead adjustment process happens ripples some people find new jobs near their old ones while others find that they must move new locations some people can do a very similar job a different company while others must start new career paths some people may be near retirement decide look only parttime work while others want an employer that offers a longterm career path frictional unemployment that results from people moving between jobs a dynamic economy may account one two percentage points total unemploymentthe level frictional unemployment will depend on how easy workers learn about alternative jobs which may reflect ease communications about job prospects economy extent frictional unemployment will also depend some extent on how willing people are move new areas find jobswhich turn may depend on history culture frictional unemployment natural rate unemployment also seem depend on age distribution population figure 84 b showed that unemployment rates are typically lower people between 2554 years age or aged 55 over than they are those who are younger primeage workers as those 2554 age bracket are sometimes called are typically at a place their lives when they want have a job income arriving at all times addition older workers who lose jobs may prefer opt retirement by contrast likely that a relatively high proportion those who are under 25 will be trying out jobs life options this leads greater job mobility hence higher frictional unemployment thus a society a relatively high proportion young workers like us beginning mid1960s when baby boomers began entering labor market will tend have a higher unemployment rate than a society a higher proportion its workers older ages

structural unemployment another factor that influences natural rate unemployment amount structural unemployment structurally unemployed are individuals who have no jobs because they lack skills valued by labor market either because demand has shifted away from skills they do have or because they never learned any skills an example former would be unemployment among aerospace engineers after us space program downsized 1970s an example latter would be high school dropouts some people worry that technology causes structural unemployment past new technologies have put lower skilled employees out work but at same time they create demand higher skilled workers use new technologies education seems be key minimizing amount structural unemployment individuals who have degrees can be retrained if they become structurally unemployed people no skills little education that option more limited

natural unemployment potential real gdp natural unemployment rate related two other important concepts full employment potential real gdp economists consider economy be at full employment when actual unemployment rate equal natural unemployment rate when economy at full employment real gdp equal potential real gdp by contrast when economy below full employment unemployment rate greater than natural unemployment rate real gdp less than potential finally when economy above full employment then unemployment rate less than natural unemployment rate real gdp greater than potential operating above potential only possible a short while since analogous all workers working overtime

wages may discourage businesses from hiring lowskill workers government rules may encourage support powerful unions which can then push up wages union workers but at a cost discouraging businesses from hiring those workers

7-4-economic-convergence.question: arguments favoring convergence several arguments suggest that lowincome countries might have an advantage achieving greater worker productivity economic growth future a first argument based on diminishing marginal returns even though deepening human physical capital will tend increase gdp per capita law diminishing returns suggests that as an economy continues increase its human physical capital marginal gains economic growth will diminish example raising average education level population by two years from a tenthgrade level a high school diploma while holding all other inputs constant would produce a certain increase output an additional twoyear increase so that average person had a twoyear college degree would increase output further but marginal gain would be smaller yet another additional twoyear increase level education so that average person would have a fouryearcollege bachelors degree would increase output still further but marginal increase would again be smaller a similar lesson holds physical capital if quantity physical capital available average worker increases by say 5000 10000 again while holding all other inputs constant will increase level output an additional increase from 10000 15000 will increase output further but marginal increase will be smaller lowincome countries like china india tend have lower levels human capital physical capital so an investment capital deepening should have a larger marginal effect these countries than highincome countries where levels human physical capital are already relatively high diminishing returns implies that lowincome economies could converge levels that highincome countries achievea second argument that lowincome countries may find easier improve their technologies than highincome countries highincome countries must continually invent new technologies whereas lowincome countries can often find ways applying technology that has already been invented well understood economist alexander gerschenkron 19041978 gave this phenomenon a memorable name advantages backwardness course he did not literally mean that an advantage have a lower standard living he was pointing out that a country that behind has some extra potential catching up finally optimists argue that many countries have observed experience those that have grown more quickly have learned from moreover once people a country begin enjoy benefits a higher standard living they may be more likely build support marketfriendly institutions that will help provide this standard living

arguments that convergence neither inevitable nor likely if economys growth depended only on deepening human capital physical capital then we would expect that economys growth rate slow down over long run because diminishing marginal returns however there another crucial factor aggregate production function technologydeveloping new technology can provide a way an economy sidestep diminishing 5-3-elasticity-and-pricing.question: does raising price bring more revenueimagine that a band on tour playing an indoor arena 15000 seats keep this example simple assume that band keeps all money from ticket sales assume further that band pays costs its appearance but that these costs like travel setting up stage are same regardless how many people are audience finally assume that all tickets have same price same insights apply if ticket prices are more expensive some seats than others but calculations become more complicated band knows that faces a downwardsloping demand curve that if band raises ticket price will sell fewer seats how should band set ticket price generate most total revenue which this example because costs are fixed will also mean highest profits band should band sell more tickets at a lower price or fewer tickets at a higher pricethe key concept thinking about collecting most revenue price elasticity demand total revenue price times quantity tickets sold imagine that band starts off thinking about a certain price which will result sale a certain quantity tickets three possibilities are table 53 if demand elastic at that price level then band should cut price because percentage drop price will result an even larger percentage increase quantity soldthus raising total revenue however if demand inelastic at that original quantity level then band should raise ticket price because a certain percentage increase price will result a smaller percentage decrease quantity soldand total revenue will rise if demand has a unitary elasticity at that quantity then an equal percentage change quantity will offset a moderate percentage change priceso band will earn same revenue whether moderately increases or decreases ticket price if demand then therefore elastic change qd change p change qd change p a given rise p will be more than offset by a larger fall q so that total revenue p q falls unitary change qd change p change qd change p a given rise p will be exactly offset by an equal fall q so that total revenue p q unchanged inelastic change qd change p change qd change p a given rise p will cause a smaller fall q so that total revenue p q rises table 53 will band earn more revenue by changing ticket prices what if band keeps cutting price because demand elastic until reaches a level where sells all 15000 seats available arena if demand remains elastic at that quantity band might try move a bigger arena so that could slash ticket prices further see a larger percentage increase quantity tickets sold however if 15000seat arena all that available or if a larger arena would add substantially costs then this option may not workconversely a few bands are so famous or have such fanatical followings that demand tickets may be inelastic right up point where arena full these bands can if they wish keep raising ticket price ironically some most popular bands could make more revenue by setting prices so high that arena not fullbut those who buy tickets would have pay very high prices however bands sometimes choose sell tickets less than absolute maximum they might be able charge often hope that fans will feel happier spend more on recordings tshirts other paraphernalia
