5-3-elasticity-and-pricing.question: elasticity tax incidencethe example cigarette taxes demonstrated that because demand inelastic taxes are not effective at reducing equilibrium quantity smoking they are mainly passed along consumers form higher prices analysis or manner how a tax burden divided between consumers producers called tax incidence typically tax incidence or burden falls both on consumers producers taxed good however if one wants predict which group will bear most burden all one needs do examine elasticity demand supply tobacco example tax burden falls on most inelastic side market if demand more inelastic than supply consumers bear most tax burden if supply more inelastic than demand sellers bear most tax burden intuition this simple when demand inelastic consumers are not very responsive price changes quantity demanded reduces only modestly when tax introduced case smoking demand inelastic because consumers are addicted product government can then pass tax burden along consumers form higher prices without much a decline equilibrium quantitysimilarly when a government introduces a tax a market an inelastic supply such as example beachfront hotels sellers have no alternative than accept lower prices their business taxes do not greatly affect equilibrium quantity tax burden now passes on sellers if supply was elastic sellers had possibility reorganizing their businesses avoid supplying taxed good tax burden on sellers would be much smaller tax would result a much lower quantity sold instead lower prices received figure 510 illustrates this relationship between tax incidence elasticity demand supply figure 510 a supply inelastic demand elastic such as example beachfront hotels while consumers may have other vacation choices sellers cant easily move their businesses by introducing a tax government essentially creates a wedge between price paid by consumers pc price received by producers pp other words total price paid by consumers part retained by sellers part paid government form a tax distance between pc pp tax rate new market price pc but sellers receive only pp per unit sold as they pay pcpp government since we can view a tax as raising costs production this could also be represented by a leftward shift supply curve where new supply curve would intercept demand at new quantity qt simplicity figure 510 omits shift equilibrium price pe tax incidence on sellers given by difference between initial equilibrium price pe price they receive after tax introduced pp figure 510 a tax burden falls disproportionately on sellers a larger proportion tax revenue shaded area due resulting lower price received by sellers than by resulting higher prices paid by buyers figure 510 b describes example tobacco excise tax where supply more elastic than demand tax incidence now falls disproportionately on consumers as shown by large difference between price they pay pc initial equilibrium price pe sellers receive a lower price than before tax but this difference much smaller than change consumers price from this analysis one can also predict whether a tax likely create a large revenue or not more elastic demand curve more likely that consumers will reduce quantity instead paying higher prices more elastic supply curve more likely that sellers will reduce quantity sold instead taking lower prices a market where was implemented 2013 has been controversial can delay industry profitability therefore hamper startups medical innovation tax was repealed late 2019 however whether tax burden falls mostly on medical device industry or on patients depends simply on elasticity demand supply

seats how should band set ticket price generate most total revenue which this example because costs are fixed will also mean highest profits band should band sell more tickets at a lower price or fewer tickets at a higher pricethe key concept thinking about collecting most revenue price elasticity demand total revenue price times quantity tickets sold imagine that band starts off thinking about a certain price which will result sale a certain quantity tickets three possibilities are table 53 if demand elastic at that price level then band should cut price because percentage drop price will result an even larger percentage increase quantity soldthus raising total revenue however if demand inelastic at that original quantity level then band should raise ticket price because a certain percentage increase price will result a smaller percentage decrease quantity soldand total revenue will rise if demand has a unitary elasticity at that quantity then an equal percentage change quantity will offset a moderate percentage change priceso band will earn same revenue whether moderately increases or decreases ticket price if demand then therefore elastic change qd change p change qd change p a given rise p will be more than offset by a larger fall q so that total revenue p q falls unitary change qd change p change qd change p a given rise p will be exactly offset by an equal fall q so that total revenue p q unchanged inelastic change qd change p change qd change p a given rise p will cause a smaller fall q so that total revenue p q rises table 53 will band earn more 4-key-concepts-and-summary.question: 41 demand supply at work labor markets labor market households are on supply side market firms are on demand side market financial capital households firms can be on either side market they are suppliers financial capital when they save or make financial investments demanders financial capital when they borrow or receive financial investments demand supply analysis labor markets we can measure price by annual salary or hourly wage received we can measure quantity labor various ways like number workers or number hours workedfactors that can shift demand curve labor include a change quantity demanded product that labor produces a change production process that uses more or less labor a change government policy that affects quantity labor that firms wish hire at a given wage demand can also increase or decrease shift response workers level education training technology number companies availability price other inputs main factors that can shift supply curve labor are how desirable a job appears workers relative alternatives government policy that either restricts or encourages quantity workers trained job number workers economy required education 42 demand supply financial markets demand supply analysis financial markets price rate return or interest rate received we measure quantity by money that flows from those who supply financial capital those who demand ittwo factors can shift supply financial capital a certain investment if people want alter their existing levels consumption if riskiness or return on one investment changes relative other investments factors that can shift demand capital include business confidence consumer confidence futuresince financial investments received present are typically repaid future 43 market system as an efficient mechanism information market price system provides a highly efficient mechanism disseminating information about relative scarcities goods services labor financial capital market participants do not need know why prices have changed only that changes require them revisit previous decisions they made about supply demand price controls hide information about true scarcity products thereby cause misallocation resources

4-self-check-questions.question: question 11 select correct
4-self-check-questions.answer: a price floor will usually shift demand supply both neither illustrate your

4-self-check-questions.question: question 6 if a usury law limits interest rates no more than 35 what would likely impact be on amount loans made interest rates paid
4-self-check-questions.answer: 6 if market interest rates stay their normal range an interest rate limit 35 would not be binding if equilibrium interest rate rose above 35 interest rate would be capped at that rate quantity loans would be lower than equilibrium quantity causing a shortage loans

4-self-check-questions.question: question 7 which following changes financial market will lead a decline interest rates a rise demand a fall demand a rise supply a fall supply
4-self-check-questions.answer: 7 b c will lead a fall interest rates at a lower demand lenders will not be able charge as much more available lenders competition borrowers will drive rates down

4-self-check-questions.question: question 8 which following changes financial market will lead an increase quantity loans made received a rise demand a fall demand a rise supply a fall supply
4-self-check-questions.answer: 8 a c will increase quantity loans more people who want borrow will result more loans being given as will more people who want lend

question 12 select correct

question 3 why demand curve constant unitary elasticity concave
5-self-check-questions.answer: 3 demand curve constant unitary elasticity concave because absolute value declines price are not identical left side curve starts high prices then price falls by smaller amounts as goes down toward right side this results a slope demand that steeper on left but flatter on right creating a curved concave shape

4-2-demand-and-supply-in-financial-marke.question: who demands who supplies financial markets any market price what suppliers receive what demanders pay financial markets those who supply financial capital through saving expect receive a rate return while those who demand financial capital by receiving funds expect pay a rate return this rate return can come a variety forms depending on type investment simplest example a rate return interest rate example when you supply money into a savings account at a bank you receive interest on your deposit interest bank pays you as a percent your deposits interest rate similarly if you demand a loan buy a car or a computer you will need pay interest on money you borrowlets consider market borrowing money credit cards 2021 almost 200 million americans were cardholders credit cards allow you borrow money from cards issuer pay back borrowed amount plus interest although most allow you a period time which you can repay loan without paying interest a typical credit card interest rate ranges from 12 18 per year may 2021 americans had about 807 billion outstanding credit card debts as 2021 just over 45 american families carried some credit card debt lets say that on average annual interest rate credit card borrowing 15 per year thus americans pay tens billions dollars every year interest on their credit cardsplus basic fees credit card or fees late paymentsfigure 45 illustrates demand supply financial market credit cards horizontal axis financial market shows quantity money loaned or borrowed this market vertical or price axis shows rate return which case credit card borrowing we can measure an interest rate table 45 shows quantity financial capital that consumers demand at various interest rates quantity that credit card firms often banks are willing supply interest rate quantity financial capital demanded borrowing billions quantity financial capital supplied lending billions 11 800 420 13 700 510 15 600 600 17 550 660 19 500 720 21 480 750 table 45 demand supply borrowing money credit cards laws demand supply continue apply financial markets according law demand a higher rate return that a higher price will decrease quantity demanded as interest rate rises consumers will reduce quantity that they borrow according law supply a higher price increases quantity supplied consequently as interest rate paid on credit card borrowing rises more firms will be eager issue credit cards encourage customers use them conversely if interest rate on credit cards falls quantity financial capital supplied credit card market will decrease quantity demanded will increase
4-2-demand-and-supply-in-financial-marke.section_name: Who Demands and Who Supplies in Financial Markets?

4-2-demand-and-supply-in-financial-marke.question: equilibrium financial markets financial market credit cards figure 45 supply curve s demand curve d cross at equilibrium point e equilibrium occurs at an interest rate 15 where quantity funds demanded quantity supplied are equal at an equilibrium quantity 600 billionif interest rate remember this measures price financial market above equilibrium level then an excess supply or a surplus financial capital will arise this market example at an interest rate 21 quantity funds supplied increases 750 billion while quantity demanded decreases 480 billion at this aboveequilibrium interest rate firms are eager supply loans credit card borrowers but relatively few people or businesses wish borrow as a result some credit card firms will lower interest rates or other fees they charge attract more business this strategy will push interest rate down toward equilibrium level if interest rate below equilibrium then excess demand or a shortage funds occurs this market at an interest rate 13 quantity funds credit card borrowers demand increases 700 billion but quantity credit card firms are willing supply only 510 billion this situation credit card firms will perceive that they are overloaded eager borrowers conclude that they have an opportunity raise interest rates or fees interest rate will face economic pressures creep up toward equilibrium levelthe fred database publishes some two dozen measures interest rates including interest rates on credit cards automobile loans personal loans mortgage loans more you can find these at fred website

shifts demand supply financial markets those who supply financial capital face two broad decisions how much save how divide up their savings among different forms financial investments we will discuss each these turn participants financial markets must decide when they prefer consume goods now or future economists call this intertemporal decision making because involves decisions across time unlike a decision about what buy from grocery store people make investment or savings decisions across a period time sometimes a long periodmost workers save retirement because their income present greater than their needs while opposite will be true once they retire thus they save today supply financial markets if their income increases they save more if their perceived situation future changes they change amount their saving example there some evidence that social security program that workers pay into order qualify government checks after retirement has tended reduce quantity financial capital that workers save if this true social security has shifted supply financial capital at any interest rate leftby contrast many college students need money today when their income low or nonexistent pay their college expenses as a result they borrow today demand from financial markets once they graduate become employed they will pay back loans individuals borrow money purchase homes or cars a business seeks financial investment so that has funds build a factory or invest a research development project that will not pay off five years ten years or even more thus when consumers businesses have greater confidence that they will be able repay future quantity demanded financial capital at any given interest rate will shift rightfor example technology boom late 1990s many businesses became extremely confident that investments new technology would have a high rate return their demand financial capital shifted right conversely during 2008 2009 great recession their demand financial capital at any given interest rate shifted leftto this point we have been looking at saving total now let us consider what affects saving different types financial investments deciding between different forms financial investments suppliers financial capital will have consider rates return risks involved rate return a positive attribute investments but risk a negative if investment a becomes more risky or return diminishes then savers will shift their funds investment band supply curve financial capital investment a will shift back left while supply curve capital investment b shifts right
4-2-demand-and-supply-in-financial-marke.section_name: Shifts in Demand and Supply in Financial Markets

4-2-demand-and-supply-in-financial-marke.question: united states as a global borrower global economy trillions dollars financial investment cross national borders every year early 2000s financial investors from foreign countries were investing several hundred billion dollars per year more us economy than us financial investors were investing abroad following work out deals one macroeconomic concerns us economy recent years work out effect growing us debt imagine that foreign investors viewed us economy as a less desirable place put their money because fears about growth us public debt using fourstep process analyzing how changes supply demand affect equilibrium outcomes how would increased us public debt affect equilibrium price quantity capital us financial marketsstep 1 draw a diagram showing demand supply financial capital that represents original scenario which foreign investors are pouring money into us economy figure 46 shows a demand curve d a supply curve s where supply capital includes funds arriving from foreign investors original equilibrium e0 occurs at interest rate r0 quantity financial investment q0step 2 will diminished confidence us economy as a place invest affect demand or supply financial capital yes will affect supply many foreign investors look us financial markets store their money safe financial vehicles low risk stable returns diminished confidence means us financial assets will be seen as more riskystep 3 will supply increase or decrease when enthusiasm foreign investors investing their money us economy diminishes supply financial capital shifts left figure 47 shows supply curve shift from s0 s1step 4 thus foreign investors diminished enthusiasm leads a new equilibrium e1 which occurs at higher interest rate r1 lower quantity financial investment q1 short us borrowers will have pay more interest on their borrowing
4-2-demand-and-supply-in-financial-marke.section_name: The United States as a Global Borrower

4-2-demand-and-supply-in-financial-marke.question: price ceilings financial markets usury laws as we noted earlier about 200 million americans own credit cards their interest payments fees total tens billions dollars each year little wonder that political pressures sometimes arise setting limits on interest rates or fees that credit card companies charge firms that issue credit cards including banks oil companies phone companies retail stores respond that higher interest rates are necessary cover losses created by those who borrow on their credit cards who do not repay on time or at all these companies also point out that cardholders can avoid paying interest if they pay their bills on timeconsider credit card market as figure 48 illustrates this financial market vertical axis shows interest rate which price financial market demanders credit card market are households businesses suppliers are companies that issue credit cards this figure does not use specific numbers which would be hypothetical any case but instead focuses on underlying economic relationships imagine a law imposes a price ceiling that holds interest rate charged on credit cards at rate rc which lies below interest rate r0 that would otherwise have prevailed market horizontal dashed line at interest rate rc figure 48 shows price ceiling demand supply model predicts that at lower price ceiling interest rate quantity demanded credit card debt will increase from its original level q0 qd however quantity supplied credit card debt will decrease from original q0 qs at price ceiling rc quantity demanded will exceed quantity supplied consequently a number people who want have credit cards are willing pay prevailing interest rate will find that companies are unwilling issue cards them result will be a credit shortagemany states do have usury laws which impose an upper limit on interest rate that lenders can charge however many cases these upper limits are well above market interest rate example if interest rate not allowed rise above 30 per year can still fluctuate below that level according market forces a price ceiling that set at a relatively high level nonbinding will have no practical effect unless equilibrium price soars high enough exceed price ceiling
4-2-demand-and-supply-in-financial-marke.section_name: Price Ceilings in Financial Markets: Usury Laws

a decision about what buy from grocery store people make investment or savings decisions across a period time sometimes a long periodmost workers save retirement because their income present greater than their needs while opposite will be true once they retire thus they save today supply financial markets if their income increases they save more if their perceived situation future changes they encourage more nurses train or work minneapolisst paulbloomington again price quantity labor market will move toward equilibrium

shifts labor demand demand curve labor shows quantity labor employers wish hire at any given salary or wage rate under ceteris paribus assumption a change wage or salary will result a change quantity demanded labor if wage rate increases employers will want hire fewer employees quantity labor demanded will decrease there will be a movement upward along demand curve if wages salaries decrease employers are more likely hire a greater number workers quantity labor demanded will increase resulting a downward movement along demand curveshifts demand curve labor occur many reasons one key reason that demand labor based on demand good or service that produced example more new automobiles consumers demand greater number workers automakers will need hire therefore demand labor called a derived demand here are some examples derived demand labor demand chefs dependent on demand restaurant meals demand pharmacists dependent on demand prescription drugs demand attorneys dependent on demand legal services as demand goods services increases demand labor will increase or shift right meet employers production requirements as demand goods services decreases demand labor will decrease or shift left table 42 shows that addition derived demand labor demand can also increase or decrease shift response several factors factors results demand output when demand good produced output increases both output price profitability increase as a result producers demand more labor ramp up production education training a welltrained educated workforce causes an increase demand that labor by employers increased levels productivity within workforce will cause demand labor shift right if workforce not welltrained or educated employers will not hire from within that labor pool since they will need spend a significant amount time money training that workforce demand such will shift left technology technology changes can act as either substitutes or complements labor when technology acts as a substitute replaces need number workers an employer needs hire example word processing decreased number typists needed workplace this shifted demand curve typists left an increase availability certain technologies may increase demand labor technology that acts as a complement labor will increase demand certain types labor resulting a rightward shift demand curve example increased use word processing other software has increased demand information technology professionals who can resolve software hardware issues related a firms network more better technology will increase demand skilled workers who know how use technology enhance workplace productivity those workers who do not adapt changes technology will experience a decrease demand number companies an increase number companies producing a given product will increase demand labor resulting a shift right a decrease number companies producing a given product will decrease demand labor resulting a shift left government regulations complying government regulations can increase or decrease demand labor at any given wage healthcare industry government rules may require that nurses be hired carry out certain medical procedures this will increase demand nurses lesstrained healthcare workers would be prohibited from carrying out these procedures demand these workers will shift left price availability other inputs labor not only input into production process example a salesperson at a call center needs a telephone a computer terminal enter data record sales if prices other inputs fall production will become more profitable suppliers will demand more labor increase production this will cause a rightward shift demand curve labor opposite also true higher prices other inputs lower demand labor table 42 factors that can shift demand

shifts labor supply supply labor upwardsloping adheres law supply higher price greater quantity supplied lower price less quantity supplied supply curve models tradeoff between supplying labor into market or using time leisure activities at every given price level higher wage more labor willing work forego leisure activities table 43 lists some factors that will cause supply increase or decrease factors results number workers an increased number workers will cause supply curve shift right an increased number workers can be due several factors such as immigration increasing population an aging population changing demographics policies that encourage immigration will increase supply labor vice versa population grows when birth rates exceed death rates this eventually increases supply labor when former reach working age another example changing demographics more women working outside home which increases supply labor required education more required education lower supply there a lower supply phd mathematicians than high school mathematics teachers there a lower supply cardiologists than primary care physicians there a lower supply physicians than nurses government policies government policies can also affect supply labor jobs alternatively government may support rules that set high qualifications certain jobs academic training certificates or licenses or experience when these qualifications are made tougher number qualified workers will decrease at any given wage on other hand government may also subsidize training or even reduce required level qualifications example government might offer subsidies nursing schools or nursing students such provisions would shift supply curve nurses right addition government policies that change relative desirability working versus not working also affect labor supply these include unemployment benefits maternity leave child care benefits welfare policy example child care benefits may increase labor supply working mothers long term unemployment benefits may discourage job searching unemployed workers all these policies must therefore be carefully designed minimize any negative labor supply effects table 43 factors that can shift supply a change salary will lead a movement along labor demand or labor supply curves but will not shift those curves however other events like those we have outlined here will cause either demand or supply labor shift thus will move labor market a new equilibrium salary quantity

technology wage inequality fourstep process economic events can change equilibrium salary or wage quantity labor consider how wave new information technologies like computer telecommunications networks has affected lowskill highskill workers us economy from perspective employers who demand labor these new technologies are often a substitute lowskill laborers like file clerks who used keep file cabinets full paper records transactions however same new technologies are a complement highskill workers like managers who benefit from technological advances by having ability monitor more information communicate more easily juggle a wider array responsibilities how will new technologies affect wages highskill lowskill workers this question fourstep process analyzing how shifts supply or demand affect a market introduced demand supply works this waystep 1 what did markets lowskill labor highskill labor look like before arrival new technologies figure 43 a figure 43 b s0 original supply curve labor d0 original demand curve labor each market each graph original point equilibrium e0 occurs at price w0 quantity q0step 2 does new technology affect supply labor from households or demand labor from firms technology change described here affects demand labor by firms that hire workers step 3 will new technology increase or decrease demand based on description earlier as substitute lowskill labor becomes available demand lowskill labor will shift left from d0 d1 as technology complement highskill labor becomes cheaper demand highskill labor will shift right from d0 d1step 4 new equilibrium lowskill labor shown as point e1 price w1 quantity q1 has a lower wage quantity hired than original equilibrium e0 new equilibrium highskill labor shown as point e1 price w1 quantity q1 has a higher wage quantity hired than original equilibrium e0 thus demand supply model predicts that new computer communications technologies will raise pay highskill workers but reduce pay lowskill workers from 1970s mid2000s wage gap widened between highskill lowskill labor according national center education statistics 1980 example a college graduate earned about 30 more than a wage inequality across us economy due improvements technology

4-1-demand-and-supply-at-work-in-labor-m.question: price floors labor market living wages minimum wages contrast goods services markets price ceilings are rare labor markets because rules that prevent people from earning income are not politically popular there one exception boards trustees or stockholders as an example propose limits on high incomes top business executivesthe labor market however presents some prominent examples price floors which are an attempt increase wages lowpaid workers us government sets a minimum wage a price floor that makes illegal an employer pay employees less than a certain hourly rate mid2009 us minimum wage was raised 725 per hour local political movements a number us cities have pushed a higher minimum wage which they call a living wage promoters living wage laws maintain that minimum wage too low ensure a reasonable standard living they base this conclusion on calculation that if you work 40 hours a week at a minimum wage 725 per hour 50 weeks a year your annual income 14500 which less than official us government definition what means a family be poverty a family two adults earning minimum wage two young children will find more cost efficient one parent provide childcare while other works income thus family income would be 14500 which significantly lower than federal poverty line a family four which was 26500 2021supporters living wage argue that fulltime workers should be assured a high enough wage so that they can afford essentials life food clothing shelter healthcare since baltimore passed first living wage law 1994 several dozen cities enacted similar laws late 1990s 2000s living wage ordinances do not apply all employers but they have specified that all employees city or employees firms that city hires be paid at least a certain wage that usually a few dollars per hour above us minimum wagefigure 44 illustrates situation a city considering a living wage law simplicity we assume that there no federal minimum wage wage appears on vertical axis because wage price labor market before passage living wage law equilibrium wage 10 per hour city hires 1200 workers at this wage however a group concerned citizens persuades city council enact a living wage law requiring employers pay no less than 12 per hour response higher wage 1600 workers look jobs city at this higher wage city as an employer willing hire only 700 workers at price floor quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded a surplus labor exists this market workers who continue have a job at a higher salary life has improved those who were willing work at old wage rate but lost their jobs wage increase life has not improved table 44 shows differences supply demand at different wages wage quantity labor demanded quantity labor supplied 8hr 1900 500 9hr 1500 900 10hr 1200 1200 11hr 900 1400 12hr 700 1600 13hr 500 1800 14hr 400 1900 table 44 living wage example a price floor

will create an inefficient outcome however there an additional twist here along creating inefficiency price floors ceilings will also transfer some consumer surplus producers or some producer surplus consumersimagine that several firms develop a promising but expensive new drug treating back pain if this therapy left market equilibrium price will be 600 per month 20000 people will use drug as shown figure 324 a original level consumer surplus t u producer surplus v w x however government decides impose a price ceiling 400 make drug more affordable at this price ceiling firms market now produce only 15000 as a result two changes occur first an inefficient outcome occurs total surplus society reduced loss social surplus that occurs when economy produces at an inefficient quantity called deadweight loss a very real sense like money thrown away that benefits no one figure 324 a deadweight loss area u w when deadweight loss exists possible both consumer producer surplus be higher this case because price control blocking some suppliers demanders from transactions they would both be willing makea second change from price ceiling that some producer surplus transferred consumers after price ceiling imposed new consumer surplus t v while new producer surplus x other words price ceiling transfers area surplus v from producers consumers note that gain consumers less than loss producers which just another way seeing deadweight loss figure 324 b shows a price floor example using a string struggling movie theaters all same city current equilibrium 8 per movie ticket 1800 people attending movies original consumer surplus g h j producer surplus i k city government worried that movie theaters will go out business reducing entertainment options available citizens so decides impose a price floor 12 per ticket as a result quantity demanded movie tickets falls 1400 new consumer surplus g new producer surplus h i effect price floor causes area h be transferred from consumer producer surplus but also causes a deadweight loss j k this analysis shows that a price ceiling like a law establishing rent controls will transfer some producer surplus consumerswhich helps explain why consumers often favor them conversely a price floor like a guarantee that farmers will receive a certain price their crops will transfer some consumer surplus producers which explains why producers often favor them however both price floors price ceilings block some transactions that buyers sellers would have been willing make creates deadweight loss removing such barriers so that prices quantities can adjust their equilibrium level will increase economys social surplus

3-key-concepts-and-summary.question: 31 demand supply equilibrium markets goods services a demand schedule a table that shows quantity demanded at different prices market a demand curve shows relationship between quantity demanded price a given market on a graph law demand states that a higher price typically leads a lower quantity demanded a supply schedule a table that shows quantity supplied at different prices market a supply curve shows relationship between quantity supplied price on a graph law supply says that a higher price typically leads a higher quantity supplied equilibrium price equilibrium quantity occur where supply demand curves cross equilibrium occurs where quantity demanded equal quantity supplied if price below equilibrium level then quantity demanded will exceed quantity supplied excess demand or a shortage will exist if price above equilibrium level then quantity supplied will exceed quantity demanded excess supply or a surplus will exist either case economic pressures will push price toward equilibrium level 32 shifts demand supply goods services economists often use ceteris paribus or other things being equal assumption while examining economic impact one event all other factors remain unchanged analysis purposes factors that can shift demand curve goods services causing a different quantity be demanded at any given price include changes tastes population income prices substitute or complement goods expectations about future conditions prices factors that can shift supply curve goods services causing a different quantity be supplied at any given price include input prices natural conditions changes technology government taxes regulations or subsidies 33 changes equilibrium price quantity fourstep process when using supply demand framework think about how an event will affect equilibrium price quantity proceed through four steps 1 sketch a supply demand diagram think about what market looked like before event 2 decide whether event will affect supply or demand 3 decide whether effect on supply or demand negative or positive draw appropriate shifted supply or demand curve 4 compare new equilibrium price quantity original ones 34 price ceilings price floors price ceilings prevent a price from rising above a certain level when a price ceiling set below equilibrium price quantity demanded will exceed quantity supplied excess demand or shortages will result price floors prevent a price from falling below a certain level when a price floor set above equilibrium price quantity supplied will exceed quantity demanded excess supply or surpluses will result price floors price ceilings often lead unintended consequences 35 demand supply efficiency consumer surplus gap between price that consumers are willing pay based on their preferences market equilibrium price producer surplus gap between price which producers are willing sell a product based on their costs market equilibrium price social surplus sum consumer surplus producer surplus total surplus larger at equilibrium quantity price than will be at any other quantity price deadweight loss loss total surplus that occurs when economy produces at an inefficient quantity

rising above a certain level ceiling while a price floor keeps a price from falling below a given level floor this section uses demand supply framework analyze price ceilings next section discusses price floorsa price ceiling a legal maximum price that one pays some good or service a government imposes price ceilings order keep price some necessary good or service affordable example 2005 during hurricane katrina price bottled water increased above 5 per gallon as a result many people called price controls on bottled water prevent price from rising so high this particular case government did not impose a price ceiling but there are other examples where price ceilings did occurin many markets goods services demanders outnumber suppliers consumers who are also potential voters sometimes unite behind a political proposal hold down a certain price some cities such as albany renters have pressed political leaders pass rent control laws a price ceiling that usually works by stating that landlords can raise rents by only a certain maximum percentage each year some best examples rent control occur urban areas such as new york washington dc or san francisco rent control becomes a politically hot topic when rents begin rise rapidly everyone needs an affordable place live perhaps a change tastes makes a certain suburb or town a more popular place live perhaps locallybased businesses expand bringing higher incomes more people into area such changes can cause a change demand rental housing as figure 321 illustrates original equilibrium e0 lies at intersection supply curve s0 demand curve d0 corresponding an equilibrium price 500 an equilibrium quantity 15000 units rental housing effect greater income or a change tastes shift demand curve rental housing right as however underlying forces that shifted demand curve right are still there at that price 500 quantity supplied remains at same 15000 rental units but quantity demanded 19000 rental units other words out under price ceiling 15000 rental units than would be case at market rent 600 17000 rental unitsprice ceilings do not simply benefit renters at expense landlords rather some renters or potential housingprice ceilings are enacted an attempt keep prices low those who need product however when market price not allowed rise equilibrium level quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied thus a likely deteriorate

price floors a price floor lowest price that one can legally pay some good or service perhaps bestknown example a price floor minimum wage which based on view that someone working full time should be able afford a basic standard living federal minimum wage 2022 was 725 per hour although some states localities have a higher minimum wage federal minimum wage yields an annual income a single person 15080 which slightly higher than federal poverty line 11880 congress periodically raises federal minimum wage as cost living rises as march 2022 most recent adjustment occurred 2009 when federal minimum wage was raised from 655 725 price floors are sometimes called price supports because they support a price by preventing from falling below a certain level around world many countries have passed laws create agricultural price supports farm prices thus farm incomes fluctuate sometimes widely even if on average farm incomes are adequate some years they can be quite low purpose price supports prevent these swingsthe most common way price supports work that government enters market buys up product adding demand keep prices higher than they otherwise would be according common agricultural policy reform effective 2019 european union eu will spend about 58 billion euros per year or 655 billion dollars per year december 2021 exchange rate or roughly 36 eu budget on price supports europes farmersfigure 322 illustrates effects a government program that assures a price above equilibrium by focusing on market wheat europe absence government intervention price would adjust so that quantity supplied would equal quantity demanded at equilibrium point e0 price p0 quantity q0 however policies keep prices high farmers keep price above what would have been market equilibrium levelthe price pf shown by dashed horizontal line diagram result a quantity supplied excess quantity demanded qd when quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded a surplus exists economists estimate that highincome areas world including united states europe japan spend roughly 1 billion per day supporting their farmers if government willing purchase excess supply or provide payments others purchase then farmers will benefit from price floor but taxpayers consumers food will pay costs agricultural economists policy makers have offered numerous proposals reducing farm subsidies many countries however political support subsidies farmers remains strong this either because population views this as supporting traditional rural way life or because industrys lobbying power 3-2-shifts-in-demand-and-supply-for-good.question: what factors affect demand we defined demand as amount some product a consumer willing able purchase at each price that suggests at least two factors that affect demand willingness purchase suggests a desire based on what economists call tastes preferences if you neither need nor want something you will not buy if you really like something you will buy more than someone who does not share your strong preference ability purchase suggests that income important professors are usually able afford better housing transportation than students because they have more income prices related goods can affect demand also if you need a new car price a honda may affect your demand a ford finally size or composition population can affect demand more children a family has greater their demand clothing more drivingage children a family has greater their demand car insurance less diapers baby formulathese factors matter both individual market demand as a whole exactly how do these various factors affect demand how do we show effects graphically
3-2-shifts-in-demand-and-supply-for-good.answer: those questions we need ceteris paribus assumption

3-2-shifts-in-demand-and-supply-for-good.question: ceteris paribus assumption a demand curve or a supply curve a relationship between two only two variables quantity on horizontal axis price on vertical axis assumption behind a demand curve or a supply curve that no relevant economic factors other than products price are changing economists call this assumption ceteris paribus a latin phrase meaning other things being equal any given demand or supply curve based on ceteris paribus assumption that all else held equal a demand curve or a supply curve a relationship between two only two variables when all other variables are kept constant if all else not held equal then laws supply demand will not necessarily hold as following clear up feature shows

how does income affect demand lets use income as an example how factors other than price affect demand figure 35 shows initial demand automobiles as d0 at point q example if price 20000 per car quantity cars demanded 18 million d0 also shows how quantity cars demanded would change as a result a higher or lower price example if price a car rose 22000 quantity demanded would decrease 17 million at point rthe original demand curve d0 like every demand curve based on ceteris paribus assumption that no other economically relevant factors change now imagine that economy expands a way that raises incomes many people making cars more affordable how will this affect demand how can we show this graphically return figure 35 price cars still 20000 but higher incomes quantity demanded has now increased 20 million cars shown at point s as a result higher income levels demand curve shifts right new demand curve d1 indicating an increase demand table 34 shows clearly that this increased demand would occur at every price not just original one price decrease d2 original quantity demanded d0 increase d1 16000 176 million 220 million 240 million 18000 160 million 200 million 220 million 20000 144 million 180 million 200 million 22000 136 million 170 million 190 million 24000 132 million 165 million 185 million 26000 128 million 160 million 180 million table 34 price demand shifts a car example now imagine that economy slows down so that many people lose their jobs or work fewer hours reducing their incomes this case decrease income would lead a lower quantity cars demanded at every given price original demand curve d0 would shift left d2 shift from d0 d2 represents such a decrease demand at any given price level quantity demanded now lower this example a price 20000 means 18 million cars sold along original demand curve but only 144 million sold after demand fell when a demand curve shifts does not mean that quantity demanded by every individual buyer changes by same amount this example not everyone would have higher or lower income not everyone would buy or not buy an additional car instead a shift a demand curve captures a pattern market as a wholein previous section we argued that higher income causes greater demand at every price this true most goods services someluxury cars vacations europe fine jewelrythe effect a rise income can be especially pronounced a product whose demand rises when income rises vice versa called a normal good a few exceptions this pattern do exist as incomes rise many people will buy fewer generic brand groceries more name brand groceries they are less likely buy used cars more likely buy new cars they will be less likely rent an apartment more likely own a home a product whose demand falls when income rises vice versa called an inferior good other words when income increases demand curve shifts left

other factors that shift demand curves income not only factor that causes a shift demand other factors that change demand include tastes preferences composition or size population prices related goods even expectations a change any one underlying factors that determine what quantity people are willing buy at a given price will cause a shift demand graphically new demand curve lies either right an increase or left a decrease original demand curve lets look at these factorschanging tastes or preferences from 1980 2021 perperson consumption chicken by americans rose from 47 pounds per year 97 pounds per year consumption beef fell from 76 pounds per year 59 pounds per year according us department agriculture usda changes like these are largely due movements taste which change quantity a good demanded at every price that they shift demand curve that good rightward chicken leftward beefchanges composition population proportion elderly citizens united states population rising rose from 98 1970 126 2000 will be a projected by us census bureau 20 population by 2030 a society relatively more children like united states 1960s will have greater demand goods services like tricycles day care facilities a society relatively more elderly persons as united states projected have by 2030 has a higher demand nursing homes hearing aids similarly changes size population can affect demand housing many other goods each these changes demand will be shown as a shift demand curve changes prices related goods changes prices related goods such as substitutes or complements also can affect demand a product a substitute a good or service that we can use place another good or service as electronic books like this one become more available you would expect see a decrease demand traditional printed books a lower price a substitute decreases demand other product example recent years as price tablet computers has fallen quantity demanded has increased because law demand since people are purchasing tablets there has been a decrease demand laptops which we can show graphically as a leftward shift demand curve laptops a higher price a substitute good has reverse effectother goods are complements each other meaning we often use goods together because consumption one good tends enhance consumption other examples include breakfast cereal milk notebooks pens or pencils golf balls golf clubs gasoline sport utility vehicles fiveway combination bacon lettuce tomato mayonnaise bread if price golf clubs rises since quantity demanded golf clubs falls because law demand demand a complement good like golf balls decreases too similarly a higher price skis would shift demand curve a complement good like ski resort trips left while a lower price a complement has reverse effectchanges expectations about future prices or other factors that affect demand while clear that price a good affects quantity demanded also true that expectations about future price or expectations about tastes preferences income so on can affect demand example if people hear that a hurricane coming they may rush store buy flashlight batteries bottled water if people learn that price a good like coffee likely rise future they may head store stock up on coffee now we show these changes demand as shifts curve therefore a shift demand happens when a change some economic factor other than price causes a different quantity be demanded at every price following work out feature shows how this happens work out shift demand a shift demand means that at any price at every price quantity demanded will be different than was before following an example a shift demand due an income increase step 1 draw graph a demand curve a normal good like pizza pick a price like p0 identify corresponding q0 see an example figure 36step 2 suppose income increases as a result change are consumers going buy more or less pizza
3-2-shifts-in-demand-and-supply-for-good.answer: more draw a dotted horizontal line from chosen price through original quantity demanded new point new q1 draw a dotted vertical line down horizontal axis label new q1 figure 37 provides an example step 3 now shift curve through new point you will see that an increase income causes an upward or rightward shift demand curve so that at any price quantities demanded will be higher as figure 38 illustrates

3-2-shifts-in-demand-and-supply-for-good.question: how production costs affect supply a supply curve shows how quantity supplied will change as price rises falls assuming ceteris paribus so that no other economically relevant factors are changing if other factors relevant supply do change then entire supply curve will shift just as we described a shift demand as a change quantity demanded at every price a shift supply means a change quantity supplied at every pricein thinking about factors that affect supply remember what motivates firms profits which are difference between revenues costs a firm produces goods services using combinations labor materials machinery or what we call inputs or factors production if a firm faces lower costs production while prices good or service firm produces remain unchanged a firms profits go up when a firms profits increase more motivated produce output since more produces more profit will earn when costs production fall a firm will tend supply a larger quantity at any given price its output we can show this by supply curve shifting righttake example a messenger company that delivers packages around a city company may find that buying gasoline one its main costs if price gasoline falls then company will find can deliver messages more cheaply than before since lower costs correspond higher profits messenger company may now supply more its services at any given price example given lower gasoline prices company can now serve a greater area increase its supply conversely if a firm faces higher costs production then will earn lower profits at any given selling price its products as a result a higher cost production typically causes a firm supply a smaller quantity at any given price this case supply curve shifts left consider supply cars shown by curve s0 figure 310 point j indicates that if price 20000 quantity supplied will be 18 million cars if price rises 22000 per car ceteris paribus quantity supplied will rise 20 million cars as point k on s0 curve shows we can show same information table form as table 35 price decrease s1 original quantity supplied s0 increase s2 16000 105 million 120 million 132 million 18000 135 million 150 million 165 million 20000 165 million 180 million 198 million 22000 185 million 200 million 220 million 24000 195 million 210 million 231 million 26000 205 million 220 million 242 million table 35 price shifts supply a car example now imagine that price steel an important ingredient manufacturing cars rises so that producing a car has become more expensive at any given price selling cars car manufacturers will react by supplying a lower quantity we can show this graphically as a leftward shift supply from s0 s1 which indicates that at any given price quantity say that economy at natural rate unemployment or at full employmenteconomists benchmark actual or real gdp against potential gdp determine how well economy performing as explained economic growth we can explain gdp growth by increases investment physical capital human capital per person as well as advances technology physical capital per person refers amount kind machinery equipment available help people get work done compare example your productivity typing a term paper on a typewriter working on your laptop word processing software clearly you will be able be more productive using word processing software technology level capital your laptop software has increased your productivity more broadly development gps technology universal product codes those barcodes on every product we buy has made much easier firms track shipments tabulate inventories sell distribute products these two technological innovations many others have increased a nations ability produce goods services a given population likewise increasing human capital involves increasing levels knowledge education skill sets per person through vocational or higher education physical human capital improvements technological advances will increase overall productivity thus gdpto see how these improvements have increased productivity output at national level we should examine evidence from united states united states experienced significant growth twentieth century due phenomenal changes infrastructure equipment technological improvements physical capital human capital population more than tripled twentieth century from 76 million 1900 over 300 million 2016 human capital modern workers far higher today because education skills workers have risen dramatically 1900 only about oneeighth us population had completed high school just one person 40 had completed a fouryear college degree by 2010 about 85 americans age 25 or older had a high school degree about 28 had a fouryear college degree as well 2019 33 americans age 25 or older had a fouryear college degree average amount physical capital per worker has grown dramatically technology available modern workers extraordinarily better than a century ago cars airplanes electrical machinery smartphones computers chemical biological advances materials science health carethe list technological advances could run on on more workers higher skill levels larger amounts physical capital per worker amazingly better technology potential gdp us economy has clearly increased a great deal since 1900this growth has fallen below its potential gdp at times has exceeded its potential example from 2008 2009 us economy tumbled into recession remained below its potential until 2018 after pandemicinduced recession march april 2020 economy again fell below potential gdp remains there as early 2022 at other times like late 1990s or from 2018 2020 economy ran at potential gdpor even slightly ahead figure 262 shows actual data increase real gdp since 1960 slightly smoother line shows potential gdp since 1960 as estimated by nonpartisan congressional budget office most economic recessions upswings are times when economy 13 below or above potential gdp a given year clearly shortrun fluctuations around potential gdp do exist but over long run upward trend potential gdp determines size economyin aggregate demandaggregate supply model we show potential gdp as a vertical line neoclassical economists who focus on potential gdp as primary determinant real gdp argue that longrun aggregate supply curve located at potential gdpthat we draw longrun aggregate supply curve as a vertical line at level potential gdp as figure 263 shows a vertical lras curve means that level aggregate supply or potential gdp will determine economys real gdp regardless level aggregate demand over time increases quantity quality physical capital increases human capital technological advancements shift potential gdp vertical lras curve gradually right economists often describe this gradual increase an economys potential gdp as a nations longterm economic growth

26-1-the-building-blocks-of-neoclassical.question: role flexible prices how does macroeconomy adjust back its level potential gdp long run what if aggregate demand increases or decreases economists base neoclassical view how macroeconomy adjusts on insight that even if wages prices are sticky or slow change short run they are flexible over time understand this better lets follow connections from shortrun longrun macroeconomic equilibriumthe aggregate demand aggregate supply diagram figure 264 shows two aggregate supply curves we draw original upward sloping aggregate supply curve sras0 a shortrun or keynesian as curve vertical aggregate supply curve lrasn longrun or neoclassical as curve which located at potential gdp original aggregate demand curve labeled ad0 so that original equilibrium occurs at point e0 at which point economy producing at its potential gdpnow imagine that some economic event boosts aggregate demand perhaps a surge export sales or a rise business confidence that leads more investment perhaps a policy decision like higher government spending or perhaps a tax cut that leads additional aggregate demand shortrun keynesian analysis that rise aggregate demand will shift aggregate demand curve out right from ad0 ad1 leading a new equilibrium at point e1 higher output lower unemployment pressure an inflationary rise price level longrun neoclassical analysis however chain economic events just beginning as economic output rises above potential gdp level unemployment falls economy now above full employment there a labor shortage eager employers are trying bid workers away from other companies encourage their current workers exert more effort work longer hours this high demand labor will drive up wages most employers review their workers salaries only once or twice a year so will take time before higher wages filter through economy as wages do rise will mean a leftward shift shortrun keynesian aggregate supply curve back sras1 because price a major input production has increased economy moves a new equilibrium e2 new equilibrium has same level real gdp as did original equilibrium e0 but there has been an inflationary increase price levelthis description shortrun shift from e0 e1 longrun shift from e1 e2 a stepbystep way making a simple point economy cannot sustain production above its potential gdp long run an economy may produce above its level potential gdp short run under pressure from a surge aggregate demand over long run however that surge aggregate demand ends up as an increase price level not as a rise output rebound economy back potential gdp also works response a shift left aggregate demand figure 265 again starts two aggregate supply curves sras0 showing original upward sloping shortrun keynesian as curve lrasn showing vertical longrun neoclassical aggregate supply curve a decrease aggregate demandfor example because a decline consumer confidence that leads less consumption more savingcauses original aggregate demand curve ad0 shift back ad1 shift from original equilibrium e0 new equilibrium e1 results a decline output economy now below full employment there a surplus labor as output falls below potential gdp unemployment rises while a lower price level ie deflation rare united states does happen occasionally during very weak periods economic activity practical purposes we might consider a lower price level adas model as indicative disinflation which a decline inflation rate thus longrun aggregate supply curve lrasn which vertical at level potential gdp ultimately determines this economys real gdpagain from neoclassical perspective this shortrun scenario only beginning chain events higher level unemployment means more workers looking jobs as a result employers can hold down on pay increasesor perhaps even replace some their higherpaid workers unemployed people willing accept a lower wage as wages stagnate or fall this decline price a key input means that shortrun keynesian aggregate supply curve shifts right from its original sras0 sras1 overall impact long run as macroeconomic equilibrium shifts from e0 e1 e2 that level output returns potential gdp where started there however downward pressure on price level thus neoclassical view changes aggregate demand can have a shortrun impact on output on unemploymentbut only a shortrun impact long run when wages prices are flexible potential gdp aggregate supply determine real gdps size

how fast speed macroeconomic adjustment how long does take wages prices adjust economy rebound its potential gdp this subject highly contentious keynesian economists argue that if adjustment from recession potential gdp takes a very long time then neoclassical theory may be more hypothetical than practical response john maynard keynes immortal words long run we are all dead neoclassical economists respond that even if adjustment takes as long as say ten years neoclassical perspective remains central importance understanding economyone subset neoclassical economists holds that wage price adjustment macroeconomy might be quite rapid theory rational expectations holds that people form most accurate possible expectations about future that they can using all information available them an economy where most people have rational expectations economic adjustments may happen very quicklyto understand how rational expectations may affect speed price adjustments think about a situation real estate market imagine that several events seem likely push up home values neighborhood perhaps a local employer announces that plans hire many more people or city announces that will build a local park or a library that neighborhood theory rational expectations points out that even though none changes will happen immediately home prices neighborhood will rise immediately because expectation that homes will be worth more future will lead buyers be willing pay more present amount immediate increase home prices will depend on how likely seems that announcements about future will actually happen on how distant local jobs neighborhood improvements are future key point that because rational expectations prices do not wait on events but adjust immediatelyat a macroeconomic level theory rational expectations points out that if aggregate supply curve vertical over time then people should rationally expect this pattern when a shift aggregate demand occurs people businesses rational expectations will know that its impact on output employment will be temporary while its impact on price level will be permanent if firms workers perceive outcome process advance if all firms workers know that everyone else perceiving process same way then they have no incentive go through an extended series shortrun scenarios like a firm first hiring more people when aggregate demand shifts out then firing those same people when aggregate supply shifts back instead everyone will recognize where this process headingtoward a change price leveland then will act on that expectation this scenario expected longrun change price level may happen very quickly without a drawnout zigzag output employment first moving one way then other theory that people firms have rational expectations can be a useful simplification but as a statement about how people businesses actually behave assumption seems too strong after all many people firms are not especially well informed either about what happening economy or about how economy works an alternate assumption that people firms act adaptive expectations they look at past experience gradually adapt their beliefs behavior as circumstances change but are not perfect synthesizers information accurate predictors future sense rational expectations theory if most people businesses have some form adaptive expectations then adjustment from short run long run will be traced out incremental steps that occur over timethe empirical evidence on speed macroeconomic adjustment prices wages not clearcut speed macroeconomic adjustment probably varies among different countries time periods a reasonable guess that initial shortrun effect a shift aggregate demand might last two five years before adjustments wages prices cause economy adjust back potential gdp thus one might think short run applying keynesian analysis as time periods less than two five years long run applying neoclassical analysis as longer than five years practical purposes this guideline frustratingly imprecise but when analyzing a complex social mechanism like an economy as evolves over time some imprecision seems unavoidable

3-1-demand-supply-and-equilibrium-in-mar.question: demand goods services economists use term demand refer amount some good or service consumers are willing able purchase at each price demand fundamentally based on needs wantsif you have no need or want something you wont buy while a consumer may be able differentiate between a need a want from an economists perspective they are same thing demand also based on ability pay if you cannot pay you have no effective demand by this definition a person who does not have a drivers license has no effective demand a carwhat a buyer pays a unit specific good or service called price total number units that consumers would purchase at that price called quantity demanded a rise price a good or service almost always decreases quantity demanded that good or service conversely a fall price will increase quantity demanded when price a gallon gasoline increases example people look ways reduce their consumption by combining several errands commuting by carpool or mass transit or taking weekend or vacation trips closer home economists call this inverse relationship between price quantity demanded law demand law demand assumes that all other variables that affect demand which we explain next module are held constantwe can show an example from market gasoline a table or a graph economists call a table that shows quantity demanded at each price such as table 31 a demand schedule this case we measure price dollars per gallon gasoline we measure quantity demanded millions gallons over some time period example per day or per year over some geographic area like a state or a country a demand curve shows relationship between price quantity demanded on a graph like figure 32 quantity on horizontal axis price per gallon on vertical axis note that this an exception normal rule mathematics that independent variable x goes on horizontal axis dependent variable y goes on vertical axis while economists often use math they are different disciplinestable 31 shows demand schedule graph figure 32 shows demand curve these are two ways describe same relationship between price quantity demanded price per gallon quantity demanded millions gallons 100 800 120 700 140 600 160 550 180 500 200 460 220 420 table 31 price quantity demanded gasoline demand curves will appear somewhat different each product they may appear relatively steep or flat or they may be straight or curved nearly all demand curves share fundamental similarity that they slope down from left right demand curves embody law demand as price increases quantity demanded decreases conversely as price decreases quantity demanded increasesconfused about these different types demand read next clear up feature

supply goods services when economists talk about supply they mean amount some good or service a producer willing supply at each price price what producer receives selling one unit a good or service a rise price almost always leads an increase quantity supplied that good or service while a fall price will decrease quantity supplied when price gasoline rises example encourages profitseeking firms take several actions expand exploration oil reserves drill more oil invest more pipelines oil tankers bring oil plants refining into gasoline build new oil refineries purchase additional pipelines trucks ship gasoline gas stations open more gas stations or keep existing gas stations open longer hours economists call this positive relationship between price quantity suppliedthat a higher price leads a higher quantity supplied a lower price leads a lower quantity suppliedthe law supply law supply assumes that all other variables that affect supply be explained next module are held constantstill unsure about different types supply see following clear up feature figure 33 illustrates law supply again using market gasoline as an example like demand we can illustrate supply using a table or a graph a supply schedule a table like table 32 that shows quantity supplied at a range different prices again we measure price dollars per gallon gasoline we measure quantity supplied millions gallons a supply curve a graphic illustration relationship between price shown on vertical axis quantity shown on horizontal axis supply schedule supply curve are just two different ways showing same information notice that horizontal vertical axes on graph supply curve are same as demand curve price per gallon quantity supplied millions gallons 100 500 120 550 140 600 160 640 180 680 200 700 220 720 table 32 price supply gasoline shape supply curves will vary somewhat according product steeper flatter straighter or curved nearly all supply curves however share a basic similarity they slope up from left right illustrate law supply as price rises say from 100 per gallon 220 per gallon quantity supplied increases from 500 gallons 720 gallons conversely as price falls quantity supplied decreases

equilibriumwhere demand supply intersect because graphs demand supply curves both have price on vertical axis quantity on horizontal axis demand curve supply curve a particular good or service can appear on same graph together demand supply determine price quantity that will be bought sold a market figure 34 illustrates interaction demand supply market gasoline demand curve d identical figure 32 supply curve s identical figure 33 table 33 contains same information tabular form price per gallon quantity demanded millions gallons quantity supplied millions gallons 100 800 500 120 700 550 140 600 600 160 550 640 180 500 680 200 460 700 220 420 720 table 33 price quantity demanded quantity supplied remember this when two lines on a diagram cross this intersection usually means something point where supply curve s demand curve d cross designated by point e figure 34 called equilibrium equilibrium price only price where plans consumers plans producers agreethat where amount product consumers want buy quantity demanded equal amount producers want sell quantity supplied economists call this common quantity equilibrium quantity at any other price quantity demanded does not equal quantity supplied so market not equilibrium at that pricein figure 34 equilibrium price 140 per gallon gasoline equilibrium quantity 600 million gallons if you had only demand supply schedules not graph you could find equilibrium by looking price level on tables where quantity demanded quantity supplied are equal word equilibrium means balance if a market at its equilibrium price quantity then has no reason move away from that point however if a market not at equilibrium then economic pressures arise move market toward equilibrium price equilibrium quantity imagine example that price a gallon gasoline was above equilibrium pricethat instead 140 per gallon price 180 per gallon dashed horizontal line at price 180 figure 34 illustrates this aboveequilibrium price at this higher price quantity demanded drops from 600 500 this decline quantity reflects how consumers react higher price by finding ways use less gasolinemoreover at this higher price 180 quantity gasoline supplied rises from 600 680 as higher price makes more profitable gasoline producers expand their output now consider how quantity demanded quantity supplied are related at this aboveequilibrium price quantity demanded has fallen 500 gallons while quantity supplied has risen 680 gallons fact at any aboveequilibrium price quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded we call this an excess supply or a surplus a surplus gasoline accumulates at gas stations tanker trucks pipelines at oil refineries this accumulation puts pressure on gasoline sellers if a surplus remains unsold those firms involved making selling gasoline are not receiving enough cash pay their workers cover their expenses this situation some producers sellers will want cut prices because better sell at a lower price than not sell at all once some sellers start cutting prices others will follow avoid losing sales these price reductions turn will stimulate a higher quantity demanded therefore if price above equilibrium level incentives built into structure demand supply will create pressures price fall toward equilibriumnow suppose that price below its equilibrium level at 120 per gallon as dashed horizontal line at this price figure 34 shows at this lower price quantity demanded increases from 600 700 as drivers take longer trips spend more minutes warming up car driveway wintertime stop sharing rides work buy larger cars that get fewer miles gallon however belowequilibrium price reduces gasoline producers incentives produce sell gasoline quantity supplied falls from 600 550 when price below equilibrium there excess demand or a shortagethat at given price quantity demanded which has been stimulated by lower price now exceeds quantity supplied which has been depressed by lower price this situation eager gasoline buyers mob gas stations only find many stations running short fuel oil companies gas stations recognize that they have an opportunity make higher profits by selling what gasoline they have at a higher price as a result price rises toward equilibrium level read demand supply efficiency more discussion on importance demand supply model
