25-key-concepts-and-summary.question: 251 aggregate demand keynesian analysis aggregate demand sum four components consumption investment government spending net exports consumption will change a number reasons including movements income taxes expectations about future income changes wealth levels investment will change response its expected profitability which turn shaped by expectations about future economic growth creation new technologies price key inputs tax incentives investment investment will also change when interest rates rise or fall political considerations determine government spending taxes exports imports change according relative growth rates prices between two economies 252 building blocks keynesian analysis keynesian economics based on two main ideas 1 aggregate demand more likely than aggregate supply be primary cause a shortrun economic event like a recession 2 wages prices can be sticky so an economic downturn unemployment can result latter an example a macroeconomic externality while surpluses cause prices fall at micro level they do not necessarily at macro level instead adjustment a decrease demand occurs only through decreased quantities one reason why prices may be sticky menu costs costs changing prices these include internal costs a business faces changing prices terms labeling recordkeeping accounting also costs communicating price change possibly unhappy customers keynesians also believe existence expenditure multiplierthe notion that a change autonomous expenditure causes a more than proportionate change gdp 253 phillips curve a phillips curve shows tradeoff between unemployment inflation an economy from a keynesian viewpoint phillips curve should slope down so that higher unemployment means lower inflation vice versa however a downwardsloping phillips curve a shortterm relationship that may shift after a few years keynesian macroeconomics argues that solution a recession expansionary fiscal policy such as tax cuts stimulate consumption investment or direct increases government spending that would shift aggregate demand curve right other side keynesian policy occurs when economy operating above potential gdp this situation unemployment low but inflationary rises price level are a concern keynesian response would be contractionary fiscal policy using tax increases or government spending cuts shift ad left 254 keynesian perspective on market forces keynesian prescription stabilizing economy implies government intervention at macroeconomic levelincreasing aggregate demand when private demand falls decreasing aggregate demand when private demand rises this does not imply that government should be passing laws or regulations that set prices quantities microeconomic markets

25-key-terms.question: contractionary fiscal policy tax increases or cuts government spending designed decrease aggregate demand reduce inflationary pressures coordination argument downward wage price flexibility requires perfect information about level lower compensation acceptable other laborers market participants disposable income income after taxes expansionary fiscal policy tax cuts or increases government spending designed stimulate aggregate demand move economy out recession expenditure multiplier keynesian concept that asserts that a change autonomous spending causes a more than proportionate change real gdp inflationary gap equilibrium at a level output above potential gdp macroeconomic externality occurs when what happens at macro level different from what happens at micro level an example would be where because coordination argument upward sloping supply curves firms become a flat aggregate supply curve illustrating that price level cannot fall stimulate aggregate demand menu costs costs firms face changing prices phillips curve tradeoff between unemployment inflation real gdp amount goods services actually sold a nation recessionary gap equilibrium at a level output below potential gdp sticky wages prices a situation where wages prices do not fall response a decrease demand or do not rise response an increase demand

25-4-the-keynesian-perspective-on-market.question: bring home pandemicinduced recession keynesian perspective pandemicinduced recession 2020 was unique unlike great recession 20072009 discussed most this chapter was not started by burst a housing bubble was started by a virus that caused sickness death millions people worldwide required substantial social policy order control its spread some ways latest recession was influenced by fluctuations aggregate demand at its depth april 2020 over 20 million people were unemployed causing a massive decline consumption aggregate demand as businesses were forced shutter or move their operations online many were pessimistic or uncertain about future state economy causing investment decline federal government attempted correct this aggregate demand shock through small business loans direct aid state local governments expanded unemployment insurance stimulus checks as a result all these measures economy was able bounce back somewhat over remainder 2020 however even at start 2022 millions people remained out work as new variants threatened upend economy once again keynesian perspective would have economic policy continue focus on aggregate restoring confidence economy president bidens proposals largely reflect these goals but millions workers still out labor market a virus that still not contained remains be seen whether those policy prescriptions will be enough medicine bring economy back normalcy

25-3-the-phillips-curve.question: discovery phillips curve 1950s aw phillips an economist at london school economics was studying keynesian analytical framework keynesian theory implied that during a recession inflationary pressures are low but when level output at or even pushing beyond potential gdp economy at greater risk inflation phillips analyzed 60 years british data did find that tradeoff between unemployment inflation which became known as phillips curve figure 258 shows a theoretical phillips curve following work out feature shows how pattern appears united states work out phillips curve united states step 1 go this website see 2005 economic report presidentstep 2 scroll down locate table b63 appendices this table titled changes special consumer price indexes 19602004 step 3 download table excel by selecting xls option then selecting location which save file step 4 open downloaded excel file step 5 view third column labeled year year this inflation rate measured by percentage change consumer price index step 6 return website scroll locate appendix table b42 civilian unemployment rate 19592004 step 7 download table excel step 8 open downloaded excel file view second column this overall unemployment rate step 9 using data available from these two tables plot phillips curve 196069 unemployment rate on xaxis inflation rate on yaxis your graph should look like figure 259 step 10 plot phillips curve 19601979 what does graph look like do you still see tradeoff between inflation unemployment your graph should look like figure 2510 over this longer period time phillips curve appears have shifted out there no tradeoff any more
25-3-the-phillips-curve.section_name: The Discovery of the Phillips Curve

25-3-the-phillips-curve.question: instability phillips curve during 1960s economists viewed phillips curve as a policy menu a nation could choose low inflation high unemployment or high inflation low unemployment or anywhere between economies could use fiscal monetary policy move up or down phillips curve as desired then a curious thing happened when policymakers tried exploit tradeoff between inflation unemployment result was an increase both inflation unemployment what had happened phillips curve shiftedthe us economy experienced this pattern deep recession from 1973 1975 again backtoback recessions from 1980 1982 many nations around world saw similar increases unemployment inflation this pattern became known as stagflation recall from aggregate demandaggregate supply model that stagflation an unhealthy combination high unemployment high inflation perhaps most important stagflation was a phenomenon that traditional keynesian economics could not explaineconomists have concluded that two factors cause phillips curve shift first supply shocks like mid1970s oil crisis which first brought stagflation into our vocabulary second changes peoples expectations about inflation other words there may be a tradeoff between inflation unemployment when people expect no inflation but when they realize inflation occurring tradeoff disappears both factors supply shocks changes inflationary expectations cause aggregate supply curve thus phillips curve shiftin short we should interpret a downwardsloping phillips curve as valid shortrun periods several years but over longer periods when aggregate supply shifts downwardsloping phillips curve can shift so that unemployment inflation are both higher as 1970s early 1980s or both lower as early 1990s or first decade 2000s
25-3-the-phillips-curve.section_name: The Instability of the Phillips Curve

25-3-the-phillips-curve.question: keynesian policy fighting unemployment inflation keynesian macroeconomics argues that solution a recession expansionary fiscal policy such as tax cuts stimulate consumption investment or direct increases government spending that would shift aggregate demand curve right example if aggregate demand was originally at adr figure 2511 so that economy was recession appropriate policy would be government shift aggregate demand right from adr adf where economy would be at potential gdp full employment keynes noted that while would be nice if government could spend additional money on housing roads other amenities he also argued that if government could not agree on how spend money practical ways then could spend impractical ways example keynes suggested building monuments like a modern equivalent egyptian pyramids he proposed that government could bury money underground let mining companies start digging up money again these suggestions were slightly tongueincheek but their purpose was emphasize that a great depression no time quibble over specifics government spending programs tax cuts when goal should be pump up aggregate demand by enough lift economy potential gdpthe other side keynesian policy occurs when economy operating above potential gdp this situation unemployment low but inflationary rises price level are a concern keynesian response would be contractionary fiscal policy using tax increases or government spending cuts shift ad left result would be downward pressure on price level but very little reduction output or very little rise unemployment if aggregate demand was originally at adi figure 2511 so that economy was experiencing inflationary rises price level appropriate policy would be government shift aggregate demand left from adi toward adf which reduces pressure a higher price level while economy remains at full employment keynesian economic model too little aggregate demand brings unemployment too much brings inflation thus you can think keynesian economics as pursuing a goldilocks level aggregate demand not too much not too little but looking what just right
25-3-the-phillips-curve.section_name: Keynesian Policy for Fighting Unemployment and Inflation

25-self-check-questions.question: question 6 does keynesian economics require government set controls on prices wages or interest rates
25-self-check-questions.answer: 6 keynesian economics does not require microeconomic price controls any sort true that many keynesian economic prescriptions were government influence total amount aggregate demand economy often through government spending tax cuts

25-self-check-questions.question: question 2 a keynesian framework using an adas diagram which following government policy choices offer a possible solution recession which offer a possible solution inflation a tax increase on consumer income a surge military spending a reduction taxes businesses that increase investment a major increase what us government spends on healthcare
25-self-check-questions.answer: 2 a tax increase on consumer income will cause consumption fall pushing ad curve left a possible solution inflation a surge military spending an increase government spending this will cause ad curve shift right if real gdp less than potential gdp then this spending would pull economy out a recession if real gdp right potential gdp then ad curve will shift farther right military spending will be inflationary a tax cut focused on business investment will shift ad right if original macroeconomic equilibrium below potential gdp then this policy can help move an economy out a recession government spending on healthcare will cause ad curve shift right if real gdp less than potential gdp then this spending would pull economy out a recession if real gdp th right potential gdp then ad curve will shift farther right healthcare spending will be inflationary

3-self-check-questions.question: question 11 if a price floor benefits producers why does a price floor reduce social surplus
3-self-check-questions.answer: 11 because losses consumers are greater than benefits producers so net effect negative since lost consumer surplus greater than additional producer surplus social surplus falls

3-self-check-questions.question: question 9 what would be impact imposing a price floor below equilibrium price
3-self-check-questions.answer: 9 a price ceiling a legal maximum price but a price floor a legal minimum price consequently would leave room price rise its equilibrium level other words a price floor below equilibrium will not be binding will have no effect

3-self-check-questions.question: question 7 what effect a price ceiling on quantity demanded product what effect a price ceiling on quantity supplied why exactly does a price ceiling cause a shortage
3-self-check-questions.answer: 7 a price ceiling which below equilibrium price will cause quantity demanded rise quantity supplied fall this why a price ceiling creates a shortage

3-self-check-questions.question: question 8 does a price ceiling change equilibrium price
3-self-check-questions.answer: 8 a price ceiling just a legal restriction equilibrium an economic condition people may or may not obey price ceiling so actual price may be at or above price ceiling but price ceiling does not change equilibrium price

3-self-check-questions.answer: 4 more fuelefficient cars means there less need gasoline this causes a leftward shift demand gasoline thus oil since demand curve shifting down supply curve equilibrium price quantity both fall cold weather increases need heating oil this causes a rightward shift demand heating oil thus oil since demand curve shifting up supply curve equilibrium price quantity both rise a discovery new oil will make oil more abundant this can be shown as a rightward shift supply curve which will cause a decrease equilibrium price along an increase equilibrium quantity supply curve shifts down demand curve so price quantity follow law demand if price goes down then quantity goes up when an economy slows down produces less output demands less input including energy which used production virtually everything a decrease demand energy will be reflected as a decrease demand oil or a leftward shift demand oil since demand curve shifting down supply curve both equilibrium price quantity oil will fall disruption oil pumping will reduce supply oil this leftward shift supply curve will show a movement up demand curve resulting an increase equilibrium price oil a decrease equilibrium quantity increased insulation will decrease demand heating this leftward shift demand oil causes a movement down supply curve resulting a decrease equilibrium price quantity oil solar energy a substitute oilbased energy so if solar energy becomes cheaper demand oil will decrease as consumers switch from oil solar decrease demand oil will be shown as a leftward shift demand curve as demand curve shifts down supply curve both equilibrium price quantity oil will fall a new popular kind plastic will increase demand oil increase demand will be shown as a rightward shift demand raising equilibrium price quantity oil

3-self-check-questions.question: question 6 a tariff a tax on imported goods suppose us government cuts tariff on imported flat screen televisions using fourstep analysis how do you think tariff reduction will affect equilibrium price quantity flat screen tvs
3-self-check-questions.answer: 6 step 1 draw graph initial supply demand curves label initial equilibrium price quantity step 2 did economic event affect supply or demand a tariff treated like a cost production so this affects supply step 3 a tariff reduction equivalent a decrease cost production which we can show as a rightward or downward shift supply step 4 a rightward shift supply causes a movement down demand curve lowering equilibrium price raising equilibrium quantity

3-self-check-questions.question: question 3 an analysis market paint an economist discovers facts listed below state whether each these changes will affect supply or demand what direction there have recently been some important costsaving inventions technology making paint paint lasting longer so that property owners need not repaint as often because severe hailstorms many people need repaint now hailstorms damaged several factories that make paint forcing them close down several months
3-self-check-questions.answer: 3 an improvement technology that reduces cost production will cause an increase supply alternatively you can think this as a reduction price necessary firms supply any quantity either way this can be shown as a rightward or downward shift supply curve an improvement product quality treated as an increase tastes or preferences meaning consumers demand more paint at any price level so demand increases or shifts right if this seems counterintuitive note that demand future longerlasting paint will fall since consumers are essentially shifting demand from future present an increase need causes an increase demand or a rightward shift demand curve factory damage means that firms are unable supply as much present technically this an increase cost production either way you look at supply curve shifts left

3-self-check-questions.answer: 5 step 1 draw graph initial supply demand curves label initial equilibrium price quantity step 2 did economic event affect supply or demand jet fuel a cost producing air travel so an increase jet fuel price affects supply step 3 an increase price jet fuel caused an increase cost air travel we show this as an upward or leftward shift supplystep 4 a leftward shift supply causes a movement up demand curve increasing equilibrium price air travel decreasing equilibrium quantity

3-self-check-questions.question: question 10 does a price ceiling increase or decrease number transactions a market why what about a price floor
3-self-check-questions.answer: 10 assuming that people obey price ceiling market price will be below equilibrium which means that qd will be more than qs buyers can only buy what offered sale so number transactions will fall qs this easy see graphically by analogous reasoning a price floor market price will be above equilibrium price so qd will be less than qs since limit on transactions here demand number transactions will fall qd note that because both price floors price ceilings reduce number transactions social surplus less

3-self-check-questions.answer: 2 make easier analyze complex problems ceteris paribus allows you look at effect one factor at a time on what you are trying analyze when you have analyzed all factors individually you add results together get final

2-self-check-questions.answer: 4 both budget constraint ppf show constraint that each operates under both show a tradeoff between having more one good but less other both show opportunity cost graphically as slope constraint budget or ppf

2-self-check-questions.question: question 2 return example figure 24 suppose there an improvement medical technology that enables more healthcare same amount resources how would this affect production possibilities curve particular how would affect opportunity cost education
2-self-check-questions.answer: 2 because improvement technology vertical intercept ppf would be at a higher level healthcare other words ppf would rotate clockwise around horizontal intercept this would make ppf steeper corresponding an increase opportunity cost education since resources devoted education would now mean forgoing a greater quantity healthcare

25-2-the-building-blocks-of-keynesian-an.question: wage price stickinesskeynes also pointed out that although ad fluctuated prices wages did not immediately respond as economists often expected instead prices wages are sticky making difficult restore economy full employment potential gdp keynes emphasized one particular reason why wages were sticky coordination argument this argument points out that even if most people would be willingat least hypotheticallyto see a decline their own wages bad economic times as long as everyone else also experienced such a decline a marketoriented economy has no obvious way implement a plan coordinated wage reductions unemployment proposed a number reasons why wages might be sticky downward most which center on argument that businesses avoid wage cuts because they may one way or another depress morale hurt productivity existing workerssome modern economists have argued a keynesian spirit that along wages other prices may be sticky too many firms do not change their prices every day or even every month when a firm considers changing prices must consider two sets costs first changing prices uses company resources managers must analyze competition market demand decide new prices they must update sales materials change billing records redo product price labels second frequent price changes may leave customers confused or angryespecially if they discover that a product now costs more than they expected these costs changing prices are called menu costslike costs printing a new set menus different prices a restaurant prices do respond forces supply demand but from a macroeconomic perspective process changing all prices throughout economy takes timeto understand effect sticky wages prices economy consider figure 254 a illustrating overall labor market while figure 254 b illustrates a market a specific good or service original equilibrium e0 each market occurs at intersection demand curve d0 supply curve s0 when aggregate demand declines demand labor shifts left d1 figure 254 a demand goods shifts left d1 figure 254 b however because sticky wages prices wage remains at its original level w0 a period time price remains at its original level p0as a result a situation excess supplywhere quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded at existing wage or priceexists markets both labor goods q1 less than q0 both figure 254 a figure 254 b when many labor markets many goods markets all across economy find themselves this position economy a recession that firms cannot sell what they wish produce at existing market price do not wish hire all who are willing work at existing market wage clear up feature discusses this problem more detail

24-1-macroeconomic-perspectives-on-deman.question: keynes law macroeconomics demand alternative says law its emphasis on supply keynes law demand creates its own supply as a matter historical accuracy just as jeanbaptiste say never wrote down anything as simpleminded as says law john maynard keynes never wrote down keynes law but law a useful simplification that conveys a certain point viewwhen keynes wrote his influential work general theory employment interest money during 1930s great depression he pointed out that during depression economys capacity supply goods services had not changed much us unemployment rates soared higher than 20 from 1933 1935 but number possible workers had not increased or decreased much factories closed but machinery equipment had not disappeared technologies that had been invented 1920s were not uninvented forgotten 1930s thus keynes argued that great depressionand many ordinary recessions as wellwere not caused by a drop ability economy supply goods as measured by labor physical capital or technology he argued economy often produced less than its full potential not because was technically impossible produce more existing workers machines but because a lack demand economy as a whole led inadequate incentives firms produce such cases he argued level gdp economy was not primarily determined by potential what economy could supply but rather by amount total demandkeynes law seems apply fairly well short run a few months a few years when many firms experience either a drop demand their output during a recession or so much demand that they have trouble producing enough during an economic boom however demand cannot tell whole macroeconomic story either after all if demand was all that mattered at macroeconomic level then government could make economy as large as wanted just by pumping up total demand through a large increase government spending component or by legislating large tax cuts push up consumption component economies do however face genuine limits how much they can produce limits determined by quantity labor physical capital technology institutional market structures that bring these factors production together these constraints on what an economy can supply at macroeconomic level do not disappear just because an increase demand

firms tend spend more on investment believing that future payoff from that investment will be substantial conversely if consumer or business confidence drops then consumption investment spending decline university michigan publishes a survey consumer confidence constructs an index consumer confidence each month survey results are then reported at httpwwwscaisrumichedu which break down change consumer confidence among different income levels according that index consumer confidence averaged around 90 prior great recession then fell below 60 late 2008 which was lowest had been since 1980 during 2010s confidence has climbed from a 2011 low 558 back a level upper 90s before falling lower 70s 2020 due coivd19 pandemic which economists consider close a healthy statethe organization economic development cooperation oecd publishes one measure business confidence business tendency surveys oecd collects business opinion survey data 21 countries on future selling prices employment among other business climate elements after sharply declining during great recession measure has risen above zero again back longterm averages indicator dips below zero when business outlook weaker than usual course either these survey measures not very precise they can however suggest when confidence rising or falling as well as when relatively high or low compared past because economists associate a rise confidence higher consumption investment demand will lead an outward shift ad curve a move equilibrium from e0 e1 a higher quantity output a higher price level as figure 248 a showsconsumer business confidence often reflect macroeconomic realities example confidence usually high when economy growing briskly low during a recession however economic confidence can sometimes rise or fall reasons that do not have a close connection immediate economy like a risk war election results foreign policy events or a pessimistic prediction about future by a prominent public figure us presidents example must be careful their public pronouncements about economy if they offer economic pessimism they risk provoking a decline confidence that reduces consumption investment shifts ad left a selffulfilling prophecy contributes causing recession that president warned against first place figure 248 b shows a shift ad left corresponding movement equilibrium from e0 e1 a lower quantity output a lower price level

figure 248 a while lower government spending will cause ad shift left as figure 248 b example united states government spending declined by 32 gdp during 1990s from 21 gdp 1991 178 gdp 1998 however from 2005 2009 peak great recession government spending increased from 19 gdp 214 gdp if changes a few percentage points gdp seem small you remember that since gdp was about 144 trillion 2009 a seemingly small change 2 gdp equal close 300 billion since 2009 government expenditures have gone back down around 1718 gdp although 2020 they rose 185tax policy can affect consumption investment spending too tax cuts individuals will tend increase consumption demand while tax increases will tend diminish tax policy can also pump up investment demand by offering lower tax rates corporations or tax reductions that benefit specific kinds investment shifting c or i will shift ad curve as a whole during a recession when unemployment high many businesses are suffering low profits or even losses us congress often passes tax cuts during 2001 recession example us congress enacted a tax cut into law at such times political rhetoric often focuses on how people experiencing hard times need relief from taxes aggregate supply aggregate demand framework however offers a complementary rationale as figure 249 illustrates original equilibrium during a recession at point e0 relatively far from full employment level output tax cut by increasing consumption shifts ad curve right at new equilibrium e1 real gdp rises unemployment falls because this diagram economy has not yet reached its potential or full employment level gdp any rise price level remains muted read following clear up feature consider question whether economists favor tax cuts or oppose them government spending tax rate changes can be useful tools affect aggregate demand we will discuss these greater detail government budgets fiscal policy chapter impacts government borrowing other policy tools can shift aggregate demand curve as well example as we will discuss monetary policy bank regulation chapter federal reserve can affect interest rates credit availability higher interest rates tend discourage borrowing thus reduce both household spending on bigticket items like houses cars investment spending by business conversely lower interest rates will stimulate consumption investment demand interest rates can also affect exchange rates which turn will have effects on export import components aggregate demandclarifying details these alternative policies how they affect components aggregate demand can wait 23-3-trade-balances-and-flows-of-financi.question: a twoperson economy robinson crusoe friday understand how economists view trade deficits surpluses consider a parable based on story robinson crusoe crusoe as you may remember from classic novel by daniel defoe first published 1719 was shipwrecked on a desert island after living alone some time he joined by a second person whom he names friday think about balance trade a twoperson economy like that robinson friday robinson friday trade goods services perhaps robinson catches fish trades them friday coconuts or friday weaves a hat out tree fronds trades robinson help carrying water a period time each individual trade selfcontained complete because each trade voluntary both robinson friday must feel that they are receiving fair value what they are giving as a result each persons exports are always equal his imports trade always balance between two neither person experiences either a trade deficit or a trade surplushowever one day robinson approaches friday a proposition robinson wants dig ditches an irrigation system his garden but he knows that if he starts this project he will not have much time left fish gather coconuts feed himself each day he proposes that friday supply him a certain number fish coconuts several months then after that time he promises repay friday out extra produce that he will be able grow his irrigated garden if friday accepts this offer then a trade imbalance comes into being several months friday will have a trade surplus that he exporting robinson more than he importing more precisely he giving robinson fish coconuts at least moment he receiving nothing return conversely robinson will have a trade deficit because he importing more from friday than he exporting this parable raises several useful issues thinking about what a trade deficit a trade surplus really mean economic terms first issue that this story robinson friday raises this better have a trade surplus or a trade deficit
23-3-trade-balances-and-flows-of-financi.answer: as any voluntary market interaction that if both parties agree transaction then they may both be better off over time if robinsons irrigated garden a success certainly possible that both robinson friday can benefit from this agreementthe parable raises a second issue what can go wrong robinsons proposal friday introduces an element uncertainty friday effect making a loan fish coconuts robinson fridays happiness this arrangement will depend on whether robinson repays that loan as planned full on time perhaps robinson spends several months loafing never builds irrigation system or perhaps robinson has been too optimistic about how much he will be able grow new irrigation system which turns out not be very productive perhaps after building irrigation system robinson decides that he does not want repay friday as much as he previously agreed any these developments will prompt a new round negotiations between friday robinson why repayment failed likely shape fridays attitude toward these renegotiations if robinson worked very hard irrigation system just did not increase production as intended friday may have some sympathy if robinson loafed or if he just refuses pay friday may become irritateda third issue that parable raises that an intimate relationship exists between a trade deficit international borrowing between a trade surplus international lending size fridays trade surplus exactly how much he lending robinson size robinsons trade deficit exactly how much he borrowing from friday economists a trade surplus literally means same thing as an outflow financial capital a trade deficit literally means same thing as an inflow financial capital this last insight worth exploring greater detail which we will do 23-4-the-national-saving-and-investment-.question: shortterm movements business cycle trade balance short run whether an economy a recession or on upswing can affect trade imbalances a recession tends make a trade deficit smaller or a trade surplus larger while a period strong economic growth tends make a trade deficit larger or a trade surplus smaller these are not hardandfast rules howeveras an example note figure 232 that us trade deficit declined by almost half from 2006 2009 one primary reason this change that during recession as us economy slowed down purchased fewer all goods including fewer imports from abroad however buying power abroad fell less so us exports did not fall by as much on other hand during 2020 pandemicinduced recession trade deficit expanded current account balance plummeted as us economy became more reliant on other countries goods services conversely mid2000s when us trade deficit became very large a contributing shortterm reason that us economy was growing as a result there was considerable aggressive buying us economy including buying imports thus a trade deficit or a much lower trade surplus often accompanies a rapidly growing domestic economy while a trade surplus or a much lower trade deficit accompanies a slowing or recessionary domestic economyregardless whether trade deficit falls or rises during expansions or downturns when trade deficit rises necessarily means a greater net inflow foreign financial capital national saving investment identity teaches that rest economy can absorb this inflow foreign financial capital several different ways example reduced private savings could offset additional inflow financial capital from abroad leaving domestic investment public saving unchanged alternatively inflow foreign financial capital could result higher domestic investment leaving private public saving unchanged yet another possibility that greater government borrowing could absorb inflow foreign financial capital leaving domestic saving investment unchanged national saving investment identity does not specify which these scenarios alone or combination will occuronly that one them must occur

22-3-how-the-u-s-and-other-countries-exp.question: historical inflation us economy figure 223 a shows level prices consumer price index stretching back 1913 this case base years when cpi defined as 100 are set average level prices that existed from 1982 1984 figure 223 b shows annual percentage changes cpi over time which inflation rate access multimedia content inflation as measured by consumer price index reflects annual percentage change cost average consumer acquiring a basket goods services that may be fixed or changed at specified intervals such as yearly first two waves inflation are easy characterize historical terms they are right after world war i world war ii however there are also two periods severe negative inflationcalled deflationin early decades twentieth century one following deep 192021 recession other during great depression 1930s since inflation a time when buying power money terms goods services reduced deflation will be a time when buying power money terms goods services increases period from 1900 about 1960 major inflations deflations nearly balanced each other out so average annual rate inflation over these years was only about 1 per year a third wave more severe inflation arrived 1970s departed early 1980stimes recession or depression often seem be times when inflation rate lower as recession 19201921 great depression recession 19801982 great recession 20082009 there were a few months 2009 that were deflationary but not at an annual rate high levels unemployment typically accompany recessions total demand goods falls pulling price level down conversely rate inflation often but not always seems start moving up when economy growing very strongly like right after wartime or during 1960s frameworks macroeconomic analysis that we developed other chapters will explain why recession often accompanies higher unemployment lower inflation while rapid economic growth often brings lower unemployment but higher inflation
22-3-how-the-u-s-and-other-countries-exp.section_name: Historical Inflation in the U.S. Economy

22-3-how-the-u-s-and-other-countries-exp.question: inflation around world around rest world pattern inflation has been very mixed figure 224 shows inflation rates over last several decades many industrialized countries not just united states had relatively high inflation rates 1970s example 1975 japans inflation rate was over 8 inflation rate united kingdom was almost 25 1980s inflation rates came down united states europe have largely stayed downcountries controlled economies 1970s like soviet union china historically had very low rates measured inflationbecause prices were forbidden rise by law except cases where government deemed a price increase be due quality improvements however these countries also had perpetual shortages goods since forbidding prices rise acts like a price ceiling creates a situation where quantity demanded often exceeds quantity supplied as russia china made a transition toward more marketoriented economies they also experienced outbursts inflation although we should regard statistics these economies as somewhat shakier inflation china averaged about 10 per year much 1980s early 1990s although has dropped off since then russia experienced hyperinflationan outburst high inflationof 2500 per year early 1990s although by 2006 russias consumer price inflation had dipped below 10 per year as figure 225 shows closest united states has ever reached hyperinflation was during 18601865 civil war confederate statesmany countries latin america experienced raging inflation during 1980s early 1990s inflation rates often well above 100 per year 1990 example both brazil argentina saw inflation climb above 2000 certain countries africa experienced extremely high rates inflation sometimes bordering on hyperinflation 1990s nigeria most populous country africa had an inflation rate 75 1995in early 2000s problem inflation appears have diminished most countries at least comparison worst times recent decades as we noted this earlier bring home feature recent years worlds worst example hyperinflation was zimbabwe where at one point government was issuing bills a face value 100 trillion zimbabwean dollarsthat bills had 100000000000000 written on front but were almost worthless many countries memory doubledigit tripledigit even quadrupledigit inflation not very far past

6-2-how-changes-in-income-and-prices-aff.question: how changes income affect consumer choices lets begin a concrete example illustrating how changes income level affect consumer choices figure 63 shows a budget constraint that represents kimberlys choice between concert tickets at 50 each getting away overnight a bedandbreakfast 200 per night kimberly has 1000 per year spend between these two choices after thinking about her total utility marginal utility applying decision rule that ratio marginal utilities prices should be equal between two products kimberly chooses point m eight concerts three overnight getaways as her utilitymaximizing choice now assume that income kimberly has spend on these two items rises 2000 per year causing her budget constraint shift out right how does this rise income alter her utilitymaximizing choice kimberly will again consider utility marginal utility that she receives from concert tickets overnight getaways seek her utilitymaximizing choice on new budget line but how will her new choice relate her original choicewe can replace possible choices along new budget constraint into three groups which dashed horizontal vertical lines that pass through original choice m figure divide all choices on upper left new budget constraint that are left vertical dashed line like choice p two overnight stays 32 concert tickets involve less good on horizontal axis but much more good on vertical axis all choices right vertical dashed line above horizontal dashed linelike choice n five overnight getaways 20 concert ticketshave more consumption both goods finally all choices that are right vertical dashed line but below horizontal dashed line like choice q four concerts nine overnight getaways involve less good on vertical axis but much more good on horizontal axisall these choices are theoretically possible depending on kimberlys personal preferences as expressed through total marginal utility she would receive from consuming these two goods when income rises most common reaction purchase more both goods like choice n which upper right relative kimberlys original choice m although exactly how much more each good will vary according personal taste conversely when income falls most typical reaction purchase less both goods as we defined chapter on demand supply again chapter on elasticity we call goods services normal goods when a rise income leads a rise quantity consumed that good a fall income leads a fall quantity consumedhowever depending on kimberlys preferences a rise income could cause consumption one good increase while consumption other good declines a choice like p means that a rise income caused her quantity consumed overnight stays decline while a choice like q would mean that a rise income caused her quantity concerts decline goods where demand declines as income rises or conversely where demand rises as income falls are called inferior goods an inferior good occurs when people trim back on a good as income rises because they can now afford more expensive choices that they prefer example a higherincome household might eat fewer hamburgers or be less likely buy a used car instead eat more steak buy a new car
6-2-how-changes-in-income-and-prices-aff.section_name: How Changes in Income Affect Consumer Choices

6-2-how-changes-in-income-and-prices-aff.question: how price changes affect consumer choices analyzing possible effect a change price on consumption lets again use a concrete example figure 64 represents sergeis consumer choice who chooses between purchasing baseball bats cameras a price increase baseball bats would have no effect on ability purchase cameras but would reduce number bats sergei could afford buy thus a price increase baseball bats good on horizontal axis causes budget constraint rotate inward as if on a hinge from vertical axis as previous section point labeled m represents originally preferred point on original budget constraint which sergei has chosen after contemplating his total utility marginal utility tradeoffs involved along budget constraint this example units along horizontal vertical axes are not numbered so discussion must focus on whether sergei will consume more or less certain goods not on numerical amountsafter price increase sergei will make a choice along new budget constraint again we can divide his choices into three segments by dashed vertical horizontal lines upper left portion new budget constraint at a choice like h sergei consumes more cameras fewer bats central portion new budget constraint at a choice like j he consumes less both goods at righthand end at a choice like l he consumes more bats but fewer camerasthe typical response higher prices that a person chooses consume less product higher price this occurs two reasons both effects can occur simultaneously substitution effect occurs when a price changes consumers have an incentive consume less good a relatively higher price more good a relatively lower price income effect that a higher price means effect buying power income has been reduced even though actual income has not changed which leads buying less good when good normal this example higher price baseball bats would cause sergei buy fewer bats both reasons exactly how much will a higher price bats cause sergeis bat consumption fall figure 64 suggests a range possibilities sergei might react a higher price baseball bats by purchasing same quantity bats but cutting his camera consumption this choice point k on new budget constraint straight below original choice m alternatively sergei might react by dramatically reducing his bat purchases instead buy more cameras key that would be imprudent assume that a change price one good will only affect consumption that good our example since sergei purchases all his products out same budget a change price baseball bats can also have a range effects either positive or negative on his purchases cameras since sergei purchases all his products out same budget a change price baseball bats can also have a range effects either positive or negative on his purchases camerasin short a higher price typically causes reduced consumption good question but can affect consumption other goods as well
6-2-how-changes-in-income-and-prices-aff.section_name: How Price Changes Affect Consumer Choices

shows a budget constraint that represents kimberlys choice between concert tickets at 50 each getting away overnight a bedandbreakfast 200 per night kimberly has 1000 per year spend between these two choices after thinking about her total utility marginal utility applying decision rule that ratio marginal utilities prices should be equal between two products kimberly chooses point m eight concerts three overnight getaways as her utilitymaximizing choice now assume that income kimberly has spend on these two items rises 2000 per year causing her budget constraint shift out right how does this rise income alter her utilitymaximizing choice kimberly will again consider utility marginal utility that she receives from concert tickets overnight getaways seek her utilitymaximizing choice on new budget line but how will her new choice relate her original choicewe can replace possible choices along new budget constraint into three groups which dashed horizontal vertical lines that pass through original choice m figure divide all choices on upper left new budget constraint that are left vertical dashed line like choice p two overnight stays 32 concert tickets involve less good on horizontal axis but much more good on vertical axis all choices right vertical dashed line above horizontal dashed linelike choice n five overnight getaways 20 concert ticketshave more consumption both goods finally all choices that are right vertical dashed line but below horizontal dashed line like choice q four concerts nine overnight getaways involve less good on vertical axis but much more good on horizontal axisall these choices are theoretically possible depending on kimberlys personal preferences as expressed through total marginal utility she would receive from consuming these two goods when income rises most common reaction purchase more both goods like choice n which upper right relative kimberlys original choice m although exactly how much more each good will vary according personal taste conversely when income falls most typical reaction purchase less both goods as we defined chapter on demand supply again chapter on elasticity we call goods services normal goods when a rise income leads a rise quantity consumed that good a fall income leads a fall quantity consumedhowever depending on kimberlys preferences a rise income could cause consumption one good increase while consumption other good declines a choice like p means that a rise income caused her quantity consumed overnight stays decline while a choice like q would mean that a rise income caused her quantity concerts decline goods where demand declines as income rises or conversely where demand rises as income falls are called inferior goods an inferior good occurs when people trim back on a good as income rises because they can now afford more expensive choices that they prefer example a higherincome household might eat fewer hamburgers or be less likely buy a used car instead eat more steak buy a new car

higher price bats cause sergeis bat consumption fall figure 64 suggests a range possibilities sergei might react a higher price baseball bats by purchasing same quantity bats but cutting his camera consumption this choice point k on new budget constraint straight below original choice m alternatively sergei might react by dramatically reducing his bat purchases instead buy more cameras key that 22-2-how-to-measure-changes-in-the-cost-.question: cpi core inflation index imagine if you were driving a company truck across country you probably would care about things like prices available roadside food motel rooms as well as trucks operating condition however manager firm might have different priorities he would care mostly about trucks ontime performance much less so about food you were eating places you were staying other words company manager would be paying attention firms production while ignoring transitory elements that impacted you but did not affect companys bottom line a sense a similar situation occurs regard measures inflation as weve learned cpi measures prices as they affect everyday household spending economists typically calculate a core inflation index by taking cpi excluding volatile economic variables this way economists have a better sense underlying trends prices that affect cost living examples excluded variables include energy food prices which can jump around from month month because weather according an article by kent bernhard during hurricane katrina 2005 a key supply point nations gasoline was nearly knocked out gas prices quickly shot up across nation some places by up 40 cents a gallon one day this was not cause an economic policy but rather a shortlived event until pumps were restored region this case cpi that month would register change as a cost living event households but core inflation index would remain unchanged as a result federal reserves decisions on interest rates would not be influenced similarly droughts can cause worldwide spikes food prices that if temporary do not affect nations economic capability as former chairman federal reserve ben bernanke noted 1999 about core inflation index provides a better guide monetary policy than other indices since measures more persistent underlying inflation rather than transitory influences on price level bernanke also noted that helps communicate that federal reserve does not need respond every inflationary shock since some price changes are transitory not part a structural change economy sum both cpi core inflation index are important but serve different audiences cpi helps households understand their overall cost living from month month while core inflation index a preferred gauge from which make important government policy changes

22-4-the-confusion-over-inflation.question: land funny money what are economic problems caused by inflation why do economists often regard them less concern than general public consider a very short story land funny money one morning everyone land funny money awakened find that everything denominated money had increased by 20 change was completely unexpected every price every store was 20 higher paychecks were 20 higher interest rates were 20 higher amount money everywhere from wallets savings accounts was 20 larger this overnight inflation prices made newspaper headlines everywhere land funny money however headlines quickly disappeared as people realized that terms what they could actually buy their incomes this inflation had no economic impact everyones pay could still buy exactly same set goods as did before everyones savings were still sufficient buy exactly same car vacation or retirement that they could have bought before equal levels inflation all wages prices ended up not mattering much at allwhen people robert shillers surveys explained their concern about inflation one typical reason was that they feared that as prices rose they would not be able afford buy as much other words people were worried because they did not live a place like land funny money where all prices wages rose simultaneously instead people live here on planet earth where prices might rise while wages do not rise at all or where wages rise more slowly than prices economists note that over most periods inflation level prices roughly similar inflation level wages so they reason that on average over time peoples economic status not greatly changed by inflation if all prices wages interest rates adjusted automatically immediately inflation as land funny money then no ones purchasing power profits or real loan payments would change however if other economic variables do not move exactly sync inflation or if they adjust inflation only after a time lag then inflation can cause three types problems unintended redistributions purchasing power blurred price signals difficulties longterm planning

unintended redistributions purchasing power inflation can cause redistributions purchasing power that hurt some help others people who are hurt by inflation include those who are holding considerable cash whether a safe deposit box or a cardboard box under bed when inflation happens buying power cash diminishes however cash only an example a more general problem anyone who has financial assets invested a way that nominal return does not keep up inflation will tend be affected by inflation example if a person has money a bank account that pays 4 interest but inflation rises 5 then real rate return money invested that bank account negative 1the problem a goodlooking nominal interest rate transforming into an uglylooking real interest rate can be worsened by taxes us income tax charged on nominal interest received dollar terms without an adjustment inflation thus government taxes a person who invests 10000 receives a 5 nominal rate interest on 500 receivedno matter whether inflation rate 0 5 or 10 if inflation 0 then real interest rate 5 all 500 a gain buying power however if inflation 5 then real interest rate zero person had no real gainbut owes income tax on nominal gain anyway if inflation 10 then real interest rate negative 5 person actually falling behind buying power but would still owe taxes on 500 nominal gainsinflation can cause unintended redistributions wage earners too wages do typically creep up inflation over time eventually last row table 221 at start this chapter showed that average hourly wage manufacturing us economy increased from 323 1970 3011 2021 which an increase by a factor close ten over that time period consumer price index increased by about a factor eight however increases wages may lag behind inflation a year or two since wage adjustments are often somewhat sticky occur only once or twice a year moreover extent which wages keep up inflation creates insecurity workers may involve painful prolonged conflicts between employers employees if government adjusts minimum wage inflation only infrequently minimum wage workers are losing purchasing power from their nominal wages as figure 226 showsone sizable group people has often received a large share their income a form that does not increase over time retirees who receive a private company pension most pensions have traditionally been set as a fixed nominal dollar amount per year at retirement this reason economists call pensions defined benefits plans even if inflation low combination inflation a fixed income can create a substantial problem over time a person who retires on a fixed income at age 65 will find that losing just 1 2 buying power per year inflation compounds a considerable loss buying power after a decade or twofortunately pensions other defined benefits retirement plans are increasingly rare replaced instead by defined contribution plans such as 401ks 403bs these plans employer contributes a fixed amount workers retirement account on a regular basis usually every pay check employee often contributes as well worker invests these funds a wide range investment vehicles these plans are tax deferred they are portable so that if individual takes a job a different employer their 401k comes them extent that investments made generate real rates return retirees are not burdened by inflation costs traditional pensionershowever ordinary people can sometimes benefit from unintended redistributions inflation consider someone who borrows 10000 buy a car at a fixed interest rate 9 if inflation 3 at time loan made then borrower must repay loan at a real interest rate 6 however if inflation rises 9 then real interest rate on loan zero this case borrowers benefit from inflation lenders loss a borrower paying a fixed interest rate who benefits from inflation just flip side an investor receiving a fixed interest rate who suffers from inflation lesson that when interest rates are fixed rises rate inflation tend penalize suppliers financial capital who receive repayment dollars that are worth less because inflation while demanders financial capital end up better off because they can repay their loans dollars that are worth less than originally expectedthe unintended redistributions buying power that inflation causes may have a broader effect on society americas widespread acceptance market forces rests on a perception that peoples actions have a reasonable connection market outcomes when inflation causes a retiree who built up a pension or invested at a fixed interest rate suffer however while someone who borrowed at a fixed interest rate benefits from inflation hard believe that this outcome was deserved any way similarly when homeowners benefit from inflation because price their homes rises while renters suffer because they are paying higher rent hard see any useful incentive effects one reasons that general public dislikes inflation a sense that makes economic rewards penalties more arbitraryand therefore likely be perceived as unfair even dangerous as next clear up feature shows

blurred price signals prices are messengers a market economy conveying information about conditions demand supply inflation blurs those price messages inflation means that we perceive price signals more vaguely like a radio program received considerable static if static becomes severe hard tell what happeningin israel when inflation accelerated an annual rate 500 1985 some stores stopped posting prices directly on items since they would have had put new labels on items or shelves every few days reflect inflation instead a shopper just took items from a shelf went up checkout register find out price that day obviously this situation makes comparing prices shopping best deal rather difficult when levels changes prices become uncertain businesses individuals find harder react economic signals a world where inflation at a high rate but bouncing up down some extent does a higher price a good mean that inflation has risen or that supply that good has decreased or that demand that good has increased should a buyer good take higher prices as an economic hint start substituting other productsor have prices substitutes risen by an equal amount should a seller good take a higher price as a reason increase productionor higher price only a sign a general inflation which prices all inputs production are rising as well true story will presumably become clear over time but at a given moment who can say high variable inflation means that incentives economy adjust response changes prices are weaker markets will adjust toward their equilibrium prices quantities more erratically slowly many individual markets will experience a greater chance surpluses shortages

problems longterm planning inflation can make longterm planning difficult discussing unintended redistributions we considered case someone trying plan retirement a pension that fixed nominal terms a high rate inflation similar problems arise all people trying save retirement because they must consider what their money will really buy several decades future when we cannot know rate future inflationinflation especially at moderate or high levels will pose substantial planning problems businesses too a firm can make money from inflationfor example by paying bills wages as late as possible so that can pay inflated dollars while collecting revenues as soon as possible a firm can also suffer losses from inflation as case a retail business that gets stuck holding too much cash only see inflation eroding value that cash however when a business spends its time focusing on how profit by inflation or at least how avoid suffering from an inevitable tradeoff strikes less time spent on improving products services or on figuring out how make existing products services more cheaply an economy high inflation rewards businesses that have found clever ways profiting from inflation which are not necessarily businesses that excel at productivity innovation or quality servicein short term low or moderate levels inflation may not pose an overwhelming difficulty business planning because costs doing business sales revenues may rise at similar rates if however inflation varies substantially over short or medium term then may make sense businesses stick shorterterm strategies evidence as whether relatively low rates inflation reduce productivity controversial among economists there some evidence that if inflation can be held moderate levels less than 3 per year need not prevent a nations real economy from growing at a healthy pace some countries that have experienced hyperinflation several thousand percent per year an annual inflation rate 2030 may feel basically same as zero however several economists have pointed suggestive fact that when us inflation heated up early 1970sto 10us growth productivity slowed down when inflation slowed down 1980s productivity edged up again not long thereafter as figure 227 shows

any benefits inflation although economic effects inflation are primarily negative two countervailing points are worth noting first impact inflation will differ considerably according whether creeping up slowly at 0 2 per year galloping along at 10 20 per year or racing point hyperinflation at say 40 per month hyperinflation can rip an economy a society apart an annual inflation rate 2 3 or 4 however a long way from a national crisis low inflation also better than deflation which occurs severe recessions second economists sometimes argue that moderate inflation may help economy by making wages labor markets more flexible discussion unemployment pointed out that wages tend be sticky their downward movements that unemployment can result a little inflation could nibble away at real wages thus help real wages decline if necessary this way even if a moderate or high rate inflation may act as sand gears economy perhaps a low rate inflation serves as oil gears labor market this argument controversial a full analysis would have account all effects inflation does however offer another reason believe that all things considered very low rates inflation may not be especially harmful

available resources are devoted healthcare none are left education this situation would be extreme even ridiculous example children are seeing a doctor every day whether they are sick or not but not attending school people are having cosmetic surgery on every part their bodies but no high school or college education exists now imagine that some these resources are diverted from healthcare education so that economy at point b instead point a diverting some resources away from a b causes relatively little reduction health because last few marginal dollars going into healthcare services are not producing much additional gain health however putting those marginal dollars into education which completely without resources at point a can produce relatively large gains this reason shape ppf from a b relatively flat representing a relatively small dropoff health a relatively large gain education now consider other end at lower right production possibilities frontier imagine that society starts at choice d which devoting nearly all resources education very few healthcare moves point f which devoting all spending education none healthcare sake concreteness you can imagine that movement from d f last few doctors must become high school science teachers last few nurses must become school librarians rather than dispensers vaccinations last few emergency rooms are turned into kindergartens gains education from adding these last few resources education are very small however opportunity cost lost health will be fairly large thus slope ppf between d f steep showing a large drop health only a small gain education lesson not that society likely make an extreme choice like devoting no resources education at point a or no resources health at point f instead lesson that gains from committing additional marginal resources education depend on how much already being spent if on one hand very few resources are currently committed education then an increase resources used education can bring relatively large gains on other hand if a large number resources are already committed education then committing additional resources will bring relatively smaller gains this pattern common enough that economists have given a name law increasing opportunity cost which holds that as production a good or service increases marginal opportunity cost producing increases as well this happens because some resources are better suited producing certain goods services instead others when government spends a certain amount more on reducing crime example original increase opportunity cost reducing crime could be relatively small however additional increases typically cause relatively larger increases opportunity cost reducing crime paying enough police security reduce crime nothing at all would be a tremendously high opportunity cost curvature production possibilities frontier shows that as we add more resources education moving from left right along horizontal axis original increase opportunity cost fairly small but gradually increases thus slope ppf relatively flat near verticalaxis intercept conversely as we add more resources healthcare moving from bottom top on vertical axis original declines opportunity cost are fairly large but again gradually diminish thus slope ppf relatively steep near horizontalaxis intercept this way law increasing opportunity cost produces outwardbending shape production possibilities frontier

2-2-the-production-possibilities-frontie.question: ppf comparative advantage while every society must choose how much each good or service should produce does not need produce every single good consumes often how much a good a country decides produce depends on how expensive produce versus buying from a different country as we saw earlier curvature a countrys ppf gives us information about tradeoff between devoting resources producing one good versus another particular its slope gives opportunity cost producing one more unit good xaxis terms other good yaxis countries tend have different opportunity costs producing a specific good either because different climates geography technology or skills suppose two countries us brazil need decide how much they will produce two crops sugar cane wheat due its climatic conditions brazil can produce quite a bit sugar cane per acre but not much wheat conversely us can produce large amounts wheat per acre but not much sugar cane clearly brazil has a lower opportunity cost producing sugar cane terms wheat than us reverse also true us has a lower opportunity cost producing wheat than brazil we illustrate this by ppfs two countries figure 25when a country can produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country we say that this country has a comparative advantage that good comparative advantage not same as absolute advantage which when a country can produce more a good our example brazil has an absolute advantage sugar cane us has an absolute advantage wheat one can easily see this a simple observation extreme production points ppfs two countries if brazil devoted all its resources producing wheat would be producing at point a if however had devoted all its resources producing sugar cane instead would be producing a much larger amount than us at point bthe slope ppf gives opportunity cost producing an additional unit wheat while slope not constant throughout ppfs quite apparent that ppf brazil much steeper than us therefore opportunity cost wheat generally higher brazil chapter on international trade you will learn that countries differences comparative advantage determine which goods they will choose produce trade when countries engage trade they specialize production goods which they have comparative advantage trade part that production goods which they do not have comparative advantage trade manufacturers produce goods where opportunity cost lowest so total production increases benefiting both trading parties

possess more careful decisionmaking than most individuals actually display after all do you or any your friends draw a budget constraint mutter yourself about maximizing utility before you head budget constraints or smoothly curving production possibilities frontiers however economics approach can be a useful way analyze understand tradeoffs economic decisions appreciate this point imagine a moment that you are playing basketball dribbling right throwing a bouncepass left a teammate who running toward basket a physicist or engineer could work out correct speed trajectory pass given 2-3-confronting-objections-to-the-econom.question: bring home choices what degree what have we learned we know that scarcity impacts all choices we make an economist might argue that people do not obtain a bachelors or masters degree because they do not have resources make those choices or because their incomes are too low andor price these degrees too high a bachelors or a masters degree may not be available their opportunity setthe price these degrees may be too high not only because actual price college tuition perhaps room board too high an economist might also say that many people full opportunity cost a bachelors or a masters degree too high these people they are unwilling or unable make tradeoff forfeiting years working earning an income earn a degreefinally statistics we introduced at start chapter reveal information about intertemporal choices an economist might say that people choose not obtain a college degree because they may have borrow money attend college interest they have pay on that loan future will affect their decisions today also could be that some people have a preference current consumption over future consumption so they choose work now at a lower salary consume now rather than postponing that consumption until after they graduate college

second objection people firms society should not act this way economics approach portrays people as selfinterested some critics this approach even if selfinterest an accurate description how people behave these behaviors are not moral instead critics argue that people should be taught care more deeply about others economists offer several
2-3-confronting-objections-to-the-econom.answer: s these concerns first economics not a form moral instruction rather seeks describe economic behavior as actually exists philosophers draw a distinction between positive statements which describe world as normative statements which describe how world should be positive statements are factual they may be true or false but we can test them at least principle normative statements are subjective questions opinion we cannot test them since we cannot prove opinions be true or false they just are opinions based on ones values example an economist could analyze a proposed subway system a certain city if expected benefits exceed costs he concludes that project worthyan example positive analysis another economist argues extended unemployment compensation during covid19 pandemic because a rich country like united states should take care its less fortunate citizensan example normative analysiseven if line between positive normative statements not always crystal clear economic analysis does try remain rooted study actual people who inhabit actual economy fortunately however assumption that individuals are purely selfinterested a simplification about human nature fact we need look no further than adam smith very father modern economics find evidence this opening sentence his book theory moral sentiments puts very clearly how selfish soever man may be supposed there are evidently some principles his nature which interest him fortune others render their happiness necessary him though he derives nothing from except pleasure seeing clearly individuals are both selfinterested altruistic second we can label selfinterested behavior profitseeking other names such as personal choice freedom ability make personal choices about buying working saving an important personal freedom some people may choose highpressure highpaying jobs so that they can earn spend considerable amounts money on themselves others may allocate large portions their earnings charity or spend on their friends family others may devote themselves a career that can require much time energy expertise but does not offer high financial rewards like being an elementary school teacher or a social worker still others may choose a job that does consume much their time or provide a high level income but still leaves time family friends contemplation some people may prefer work a large company others might want start their own business peoples freedom make their own economic choices has a moral value worth respecting third selfinterested behavior can lead positive social results example when people work hard make a living they create economic output consumers who are looking best deals will encourage businesses offer goods services that meet their needs adam smith writing wealth nations named this property invisible hand describing how consumers producers interact a market economy smith wrote every individualgenerally indeed neither intends promote public interest nor knows how much he promoting by preferring support domestic that foreign industry he intends only his own security by directing that industry such a manner as its produce may be greatest value he intends only his own gain he this as many other cases led by an invisible hand promote an end which was no part his intentionby pursuing his own interest he frequently promotes that society more effectually than when he really intends promote itthe metaphor invisible hand suggests remarkable possibility that broader social good can emerge from selfish individual actions fourth even people who focus on their own selfinterest economic part their life often set aside their own narrow selfinterest other parts life example you might focus on your own selfinterest when asking your employer a raise or negotiating buy a car then you might turn around focus on other people when you volunteer read stories at local library help a friend move a new apartment or donate money a charity selfinterest a reasonable starting point analyzing many economic decisions without needing imply that people never do anything that not their own immediate selfinterest

that industry such a manner as its produce may be greatest value he intends only his own gain he this as many other cases led by an invisible hand promote an end which was no part his intentionby pursuing his own interest he frequently promotes that society more effectually than when he really intends promote itthe metaphor invisible hand suggests remarkable possibility that broader social good can emerge from selfish individual actions fourth even people who focus on their own selfinterest economic part their life often set aside their own narrow selfinterest other parts life example you might focus on your own selfinterest when asking your employer a raise or negotiating buy a car then you might turn around focus on other people when you volunteer read stories at local library
