possess more careful decisionmaking than most individuals actually display after all do you or any your friends draw a budget constraint mutter yourself about maximizing utility before you head shopping mall do members us congress contemplate production possibilities frontiers before they vote on annual budget messy ways which people societies operate somehow doesnt look much like neat budget constraints or smoothly curving production possibilities frontiers however economics approach can be a useful way analyze understand tradeoffs economic decisions appreciate this point imagine a moment that you are playing basketball dribbling right throwing a bouncepass left a teammate who running toward basket a physicist or engineer could work out correct speed trajectory pass given different movements involved weight bounciness ball however when you are playing basketball you do not perform any these calculations you just pass ball if you are a good player you will do so high accuracysomeone might argue scientists formula bouncepass requires a far greater knowledge physics far more specific information about speeds movement weights than basketball player actually has so must be an unrealistic description how basketball passes actually occur this reaction would be wrongheaded fact that a good player can throw ball accurately because practice skill without making a physics calculation does not mean that physics calculation wrongsimilarly from an economic point view someone who shops groceries every week has a great deal practice how purchase combination goods that will provide that person utility even if shopper does not phrase decisions terms a budget constraint government institutions may work imperfectly slowly but general a democratic form government feels pressure from voters social institutions make choices that are most widely preferred by people that society thus when thinking about economic actions groups people firms society reasonable as a first approximation analyze them tools economic analysis more on this read about behavioral economics chapter on consumer choices

second objection people firms society should not act this way economics approach portrays people as selfinterested some critics this approach even if selfinterest an accurate description how people behave these behaviors are not moral instead critics argue that people should be taught care more deeply about others economists offer several
2-3-confronting-objections-to-the-econom.answer: s these concerns first economics not a form moral instruction rather seeks describe economic behavior as actually exists philosophers draw a distinction between positive statements which describe world as normative statements which describe how world should be positive statements are factual they may be true or false but we can test them at least principle normative statements are subjective questions opinion we cannot test them since we cannot prove opinions be true or false they just are opinions based on ones values example an economist could analyze a proposed subway system a certain city if expected benefits exceed costs he concludes that project worthyan example positive analysis another economist argues extended unemployment compensation during covid19 pandemic because a rich country like united states should take care its less fortunate citizensan example normative analysiseven if line between positive normative statements not always crystal clear economic analysis does try remain rooted study actual people who inhabit actual economy fortunately however assumption that individuals are purely selfinterested a simplification about human nature fact we need look no further than adam smith very father modern economics find evidence this opening sentence his book theory moral sentiments puts very clearly how selfish soever man may be supposed there are evidently some principles his nature which interest him fortune others render their happiness necessary him though he derives nothing from except pleasure seeing clearly individuals are both selfinterested altruistic second we can label selfinterested behavior profitseeking other names such as personal choice freedom ability make personal choices about buying working saving an important personal freedom some people may choose highpressure highpaying jobs so that they can earn spend considerable amounts money on themselves others may allocate large portions their earnings charity or spend on their friends family others may devote themselves a career that can require much time energy expertise but does not offer high financial rewards like being an elementary school teacher or a social worker still others may choose a job that does consume much their time or provide a high level income but still leaves time family friends contemplation some people may prefer work a large company others might want start their own business peoples freedom make their own economic choices has a moral value worth respecting third selfinterested behavior can lead positive social results example when people work hard make a living they create economic output consumers who are looking best deals will encourage businesses offer 2-1-how-individuals-make-choices-based-o.question: concept opportunity cost economists use term opportunity cost indicate what people must give up obtain what they desire idea behind opportunity cost that cost one item lost opportunity do or consume something else short opportunity cost value next best alternative alphonso opportunity cost a burger four bus tickets he would have give up he would decide whether or not choose burger depending on whether value burger exceeds value forgone alternativein this case bus tickets since people must choose they inevitably face tradeoffs which they have give up things they desire obtain other things they desire more a fundamental principle economics that every choice has an opportunity cost if you sleep through your economics class opportunity cost learning you miss from not attending class if you spend your income on video games you cannot spend on movies if you choose marry one person you give up opportunity marry anyone else short opportunity cost all around us part human existencethe following work out feature shows a stepbystep analysis a budget constraint calculation read through understand another important conceptslopethat we further explain appendix use mathematics principles economics work out understanding budget constraints budget constraints are easy understand if you apply a little math appendix use mathematics principles economics explains all math you are likely need this book therefore if math not your strength you might want take a look at appendixstep 1 equation any budget constraint budgetp1 q1 p2 q2budgetp1 q1 p2 q2 where p q are price quantity items purchased which we assume here be two items budget amount income one has spendstep 2 apply budget constraint equation scenario alphonsos case this works out be budget p1 q1 p2 q2 10 budget2 per burger quantity burgers 050 per bus ticket quantity bus tickets102 qburgers 050 qbus ticketsbudget p1 q1 p2 q2 10 budget2 per burger quantity burgers 050 per bus ticket quantity bus tickets102 qburgers 050 qbus tickets step 3 using a little algebra we can turn this into familiar equation a line y b mxy b mx alphonso this 10 2 qburgers 050 qbus tickets10 2 qburgers 050 qbus tickets step 4 simplify equation begin by multiplying both sides equation by 2 2 10 2 2 qburgers 2 05 qbus tickets 20 4 qburgers 1 qbus tickets2 10 2 2 qburgers 2 05 qbus tickets 20 4 qburgers 1 qbus tickets step 5 subtract one bus ticket from both sides20 qbus tickets 4 qburgers20 qbus tickets 4 qburgers divide each side by 4 yield
2-1-how-individuals-make-choices-based-o.answer: 5 025 qbus tickets qburgersorqburgers 5 025 qbus tickets5 025 qbus tickets qburgersorqburgers 5 025 qbus tickets step 6 notice that this equation fits budget constraint figure 22 vertical intercept 5 slope 025 just as equation says if you plug 20 bus tickets into equation you get 0 burgers if you plug other numbers bus tickets into equation you get results see table 21 which are points on alphonsos budget constraint point quantity burgers at 2 quantity bus tickets at 50 cents a 5 0 b 4 4 c 3 8 d 2 12 e 1 16 f 0 20 table 21 step 7 notice that slope a budget constraint always shows opportunity cost good which on horizontal axis alphonso slope 025 indicating that every bus ticket he buys he must give up 14 burger phrase differently every four tickets he buys alphonso must give up 1 burger there are two important observations here first algebraic sign slope negative which means that only way get more one good give up some other second we define slope as price bus tickets whatever on horizontal axis graph divided by price burgers whatever on vertical axis this case 0502 025 if you want determine opportunity cost quickly just divide two prices

2-1-how-individuals-make-choices-based-o.question: identifying opportunity cost many cases reasonable refer opportunity cost as price if your cousin buys a new bicycle 300 then 300 measures amount other consumption that he has forsaken practical purposes there may be no special need identify specific alternative product or products that he could have bought that 300 but sometimes price as measured dollars may not accurately capture true opportunity cost this problem can loom especially large when costs time are involvedfor example consider a boss who decides that all employees will attend a twoday retreat build team spirit outofpocket monetary cost event may involve hiring an outside consulting firm run retreat as well as room board all participants however an opportunity cost exists as well during two days retreat none employees are doing any other workattending college another case where opportunity cost exceeds monetary cost outofpocket costs attending college include tuition books room board other expenses however addition during hours that you are attending class studying impossible work at a paying job thus college imposes both an outofpocket cost an opportunity cost lost earnings some cases realizing opportunity cost can alter behavior imagine example that you spend 8 on lunch every day at work you may know perfectly well that bringing a lunch from home would cost only 3 a day so opportunity cost buying lunch at restaurant 5 each day that 8 buying lunch costs minus 3 your lunch from home would cost five dollars each day does not seem be that much however if you project what that adds up a year250 days a year 5 per day equals 1250 cost perhaps a decent vacation if you describe opportunity cost as a nice vacation instead 5 a day you might make different choices

marginal decisionmaking diminishing marginal utility budget constraint framework helps emphasize that most choices real world are not about getting all one thing or all another that they are not about choosing either point at one end budget constraint or else point all way at other end instead most choices involve marginal analysis which means examining benefits costs choosing a little more or a little less a good people naturally compare costs benefits but often we look at total costs total benefits when optimal choice necessitates comparing how costs benefits change from one option another you might think marginal analysis as change analysis marginal analysis used throughout economicswe now turn notion utility people desire goods services satisfaction or utility those goods services provide utility as we will see chapter on consumer choices subjective but that does not make less real economists typically assume that more some good one consumes example slices pizza more utility one obtains at same time utility a person receives from consuming first unit a good typically more than utility received from consuming fifth or tenth unit that same good when alphonso chooses between burgers bus tickets example first few bus rides that he chooses might provide him a great deal utilityperhaps they help him get a job interview or a doctors appointment however later bus rides might provide much less utilitythey may only serve kill time on a rainy day similarly first burger that alphonso chooses buy may be on a day when he missed breakfast ravenously hungry however if alphonso has multiple burgers every day last few burgers may taste pretty boring general pattern that consumption first few units any good tends bring a higher level utility a person than consumption later units a common pattern economists refer this pattern as law diminishing marginal utility which means that as a person receives more a good additional or marginal utility from each additional unit good declines other words first slice pizza brings more satisfaction than sixththe law diminishing marginal utility explains why people societies rarely make allornothing choices you would not say my favorite food ice cream so i will eat nothing but ice cream from now on instead even if you get a very high level utility from your favorite food if you ate exclusively additional or marginal utility from those last few servings would not be very high similarly most workers do not say i enjoy leisure so ill never work instead workers recognize that even though some leisure very nice a combination all leisure no income not so attractive budget constraint framework suggests that when people make choices a world scarcity they will use marginal analysis think about whether they would prefer a little more or a little lessa rational consumer would only purchase additional units some product as long as marginal utility exceeds opportunity cost suppose alphonso moves down his budget constraint from point a point b point c further as he consumes more bus tickets marginal utility bus tickets will diminish while opportunity cost that marginal utility foregone burgers will increase eventually opportunity cost will exceed marginal utility an additional bus ticket if alphonso rational he wont purchase more bus tickets once marginal utility just equals opportunity cost while we cant yet say exactly how many bus tickets alphonso will buy that number unlikely be most he can afford 20

2-1-how-individuals-make-choices-based-o.question: sunk costs budget constraint framework all decisions involve what will happen next that what quantities goods will you consume how many hours will you work or how much will you save these decisions do not look back past choices thus budget constraint framework assumes that sunk costs which are costs that were incurred past cannot be recovered should not affect current decision consider case selena who pays 8 see a movie but after watching film 30 minutes she knows that truly terrible should she stay watch rest movie because she paid ticket or should she leave money she spent a sunk cost unless theater manager sympathetic selena will not get a refund however staying movie still means paying an opportunity cost time her choice whether spend next 90 minutes suffering through a cinematic disaster or do somethinganythingelse lesson sunk costs forget about money time that irretrievably gone instead focus on marginal costs benefits current future optionsfor people firms alike dealing sunk costs can be frustrating often means admitting an earlier error judgment many firms example find hard give up on a new product that doing poorly because they spent so much money creating launching product however lesson sunk costs ignore them make decisions based on what will happen future

from a model two goods one many goods budget constraint diagram containing just two goods like most models used this book not realistic after all a modern economy people choose from thousands goods however thinking about a model many goods a straightforward extension what we discussed here instead drawing just one budget constraint showing tradeoff between two goods you can draw multiple budget constraints showing possible tradeoffs between many different pairs goods more advanced classes economics you would use mathematical equations that include many possible goods services that can be purchased together their quantities prices show how total spending on all goods services limited overall budget available graph two goods that we presented here clearly illustrates that every choice has an opportunity cost which point that does carry over real world

2-1-how-individuals-make-choices-based-o.question: sunk costs budget constraint framework all decisions involve what will happen next that what quantities goods will you consume how many hours will you work or how much will you save these decisions do not look back past choices thus budget constraint framework assumes that sunk costs which are costs that were incurred past cannot be recovered should not affect current decision consider case selena who pays 8 see a movie but after watching film 30 minutes she knows that truly terrible should she stay watch rest movie because she paid ticket or should she leave money she spent a sunk cost unless theater manager sympathetic selena will not get a refund however staying movie still means paying an opportunity cost time her choice whether spend next 90 minutes suffering through a cinematic disaster or do somethinganythingelse lesson sunk costs forget about money time that irretrievably gone instead focus on marginal costs benefits current future optionsfor people firms alike dealing sunk costs can be frustrating often means admitting an earlier error judgment many firms example find hard give up on a new product that doing poorly because they spent so much money creating launching product however lesson sunk costs ignore them make decisions based on what will happen future

from a model two goods one many goods budget constraint diagram containing just two goods like most models used this book not realistic after all a modern economy people choose from thousands goods however thinking about a model many goods a straightforward extension what we discussed here instead drawing just one budget constraint showing tradeoff between two goods you can draw multiple budget constraints showing possible tradeoffs between many different pairs goods more advanced classes economics you would use mathematical equations that include many possible goods services that can be purchased together their quantities prices show how total spending on all goods services limited overall budget available graph two goods that we presented here clearly illustrates that every choice has an opportunity cost which point that does carry over real world

1-4-how-to-organize-economies-an-overvie.question: regulations rules game markets government regulations are always entangled there no such thing as an absolutely free market regulations always define rules game economy economies that are primarily marketoriented have fewer regulationsideally just enough maintain an even playing field participants at a minimum these laws govern matters like safeguarding private property against theft protecting people from violence enforcing legal contracts preventing fraud collecting taxes conversely even most commandoriented economies operate using markets how else would buying selling occur government heavily regulates decisions what produce prices charge heavily regulated economies often have underground economies or black markets which are markets where buyers sellers make transactions without governments approval question how organize economic institutions typically not a straightforward choice between all market or all government but instead involves a balancing act over appropriate combination market freedom government rules

1-4-how-to-organize-economies-an-overvie.question: bring home information overload information age world provides nearly instant access a wealth information consider that as recently as late 1970s farmers almanac along weather bureau us department agriculture were primary sources american farmers used determine when plant harvest their crops today these decisions are driven by data farmers access detailed data streams driven by global positioning systems historical rainfall patterns complex weather monitoring services they combine this information crop yield data soil quality measurements from prior years maximizing production efficiently can mean difference between a farm that remains profitable one that may need sell its land data helps eliminate guesswork information helps us make decisions as simple as what wear today how many reporters media should send cover an event each these decisions an economic decision after all resources are scarce if media send ten reporters cover an announcement they are not available cover other stories or complete other tasks information provides necessary knowledge make best possible decisions on how utilize scarce resources welcome world economics

rise globalizationrecent decades have seen a trend toward globalization which expanding cultural political economic connections between people around world one measure this increased buying selling goods services assets across national bordersin other words international trade financial capital flows globalization has occurred a number reasons improvements shipping as illustrated by container ship figure 110 air cargo have driven down transportation costs innovations computing telecommunications have made easier cheaper manage longdistance economic connections production sales many valuable products services modern economy can take form informationfor example computer software financial advice travel planning music books movies blueprints designing a building these products many others can be transported over telephones computer networks at everlower costs finally international agreements treaties between countries have encouraged greater trade table 12 presents one measure globalization shows percentage domestic economic production that was exported a selection countries from 2010 2015 according an entity known as world bank exports are goods services that one produces domestically sells abroad imports are goods services that one produces abroad then sells domestically gross domestic product gdp measures size total production an economy thus ratio exports divided by gdp measures what share a countrys total economic production sold other countries country 2010 2011 2012 2013 2014 2015 higher income countries united states 124 136 136 135 135 126 belgium 762 814 822 828 840 844 canada 291 307 300 301 317 315 france 260 278 281 283 290 300 middle income countries brazil 109 119 126 126 112 130 mexico 299 312 326 317 323 353 south korea 494 557 563 539 503 459 lower income countries chad 368 389 369 322 342 298 china 294 285 273 264 239 224 india 220 239 240 248 229 nigeria 253 313 314 180 184 table 12 extent globalization exportsgdp source httpdatabankworldbankorgdata recent decades exportgdp ratio has generally risen both worldwide us economy interestingly share us exports proportion us economy well below global average part because large economies like united states can contain more division labor inside their national borders however smaller economies like belgium korea canada need trade across their borders other countries take full advantage division labor specialization economies scale this sense enormous us economy less affected by globalization than most other countries table 12 indicates that many medium low income countries around world like mexico china have also experienced a surge globalization recent decades if an astronaut orbit could put on special glasses that make all economic transactions visible as brightly colored lines look down at earth astronaut would see planet covered connections despite rise globalization over last few decades recent years weve seen significant pushback against globalization from people across world concerned about loss jobs loss political sovereignty increased economic inequality prominent examples this pushback include 2016 vote great britain exit european union ie brexit election donald j trump president united states hopefully you now have an idea about economics before you move any other chapter study be sure read very important appendix this chapter called use mathematics principles economics essential that you learn more about how read use models economics

problem scarcity think about all things you consume food shelter clothing transportation healthcare entertainment how do you acquire those items you do not produce them yourself you buy them how do you afford things you buy you work pay if you do not someone else does on your behalf yet most us never have enough income buy all things we want this because scarcity so how do we solve every society at every level must make choices about how use its resources families must decide whether spend their money on a new car or a fancy vacation towns must choose whether put more budget into police fire protection or into school system nations must decide whether devote more funds national defense or protecting environment most cases there just isnt enough money budget do everything how do we use our limited resources best way possible that obtain most goods services we can there are a couple options first we could each produce everything we each consume alternatively we could each produce some what we want consume trade rest what we want lets explore these options why do we not each just produce all things we consume think back pioneer days when individuals knew how do so much more than we do today from building their homes growing their crops hunting food repairing their equipment most us do not know how do allor anyof those things but not because we could not learn rather we do not have reason why something called division specialization labor a production innovation first put forth by adam smith figure 13 his book wealth nations

division specialization labor formal study economics began when adam smith 17231790 published his famous book wealth nations 1776 many authors had written on economics centuries before smith but he was first address subject a comprehensive way first chapter smith introduces concept division labor which means that way one produces a good or service divided into a number tasks that different workers perform instead all tasks being done by same person illustrate division labor smith counted how many tasks went into making a pin drawing out a piece wire cutting right length straightening putting a head on one end a point on other packaging pins sale name just a few smith counted 18 distinct tasks that different people performedall a pin believe or not modern businesses divide tasks as well even a relatively simple business like a restaurant divides task serving meals into a range jobs like top chef sous chefs lessskilled kitchen help servers wait on tables a greeter at door janitors clean up a business manager handle paychecks billsnot mention economic connections a restaurant has suppliers food furniture kitchen equipment building where located a complex business like a large manufacturing factory such as shoe factory figure 14 or a hospital can have hundreds job classifications

1-1-what-is-economics-and-why-is-it-impo.question: why division labor increases production when we divide subdivide tasks involved producing a good or service workers businesses can produce a greater quantity output his observations pin factories smith noticed that one worker alone might make 20 pins a day but that a small business 10 workers some whom would need complete two or three 18 tasks involved pinmaking could make 48000 pins a day how can a group workers each specializing certain tasks produce so much more than same number workers who try produce entire good or service by themselves smith offered three reasons first specialization a particular small job allows workers focus on parts production process where they have an advantage later chapters we will develop this idea by discussing comparative advantage people have different skills talents interests so they will be better at some jobs than at others particular advantages may be based on educational choices which are turn shaped by interests talents only those medical degrees qualify become doctors instance some goods geography affects specialization example easier be a wheat farmer north dakota than florida but easier run a tourist hotel florida than north dakota if you live or near a big city easier attract enough customers operate a successful dry cleaning business or movie theater than if you live a sparsely populated rural area whatever reason if people specialize production what they do best they will be more effective than if they produce a combination things some which they are good at some which they are not second workers who specialize certain tasks often learn produce more quickly higher quality this pattern holds true many workers including assembly line laborers who build cars stylists who cut hair doctors who perform heart surgery fact specialized workers often know their jobs well enough suggest innovative ways do their work faster better a similar pattern often operates within businesses many cases a business that focuses on one or a few products sometimes called its core competency more successful than firms that try make a wide range products third specialization allows businesses take advantage economies scale which means that many goods as level production increases average cost producing each individual unit declines example if a factory produces only 100 cars per year each car will be quite expensive make on average however if a factory produces 50000 cars each year then can set up an assembly line huge machines workers performing specialized tasks average cost production per car will be lower ultimate result workers who can focus on their preferences talents learn do their specialized jobs better work larger organizations that society as a whole can produce consume far more than if each person tried produce all their own goods services division specialization labor has been a force against problem scarcity
1-1-what-is-economics-and-why-is-it-impo.section_name: Why the Division of Labor Increases Production

1-1-what-is-economics-and-why-is-it-impo.question: why division labor increases production when we divide subdivide tasks involved producing a good or service workers businesses can produce a greater quantity output his observations pin factories smith noticed that one worker alone might make 20 pins a day but that a small business 10 workers some whom would need complete two or three 18 tasks involved pinmaking could make 48000 pins a day how can a group workers each specializing certain tasks produce so much more than same number workers who try produce entire good or service by themselves smith offered three reasons first specialization a particular small job allows workers focus on parts production process where they have an advantage later chapters we will develop this idea by discussing comparative advantage people have different skills talents interests so they will be better at some jobs than at others particular advantages may be based on educational choices which are turn shaped by interests talents only those medical degrees qualify become doctors instance some goods geography affects specialization example easier be a wheat farmer north dakota than florida but easier run a tourist hotel florida than north dakota if you live or near a big city easier attract enough customers operate a successful dry cleaning business or movie theater than if you live a sparsely populated rural area whatever reason if people specialize production what they do best they will be more effective than if they produce a combination things some which they are good at some which they are not second workers who specialize certain tasks often learn produce more quickly higher quality this pattern holds true many workers including assembly line laborers who build cars stylists who cut hair doctors who perform heart surgery fact specialized workers often know their jobs well enough suggest innovative ways do their work faster better a similar pattern often operates within businesses many cases a business that focuses on one or a few products sometimes called its core competency more successful than firms that try make a wide range products third specialization allows businesses take advantage economies scale which means that many goods as level production increases average cost producing each individual unit declines example if a factory produces only 100 cars per year each car will be quite expensive make on average however if a factory produces 50000 cars each year then can set up an assembly line huge machines workers performing specialized tasks average cost production per car will be lower ultimate result workers who can focus on their preferences talents learn do their specialized jobs better work larger organizations that society as a whole can produce consume far more than if each person tried produce all their own goods services division specialization labor has been a force against problem scarcity

21-3-what-causes-changes-in-unemployment.question: cyclical unemployment lets make plausible assumption that short run from a few months a few years quantity hours that average person willing work a given wage does not change much so labor supply curve does not shift much addition make standard ceteris paribus assumption that there no substantial shortterm change age structure labor force institutions laws affecting labor market or other possibly relevant factors one primary determinant demand labor from firms how they perceive state macro economy if firms believe that business expanding then at any given wage they will desire hire a greater quantity labor labor demand curve shifts right conversely if firms perceive that economy slowing down or entering a recession then they will wish hire a lower quantity labor at any given wage labor demand curve will shift left economists call variation unemployment that economy causes moving from expansion recession or from recession expansion ie business cycle cyclical unemploymentfrom standpoint supplyanddemand model competitive flexible labor markets unemployment represents something a puzzle a supplyanddemand model a labor market as figure 215 illustrates labor market should move toward an equilibrium wage quantity at equilibrium wage we equilibrium quantity qe labor supplied by workers should be equal quantity labor demanded by employersone possibility unemployment that people who are unemployed are those who are not willing work at current equilibrium wage say 10 an hour but would be willing work at a higher wage like 20 per hour monthly current population survey would count these people as unemployed because they say they are ready looking work at 20 per hour however from an economists perspective these people are choosing be unemployedprobably a few people are unemployed because unrealistic expectations about wages but they do not represent majority unemployed instead unemployed people often have friends or acquaintances similar skill levels who are employed unemployed would be willing work at jobs wages similar what those people are receiving however employers their friends acquaintances do not seem be hiring other words these people are involuntarily unemployed what causes involuntary unemployment

why wages might be sticky downward if a labor market model flexible wages does not describe unemployment very wellbecause predicts that anyone willing work at going wage can always find a jobthen may prove useful consider economic models which wages are not flexible or adjust only very slowly particular even though wage increases may occur relative ease wage decreases are few far between one set reasons why wages may be sticky downward as economists put involves economic laws institutions lowskilled workers receiving minimum wage illegal reduce their wages union workers operating under a multiyear contract a company wage cuts might violate contract create a labor dispute or a strike however minimum wages union contracts are not a sufficient reason why wages would be sticky downward us economy as a whole after all out 733 million or so employed workers us economy who earn wages by hour only about 11 millionless than 2 totaldo not receive compensation above minimum wage similarly labor unions represent only about 12 american wage salary workers other highincome countries more workers may have their wages determined by unions or minimum wage may be set at a level that applies a larger share workers however united states these two factors combined affect only about 15 or less labor forceeconomists looking reasons why wages might be sticky downwards have focused on factors that may characterize most labor relationships economy not just a few many have proposed a number different theories but they share a common toneone argument that even employees who are not union members often work under an implicit contract which that employer will try keep wages from falling when economy weak or business having trouble employee will not expect huge salary increases when economy or business strong this wagesetting behavior acts like a form insurance employee has some protection against wage declines bad times but pays that protection lower wages good times clearly this sort implicit contract means that firms will be hesitant cut wages lest workers feel betrayed work less hard or even leave firm efficiency wage theory argues that workers productivity depends on their pay so employers will often find worthwhile pay their employees somewhat more than market conditions might dictate one reason that employees who receive better pay than others will be more productive because they recognize that if they were lose their current jobs they would suffer a decline salary as a result they are motivated work harder stay current employer addition employers know that costly timeconsuming hire train new employees so they would prefer pay workers a little extra now rather than lose them have hire train new workers thus by avoiding wage cuts employer minimizes costs training hiring new workers reaps benefits wellmotivated employeesthe adverse selection wage cuts argument points out that if an employer reacts poor business conditions by reducing wages all workers then best workers those best employment alternatives at other firms are most likely leave least attractive workers fewer employment alternatives are more likely stay consequently firms are more likely choose which workers should depart through layoffs firings rather than trimming wages across board sometimes companies that are experiencing difficult times can persuade workers take a pay cut short term still retain most firms workers however far more typical companies lay off some workers rather than cut wages everyonethe insideroutsider model labor force simple terms argues that those already working firms are insiders while new employees at least a time are outsiders a firm depends on its insiders keep organization running smoothly be familiar routine procedures train new employees however cutting wages will alienate insiders damage firms productivity prospectsfinally relative wage coordination argument points out that even if most workers were hypothetically willing see a decline their own wages bad economic times as long as everyone else also experiences such a decline there no obvious way a decentralized economy implement such a plan instead workers confronted possibility a wage cut will worry that other workers will not have such a wage cut so a wage cut means being worse off both absolute terms relative others as a result workers fight hard against wage cuts these theories why wages tend not move downward differ their logic their implications figuring out strengths weaknesses each theory an ongoing subject research controversy among economists all tend imply that wages will decline only very slowly if at all even when economy or a business having tough times when wages are inflexible unlikely fall then either shortrun or longrun unemployment can result figure 216 illustrates thisfigure 217 shows interaction between shifts labor demand wages that are sticky downward figure 217 a illustrates situation which demand labor shifts right from d0 d1 this case equilibrium wage rises from w0 w1 equilibrium quantity labor hired increases from q0 q1 does not hurt employee morale at all wages risefigure 217 b shows situation which demand labor shifts left from d0 d1 as would tend do a recession because wages are sticky downward they do not adjust toward what would have been new equilibrium wage w1 at least not short run instead after shift labor demand curve same quantity workers willing work at that wage as before however quantity workers demanded at that wage has declined from original equilibrium q0 q2 gap between original equilibrium quantity q0 new quantity demanded labor q2 represents workers who would be willing work at going wage but cannot find jobs gap represents economic meaning unemploymentthis analysis helps explain connection that we noted earlier that unemployment tends rise recessions decline during expansions overall state economy shifts labor demand curve combined wages that are sticky downwards unemployment changes rise unemployment that occurs because a recession cyclical unemployment

21-4-what-causes-changes-in-unemployment.question: long run natural rate unemployment natural rate unemployment not natural sense that water freezes at 32 degrees fahrenheit or boils at 212 degrees fahrenheit not a physical unchanging law nature instead only natural rate because unemployment rate that would result from combination economic social political factors that exist at a timeassuming economy was neither booming nor recession these forces include usual pattern companies expanding contracting their workforces a dynamic economy social economic forces that affect labor market or public policies that affect either eagerness people work or willingness businesses hire lets discuss these factors more detail

21-4-what-causes-changes-in-unemployment.question: frictional unemployment a market economy some companies are always going broke a variety reasons old technology poor management good management that happened make bad decisions shifts tastes consumers so that less firms product desired a large customer who went broke or tough domestic or foreign competitors conversely other companies will be doing very well just opposite reasons looking hire more employees a perfect world all those who lost jobs would immediately find new ones however real world even if number job seekers equal number job vacancies takes time find out about new jobs interview figure out if new job a good match or perhaps sell a house buy another proximity a new job economists call unemployment that occurs meantime as workers move between jobs frictional unemployment frictional unemployment not inherently a bad thing takes time on part both employer individual match those looking employment correct job openings individuals companies be successful productive you want people find job which they are best suited not just first job offeredin mid2000s before 20082009 recession was true that about 7 us workers saw their jobs disappear any threemonth period however periods economic growth these destroyed jobs are counterbalanced economy as a whole by a larger number jobs created 2019 example there were typically about 6 million unemployed people at any given time us economy even though about twothirds those unemployed people found a job 14 weeks or fewer unemployment rate did not change much during year because those who found new jobs were largely offset by others who lost jobsof course would be preferable if people who were losing jobs could immediately easily move into newly created jobs but real world that not possible someone who laid off by a textile mill south carolina cannot turn around immediately start working a textile mill california instead adjustment process happens ripples some people find new jobs near their old ones while others find that they must move new locations some people can do a very similar job a different company while others must start new career paths some people may be near retirement decide look only parttime work while others want an employer that offers a longterm career path frictional unemployment that results from people moving between jobs a dynamic economy may account one two percentage points total unemploymentthe level frictional unemployment will depend on how easy workers learn about alternative jobs which may reflect ease communications about job prospects economy extent frictional unemployment will also depend some extent on how willing people are move new areas find jobswhich turn may depend on history culture frictional unemployment natural rate unemployment also seem depend on age distribution population figure 214 b showed that unemployment rates are typically lower people between 2554 years age or aged 55 over than they are those who are younger primeage workers as those 2554 age bracket are sometimes called are typically at a place their lives when they want have a job income arriving at all times addition older workers who lose jobs may prefer opt retirement by contrast likely that a relatively high proportion those who are under 25 will be trying out jobs life options this leads greater job mobility hence higher frictional unemployment thus a society a relatively high proportion young workers like us beginning mid1960s when baby boomers began entering labor market will tend have a higher unemployment rate than a society a higher proportion its workers older ages

structural unemployment another factor that influences natural rate unemployment amount structural unemployment structurally unemployed are individuals who have no jobs because they lack skills valued by labor market either because demand has shifted away from skills they do have or because they never learned any skills an example former would be unemployment among aerospace engineers after us space program downsized 1970s an example latter would be high school dropouts some people worry that technology causes structural unemployment past new technologies have put lower skilled employees out work but at same time they create demand higher skilled workers use new technologies education seems be key minimizing amount structural unemployment individuals who have degrees can be retrained if they become structurally unemployed people no skills little education that option more limited

natural unemployment potential real gdp natural unemployment rate related two other important concepts full employment potential real gdp economists consider economy be at full employment when actual unemployment rate equal natural unemployment rate when economy at full employment real gdp equal potential real gdp by contrast when economy below full employment unemployment rate greater than natural unemployment rate real gdp less than potential finally when economy above full employment then unemployment rate less than natural unemployment rate real gdp greater than potential operating above potential only possible a short while since analogous all workers working overtime

conversely if a business tries pay workers less than their productivity then a competitive labor market other businesses will find worthwhile hire away those workers pay them morehowever adjustments instead surprise decline productivity that occurred 1970s productivity unexpectedly rose mid1990s annual growth rate real output per hour labor increased from 17 from 19801995 an annual rate 26 from least a time however wages are still set according earlier expectations no productivity growth so wages do not rise result that at prevailing wage level w quantity labor demanded qd will a time exceed quantity labor supplied qs unemployment will be very lowactually below natural level unemployment a time this pattern unexpectedly high productivity helps explain why unemployment rate stayed below 45quite a low level by historical standardsfrom 1998 until after us economy had entered a recession 2001levels unemployment will tend be somewhat higher on average when productivity unexpectedly low 21-4-what-causes-changes-in-unemployment.question: public policy natural rate unemploymentpublic policy can also have a powerful effect on natural rate unemployment on supply side labor market public policies assist unemployed can affect how eager people are find work example if a worker who loses a job guaranteed a generous package unemployment insurance welfare benefits food stamps government medical benefits then opportunity cost unemployment lower that worker will be less eager seek a new jobwhat seems matter most not just amount these benefits but how long they last a society that provides generous help unemployed that cuts off after say six months may provide less an incentive unemployment than a society that provides less generous help that lasts several years conversely government assistance job search or retraining can some cases encourage people back work sooner see clear up learn how us handles unemployment insurance on demand side labor market government rules social institutions presence unions can affect willingness firms hire example if a government makes hard businesses start up or expand by wrapping new businesses bureaucratic red tape then businesses will become more discouraged about hiring government regulations can make harder start a business by requiring that a new business obtain many permits pay many fees or by restricting types quality products that a company can sell other government regulations like zoning laws may limit where companies can conduct business or whether businesses are allowed be open during evenings or on sundaywhatever rationales may be offered such laws terms social valuelike value some muslim people place on not working on friday or some jewish people on not working on saturday or some christian people on not working on sundaythese kinds restrictions create a difference between some willing workers other willing employers thus contribute a higher natural rate unemployment similarly if government makes difficult fire or lay off workers businesses may react by trying not hire more workers than strictly necessarysince laying these workers off would be costly difficult high minimum wages may discourage businesses from hiring lowskill workers government rules may encourage support powerful unions which can then push up wages union workers but at a cost discouraging businesses from hiring those workers

outline three common reasons that economists propose this change below internet has provided a remarkable new tool through which job seekers can find out about jobs at different companies can make contact relative ease an internet search far easier than trying find a list local employers then hunting up phone numbers all their human resources departments requesting a list jobs application forms social networking sites such as linkedin have changed how people find work as well growth temporary worker industry has probably helped reduce natural rate unemployment early 1980s only about 05 all workers held jobs through temp agencies by early 2000s figure had risen above 2 temp agencies can provide jobs workers while they are looking permanent work they can also serve as a clearinghouse helping workers find out about jobs certain employers getting a tryout employer many workers a temp job a steppingstone a permanent job that they might not have heard about or obtained any other way so growth temp jobs will also tend reduce frictional unemployment aging baby boom generationthe especially large generation americans born between 1946 1964meant that proportion young workers economy was relatively high 1970s as boomers entered labor market but relatively low today as we noted earlier middleaged older workers are far more likely experience low unemployment than younger workers a factor that tends reduce natural rate unemployment as baby boomers age combined result these factors that natural rate unemployment was on average lower 1990s early 2000s than 1980s 20082009 great recession pushed monthly unemployment rates up 10 late 2009 however even at that time congressional budget office was forecasting that by 2015 unemployment rates would fall back about 5 during first two months 2020 unemployment rate held steady at 35 as first quarter 2022 congressional budget office estimates natural rate be 46

21-4-what-causes-changes-in-unemployment.question: natural rate unemployment europe by standards other highincome economies natural rate unemployment us economy appears relatively low through good economic years bad many european economies have had unemployment rates hovering near 10 or even higher since 1970s european rates unemployment have been higher not because recessions europe have been deeper but rather because conditions underlying supply demand labor have been different europe a way that has created a much higher natural rate unemployment many european countries have a combination generous welfare unemployment benefits together nests rules that impose additional costs on businesses when they hire addition many countries have laws that require firms give workers months notice before laying them off provide substantial severance or retraining packages after laying them off legally required notice before laying off a worker can be more than three months spain germany denmark belgium legally required severance package can be as high as a years salary or more austria spain portugal italy greece such laws will surely discourage laying off or firing current workers however when companies know that will be difficult fire or lay off workers they also become hesitant about hiring first placewe can attribute typically higher levels unemployment many european countries recent years which have prevailed even when economies are growing at a solid pace fact that sorts laws regulations that lead a high natural rate unemployment are much more prevalent europe than united states

labor force participation rate another important statistic labor force participation rate this percentage adults an economy who are either employed or who are unemployed looking a job using data figure 212 table 211 those included this calculation would be 162052 million individuals labor force we calculate rate by taking number people labor force that number employed number unemployed divided by total adult population multiplying by 100 get percentage data from november 2021 labor force participation rate 618 historically civilian labor force participation rate united states climbed beginning 1960s as women increasingly entered workforce peaked at just over 67 late 1999 early 2000 since then labor force participation rate has steadily declined slowly about 66 2008 early great recession then more rapidly during after that recession labor force then climbed slowly during 2010s but declined again during pandemic marchapril 2020 remained lower than prepandemic levels as early 2022

how does us bureau labor statistics collect us unemployment datathe unemployment rate announced by us bureau labor statistics on first friday each month previous month based on current population survey cps which bureau has carried out every month since 1940 bureau takes great care make this survey representative country as a whole country first divided into 3137 areas us bureau census then selects 729 these areas survey divides 729 areas into districts about 300 households each divides each district into clusters about four dwelling units every month census bureau employees call about 15000 fourhousehold clusters a total 60000 households employees interview households four consecutive months then rotate them out survey eight months then interview them again same four months following year before leaving sample permanentlybased on this survey state industry urban rural areas gender age race or ethnicity level education statistics comprise components that contribute unemployment rates a wide variety other information available too example how long have people been unemployed did they become unemployed because they quit or were laid off or their employer went out business unemployed person only wage earner family current population survey a treasure trove information about employment unemployment if you are wondering what difference between cps eps read following clear up feature

20-4-economic-convergence.question: arguments favoring convergence several arguments suggest that lowincome countries might have an advantage achieving greater worker productivity economic growth future a first argument based on diminishing marginal returns even though deepening human physical capital will tend increase gdp per capita law diminishing returns suggests that as an economy continues increase its human physical capital marginal gains economic growth will diminish example raising average education level population by two years from a tenthgrade level a high school diploma while holding all other inputs constant would produce a certain increase output an additional twoyear increase so that average person had a twoyear college degree would increase output further but marginal gain would be smaller yet another additional twoyear increase level education so that average person would have a fouryearcollege bachelors degree would increase output still further but marginal increase would again be smaller a similar lesson holds physical capital if quantity physical capital available average worker increases by say 5000 10000 again while holding all other inputs constant will increase level output an additional increase from 10000 15000 will increase output further but marginal increase will be smaller lowincome countries like china india tend have lower levels human capital physical capital so an investment capital deepening should have a larger marginal effect these countries than highincome countries where levels human physical capital are already relatively high diminishing returns implies that lowincome economies could converge levels that highincome countries achievea second argument that lowincome countries may find easier improve their technologies than highincome countries highincome countries must continually invent new technologies whereas lowincome countries can often find ways applying technology that has already been invented well understood economist alexander gerschenkron 19041978 gave this phenomenon a memorable name advantages backwardness course he did not literally mean that an advantage have a lower standard living he was pointing out that a country that behind has some extra potential catching up finally optimists argue that many countries have observed experience those that have grown more quickly have learned from moreover once people a country begin enjoy benefits a higher standard living they may be more likely build support marketfriendly institutions that will help provide this standard living

arguments that convergence neither inevitable nor likely if economys growth depended only on deepening human capital physical capital then we would expect that economys growth rate slow down over long run because diminishing marginal returns however there another crucial factor aggregate production function technologydeveloping new technology can provide a way an economy sidestep diminishing marginal returns capital deepening figure 207 shows how figures horizontal axis measures amount capital deepening which on this figure an overall measure that includes deepening both physical human deepening increase output by eversmaller amounts shape aggregate production line technology 1 shows that ability capital deepening by itself generate sustained economic growth limited since production function technology 3 represents a still higher level technology so that every level inputs on horizontal axis produces a higher level output on vertical axis than either other two gains output than earlier inventions like steam engine or railroad one reason that technological ideas do not seem run into diminishing returns that we often can apply widely ideas new technology at a someone mow your lawn or clean your house part gdp but doing these tasks yourself not part gdp one remarkable change us economy recent decades growth womens participation labor force as 1970 only
